The floor of the chamber, except for a 10-foot radius right inside the door, is covered with tough, sticky webbing that will entrap a character who comes into contact with it.

[h2]4.	Slave Quarters[/h2]
This “barracks,” nothing more than a hollowed-out area devoid of furnishings, is where slaves are quartered when they are eating or sleeping. At any given time [mark]11-20 slaves[/mark] [roll:d20+10]  will be present, overseen by [mark]a pair of quaggoth guards[/mark] stationed at the opening in the southeast corner. If the guards are attacked or threatened, they will run outside, abandoning the slaves, and attempt to re-enter the fortress by way of the secret door at area 1 or the double doors leading to the storeroom (area 6).

The slaves will not take any aggressive action and will not flee except in a life-or-death situation, realizing that their chances for survival outside in the forest are virtually nil.

[h2]5.	Armory[/h2]
This chamber is separated from the corridor by a thick wooden door bound with iron and closed with a large, [mark]intricate metal lock (-10% to any thief's chance to pick it)[/mark]. A [mark]glyph of warding has been cast on the door[/mark]; anyone who picks the lock and attempts to open the door without neutralizing the glyph will take 1-10 [roll:1d10] points of damage unless a save vs. paralyzation is made.

Inside the chamber is a storehouse of weapons and armor. The following are all of drow origin: 
[ul][li]five javelins, [li]two light crossbows, [li]30 crossbow darts, [li]four +1 short swords, [li]two +1 daggers, [li]two suits of + 1 chainmail, [li]three +1 bucklers, [li]and two packets of drow sleep poison, each sufficient to coat 10 crossbow darts. One of the packets is trapped with a needle mechanism that will be activated by anyone who handles the packet without first disarming the trap. The handler must save vs. poison at-4 to avoid being affected by the sleep poison.[/ul]

Also in the room are several sets of ordinary armor and weapons (chainmail, bucklers, short swords, daggers, light crossbows) used by the drow when they go outside the fortress.

[h2]6.	Storeroom and Sludge Well[/h2]
Stacked along the walls of this large chamber are various sacks and crates of foodstuffs and trade goods, but no gems or jewelry or other valuables. This material is booty from various raids that has not yet been transported back to the drow underworld.

[mark]In an alcove in the northern part of the room[/mark] is a 5-foot-diameter hole that leads to the lowest depth of the fertilizer (sludge) pit located beneath this level. Slaves draw fertilizer up from the pit by lowering buckets into the hole on ropes and winches, then pulling them back up with full loads of the sludge. The buckets are then carted outside, and their contents dumped into the trenches that ring the northern edge of the fortress complex.

[mark]The two sets of double doors between this area and the outside[/mark] of the fortress are wide open any time slaves are on duty. The slaves work in pairs, filling their buckets with sludge and then going out through the northern doorway, heading for the fertilizer trenches. Once every 1 1/2 turns, two different slaves return from the trenches, entering through the northern doorway, with buckets to be re-filled. It only takes one turn for a pair of slaves to load their buckets and leave again, which means that for five minutes out of every fifteen the northern end of this room is vacant.

[mark]The southern doorway[/mark] opens onto a short corridor leading into the storeroom; directly across the room at this point is another corridor which leads to [b]area 7[/b]. Once [mark]every 15 minutes or so[/mark], two slaves enter the southern doorway laden with sacks of raw materials or buckets of water from the river and disappear into the long corridor.

The slaves who work in this area are not strictly supervised or even necessarily watched over. [mark]However, bugbears and quaggoth are constantly roaming the rooms and corridors of this level[/mark], and can be expected to rush to the scene if they hear sounds of excessive activity, or some sort of disturbance.

[h2]7.	Fertilizer Chamber[/h2]
This featureless room, in the exact center of the tree trunk, is located at the end of a steeply ascending corridor that rises to 65 feet above ground level. The corridor is separated from area 6 by a set of wooden double doors that (like the doors in [b]area 6[/b]) are only closed and locked during the few hours each night when the slaves are not working.

The floor of the chamber itself is level. In the center of the room is a 10-foot-diameter hole that serves as a receptacle for the water and raw materials (dumped into it by slaves) that combine and decompose into sludge. It is this sludge, which settles to the bottom of the pit beneath this area, that is hauled up through the hole in [b]area 6[/b] and taken out to the fertilizer trenches. (See the cross-section diagramat right)

Pairs of slaves come up the corridor and into this chamber every 15 miuntes or so. It takes only a moment for each two workers to dump their burdens into the hole; the return trip down the corridor and into [b]area 6[/b] takes only a round or two. Consequently, the corridor and the chamber are unoccupied more often than not. As with [b]area 6,[/b] the slaves working to keep the sludge pit full of raw materials are generally unsupervised, but there are guards continually roaming around the area who might be (randomly) encountered, and there is almost always at least one quaggoth in [b]area 8[/b], which is adjacent to the fertilizer chamber.

[h2]8.	Quaggoth Quarters[/h2]
This chamber is “decorated” with large, strange-looking furs that cover most of the floor. The only other objects of note are two wooden chests on the westernmost wall. The larger of the two is unlocked and holds several more of the strange furs. The smaller chest is locked; it contains an abundance of wood shavings with 80 copper pieces and 50 silver pieces scattered throughout the pile. The chest can be unlocked with a key which is found beneath one of the furs on the floor. There are 10 such furs, and there is a 10% cumulative chance that the key will be found under any one of them.

There will be [mark]up to 7  quaggoth[/mark]  [roll:1d8-1], unarmed and asleep, in this room at any given time. The door to the chamber is always closed, but never locked. However, [u]it is a very noisy door[/u] – if precautions are not taken by anyone opening it, the movement of the door will disturb any quaggoth inside the room. 
[ul][li]If the creatures inside the room are outnumbered, they will try to flee into [b]area 7[/b] and down the corridor. [li]If the quaggoth outnumber the intruders, they will fight ferociously until and unless the battle seems to be going against them, and the survivors will then try to escape the area.[/ul]

[h2]9.	Stirge Nests[/h2]
This chamber is reached after passing through a locked door on the south wall of [b]area 6[/b], going up a narrow, curving corridor, and opening an unlocked door in the northwest corner of the chamber. [mark]The door in the southwest corner of the room is locked[/mark].

[container:tablebox-40]The holes also open onto the outside surface of the tree, as indicated on the map; however, the passages are so twisted and convoluted that no light gets through them from the outside world to the inside of the chamber.[/container] The northern wall of this room is littered with holes about six inches in diameter each. These are the nests of [mark]a swarm of angry stirges[/mark]. [ul][li]Every round that at least one character or creature is in this room, 1-3 stirges will come forth from their nests and attack the nearest target. [li]Any character bold (or stupid) enough to thrust an arm into one of the holes will have equal chances of finding a stirge, 1-3 gems of 20 gp value each, or both. [li]If a nest is occupied in such a case, the stirge therein gets an immediate attack and automatically hits the intruder.[/ul]

[h2]10.	Water Trap[/h2]
This irregularly shaped chamber is located behind an unlocked door and at the end of a curving, gently descending corridor which is very smooth and has a thin film of water over most of it, making it even more slippery. 

[container:indent-40]The walls of the corridor are covered with a mass of thorny material. Characters who attempt to negotiate the sloping passageway without taking some precautions against the slipperiness must roll their dexterity or less on d20 every round to stay on their feet. Failure to make this roll means the character has lost his balance and hit the thorns for 1-4 points of damage [roll:1d4]. [/container]

The corridor levels out over the last half of the distance between the door and the chamber, so that movement becomes considerably easier: In this section of the corridor, a character must fail two consecutive saves vs. dexterity (as described above) in a single round to take any damage from the thorns.

[mark]If characters travel to the end of the corridor[/mark], they will see a chamber of no special note, except that the floor is covered with a semi-transparent membrane. Hung on a single large thorn, just above the floor right at the end of the corridor, is a pouch which will be spotted by anyone who examines the wall. The pouch contains a ring of swimming.

This chamber is relatively close to the river, at a spot where the water table is exceptionally high. The membrane is holding back a large pool of water. 

[ul][li]If any character weighing more than 120 pounds (including his or her possessions) steps onto the membrane, or if it is hit or punctured with a weapon, the membrane will rupture. Water will cascade out, filling the chamber and quickly rising to inundate the level part of the corridor nearest the chamber. 
[li]Characters must pass a [mark]DC10 STR check to avoid being swept off their feet and back down the corridor[/mark]. Failure to save means that the victim takes 1d10+2 damage [roll:1d10+2] from contact with the thorns. 
[li]A character wearing the ring of swimming when the membrane is ruptured will be able to swim to higher ground (further back in the corridor) without taking any damage.[/ul]

[h2]11. Drow Barracks[/h2]
[mark]All of the doors leading off the corridor to this three-section area are closed and secured with simple latches – not locks. Opening any one of them, or making any measurable noise anywhere near a door in the level part of the long corridor, will alert all of the occupants of the section in question.[/mark]

[container:indent-40]
[b]A.[/b]	This is a barracks for drow females. Six living areas, each containing a cot and a wooden chest, are along the walls, partitioned off from each other with silk curtains. One curtain, in the northwest corner of the room, is open. Sitting on her cot preparing to go on guard duty (if the party achieves surprise) will be [mark]a female dark elf. She is a 4th level fighter[/mark] with +1 chainmail, a +1 buckler, and +1 weapons.

[ul][li][mark]If she is not aware[/mark] of the party's presence before they open the door to this chamber, she will immediately scream an alarm which will be heard by the occupants of [b]section B[/b]. 
[li][mark]If she has been alerted[/mark] to a possible disturbance, she will be poised for battle when the party opens the door. 
[li]The elves from [b]section B[/b] will [mark]arrive to assist her[/mark] on the round after the party enters the barracks.[/ul]

Each wooden chest that is investigated will be found to contain ordinary (non-magical) clothing, personal items, and the equivalent of 2-12 platinum pieces.

[b]B.[/b]	This is a cooking and eating area containing two large tables and a total of 12 chairs. The doors leading to [b]sections A and C[/b] are closed but unlocked. Seated around one of the tables are [mark]three male dark elves[/mark], two of them wearing black chainmail and the third one more casually dressed. 

[ul][li]The [mark]two elves in chainmail are 2nd level fighters[/mark] (+1 sword / dagger). If they are intruded upon, they will immediately draw their weapons and attack.
[li]The [mark]third elf is a fighter/sorcerer  of 4th level[/mark]. At the first sign of any sort of disturbance, he will shout an alarm. Instead of getting into the melee, the third elf will attempt to stay away from the action and cast spells. In addition to the innate magical abilities possessed by all male drow, this magic-user can cast [i]forget[/i], [i]shatter[/i], [i]burning hands[/i], [i]shield[/i], and [i]push[/i].
[li] Any alarm raised will be heard by the occupants of [b]sections A and C[/b], and those elves will join the fight on the second round thereafter.[/ul]

This room contains little of value – some miscellaneous foodstuffs and ordinary utensils, plus a set of dinnerware spread out on the table the elves are occupying. The plates and cups are made of hammered silver and are worth a total of 75 gp.

[b]C.[/b]	This is a barracks for drow males, identical in configuration to the female barracks, except that there are no partitions separating the sets of cots and chests. Sitting on a bed in the southeast corner of the room is [mark]a 3rd level fighter who is unarmored[/mark], but with his weapons close at hand. He will shout for help if the room is broken into, and then will attempt to hold off the invaders until the [mark]elves from the kitchen area arrive to join the fight one round later[/mark].

Each of the chests in this room contains ordinary clothing and personal items, plus the equivalent of 1-10 platinum pieces.
[/container]

[h2]12.	Anteroom[/h2]
The corridor leading to this chamber is sealed off from the main corridor by a locked door. The passage slopes gradually downward until opening onto a room that is little more than an extension of the corridor. The chamber is empty and featureless.

[h2]13.	Sludge Chamber[/h2]
The door leading to this room is securely bolted. It also happens to be watertight – a fact that will become obvious when anyone lifts the single bolt.  This room was the original outlet for the sludge pit, where slaves went to fill their buckets with fertilizer for the trenches. The outlet was poorly engineered, however, and the sludge backed up into the room, making it necessary to block the chamber off from the rest of the complex and build another outlet (in [b]area 6[/b]).

[ul][li][mark]As soon as the door is freed[/mark], it will burst open, releasing a stream of thick, black liquid that will cover the floor of room 12 to a depth of five feet before the flood subsides. 

[li]The sludge is not harmful in itself, but the stench of such a large quantity of the stuff can be overpowering. A character of [mark]five feet or less in height will be submerged in the sludge[/mark] within 1 round after the door bursts open, and will suffocate within a number of rounds equal to their CON modifier, unless the victim can get back to the sloping corridor in time, or unless his or her head is lifted above the surface of the liquid by a taller companion. 

[li][mark]A character who is not immediately submerged[/mark] must make a DC 15 CON check or be overcome by the stench. Failure to save will cause the victim to become dizzy and keel over into the liquid, with the same chance for suffocation thereafter as for shorter characters who become submerged.[/ul]

[h2]14. Tentamort Lair[/h2]
If characters stay on the main branch of the long corridor and do not notice and pass through the secret door, they will attract the attention of [mark]a tentamort[/mark] (see FIEND FOLIO Tome) that resides in the chamber to the south.  Statblock: [blocklink:1526575]

[ul][li]This chamber is separated from the adjacent corridor by a curved wall running the length of the corridor at this point – except for a two-foot-high opening between the wall and the floor. It is through this opening that the tentamort will attempt to attack with its tentacles. 

[li]To attack the body of the monster, characters must slide themselves through the slot and carry the battle into the room. The tentamort will not try to move through the slot to escape an attack, and can draw its tentacles back into the room if it desires to attack an intruder.[/li][/ul]

If characters detect the secret door, move through the passage that leads off it, and exit through the other secret door, they can avoid the tentamort and gain access to a small, empty chamber with a locked door on its west wall. This doorway leads to an ascending passageway that spirals up inside the trunk and eventually leads to the second level of the fortress.

articles.title: FoD: General Information
articles.content:
The preceding background information will give players sufficient facts to understand the history of the area, in addition to providing a purpose for the journey into the forest. The background includes some hints that dark elves (drow) are behind the organization of the evil forces within the forest.

[container:tablebox-40][img:6422745|nolink][/container]A general description of the motives of the drow will be helpful to the DM in giving the adventure a logical foundation and. if desired, incorporating this adventure into an ongoing campaign. The ruling forces in the drow underworld are noble houses, whose actions are sometimes influenced by the merchant clans they are allied with. Inter-house rivalries pervade drow society; one faction or another is always trying to demonstrate its superiority over the others by wreaking more havoc and plundering more treasure than its competitors.

One noble house has gone to great lengths to formulate a scheme designed to assure its rise to the top of the noble hierarchy. This house, led by a noble named Erihn (“Arron”), has achieved the construction and occupation of a huge above-ground fortress, deep inside the evil forest. It serves as a base from which the drow, and various evil creatures in their service, can raid the prosperous Lands of Launewt, stripping the country of its valuables and riches and funneling prisoners and possessions by caravans back to the dark elves' underworld home.

The fortress took years to build, but the passage of time has little significance to elves; the end result is something that might be called a masterpiece, were its origin and purpose not so shrouded in evil. While the construction was proceeding, dark elves from the house of Erihn began to methodically make subjects and allies out of the evil humanoids who live in the forest, working toward the day when they could consolidate enough evil power to overrun every community in the Lands of Launewt.

The raids that have taken place in recent weeks were disguised to appear as isolated incidents, but their increasing frequency and ferocity suggest to some members of the council that they are really the first skirmishes in a massive assault which could descend upon the Lands of Launewt at any time. As the members of the adventuring party will discover when they reach the site of the fortress, the threat of an all-out assault is a very real one indeed.

Except where otherwise specified, the drow encountered in the forest and within the fortress conform in all respects to the description of the dark elves in the FIEND FOLIO™ Tome.

articles.title: FoD: Second Level
articles.content:
[mark]Elevation ranges from 200 feet to 300 feet above ground level[/mark]

[h2]15. Snare Trap[/h2]
[aloud]The ascending, spiraling passageway ends at an archway that opens into the outside air. A small wooden door is in plain sight along the inner wall of the corridor right at the end of the ascent, and seems to lead back into the tree. There is light here in the outside area – just enough illumination to make out general features, such as the door.[/aloud]

[ul][li][mark]Pulling open the false door will trigger a trap.[/mark] A rope of entanglement will lash out from inside the false doorway, wrapping itself around the legs of the character in front of the door and continuing to push outward until it “shoves” the victim off the branch. This activity all takes place virtually instantaneously – the victim being held [mark]cannot react quickly enough to grab a handhold[/mark], and no one else in the vicinity is able to keep the entwined character from falling off the branch.

[li]The rope is 50 feet long, with its other end anchored to a post inside the false doorway. A victim who is pulled off the branch by the rope will [mark]dangle in the air against the side of the tree[/mark], roughly 40 feet below the branch and more than 150 feet above the ground.

[li]The rope of entanglement will remain stretched out to its full length and will [mark]not attempt to entwine other characters unless they try to grab it or sever it[/mark]. This rope can entangle up to four man-sized creatures before its length is “used up,” at which point the first character grabbed by it will have been pulled back up to a point just below the branch. 

[li][mark]If the rope is severed or untied[/mark] from its mooring within the false doorway, any characters being held by it who aren’t also anchored to the branch will plummet to the ground.[/ul]

[h2]16. Rat Cage[/h2]
[aloud]The door to this room is different from all the others in the fortress. It is made of metal mesh reinforced by wood strips, with a sliding panel in the bottom of the door. The entire door is also hinged and latched.  By looking through the mesh, characters can see a large number of giant rats inside the chamber [/aloud]

The [mark]door can be opened wide[/mark] if anyone cares to enter the room. IIf they do, there are [mark]15 rats[/mark], which are used as food for the giant owl nesting in [b]area 17[/b] .

[mark]The sliding panel at the bottom of the door[/mark] opens to reveal a chamber large enough to hold one rat at a time. This panel works in conjunction with another panel at the rear of the chamber – when the door panel is closed, the rear panel is open, and vice versa.

The way to get a rat out of the room without actually entering the chamber is to:

[ul][li]Open the panel on the door, place a small amount of suitable food in the chamber, and slide the panel shut again. This action opens the rear door to the small chamber, allowing one rat to move into the feeding area. Characters will be able to see through the mesh covering the panel and know that this has occurred (always within one round of the time food is placed in the chamber). 

[li]Then slide the panel open again, kill the rat before it can escape, and throw the carcass to the owl to allow the party unimpeded access to the rope bridge. 

[li]Once they figure out what the rats are for, characters may also figure out that it’s a good idea to kill two rats and save one to give to the owl on their return trip.[/ul]

[h2]17. Owl Nest[/h2]
[aloud]The passage leading past the rat cage opens onto a large branch. Thirty feet from the opening is an assortment of twigs and small branches and a large nest. Sleeping in the nest is a giant owl.  To the right, twenty feet from the opening and ten feet away from the owl’s nest, an long rope bridge is anchored into the branch, extending across to another branch.[/aloud] 

[ul][li][mark]If characters approach the rope bridge without first tossing a dead rat[/mark] in the direction of the owl, the bird will screech a warning and move toward the party, attempting to cut off their access to the bridge. 

[li]It is possible for [mark]one character, or two at most, to run to the bridge[/mark] and begin to move along its length before the owl gets within attacking range – but in such a case, the [mark]character(s) on the bridge will be the owl’s preferred target[/mark], and they will be hard pressed to keep their footing on the bridge while the owl is hovering over them and swiping at them with its claws and beak.

[li]The bird will try, first and foremost, to [mark]force characters to retreat back [/mark]along the bridge to the branch containing the nest. [mark]If the characters do not retreat[/mark], the owl will make a direct attack, attempting to either disable the invaders or knock them off the bridge (a fall from this height would almost certainly be fatal). 

[li][mark]If no one tries to move immediately onto the bridge[/mark], the owl will take up a defensive position at the entrance to the bridge and try to keep the party from moving any further into the fortress.

[li]Characters who are still on the branch will [mark]not be able to offer any direct assistance[/mark] to those on the bridge, except possibly through magical means or by attacking the bird with missile weapons. 

[li]If characters on the bridge demonstrate an intention to retreat back to the branch containing the nest, the owl will break off any direct attack it may have made and allow the retreat to take place.

[li][mark]If characters move toward the nest[/mark], the owl will abandon any characters on the bridge and move to defend (to the death) the two eggs in its nest.

[li]All of these possible [mark]problems can be avoided if the bird is fed one of the rats[/mark]. It will snatch up the carcass and retire to its nest, taking [mark]five rounds to devour its meal[/mark]. During this time, the party can move onto and across the bridge without resistance.[/ul]

[mark]The rope bridge is somewhat rickety[/mark], but strong enough to support a fully armored human (or any smaller creature). 

[ul][li]Characters who [mark]attempt to negotiate it at their full normal movement rate[/mark] have a 5% chance per point of dexterity to make it all the way across without losing their balance and possibly falling. 

[li]If a character loses his balance while on the bridge, he or she must make a [u]DC 10 dexterity check to avoid falling[/u] off. 

[li]The [mark]danger of slipping and falling can be negated[/mark] if characters move across the bridge cautiously, going at half their normal movement rate – which should still give them plenty of time to get across the bridge and through the passage into [b]area 18[/b] before the owl gets hungry again.[/ul]

[h2]18. Corridor of Captives[/h2]
[mark]The door leading to this area[/mark] is locked on the inside but can be opened easily from the outside. It will swing shut and lock behind any character or group who enters the corridor if it is not propped open. The corridor has no light sources of its own; as soon as the door is opened, much wailing and whimpering can be heard coming from the darkness ahead.

This long, curved corridor has [mark]no features except for many pairs of wrist and ankle shackles[/mark] set into the walls – [mark]and the unfortunate creatures connected to those shackles[/mark]. It is a “holding hall” for prisoners of the drow, humanoids who have been captured out in the forest as well as inside the fortress and who, for one reason or another, are unsuitable for slave work.

[mark]After ascending the ramp up to the main part of the corridor[/mark] characters will be able to see (if they have a light source) the first of the prisoners. He has been here longer than any other prisoner, and he looks it: [mark]emaciated, near death, and delirious – of no use to the party[/mark], either for assistance or information. His wrists and ankles are shackled and bound to the wall by foot-long chains imbedded into the wood, holding him in something of a spread-eagle position.

[ul][li][mark]The chains and shackles are ordinary iron[/mark]. The shackles cannot be broken open without causing fatal injury to the prisoner being held by them. 

[li][mark]They can be opened by[/mark] using the key possessed by the guard in [b]area 19[/b] or a successful attempt to pick the lock (DC 10). 

[li]The chains can be [mark]snapped by repeated blows[/mark] with an edged weapon at least as long and as heavy as a long sword. Each [mark]chain takes 24 points of damage[/mark] before breaking.

[li]A chain [mark]cannot be damaged at all unless it is struck while draped across a surface harder than the surrounding wood[/mark] – the stone or metal head of a hammer, for instance, would suffice. If a chain is struck while it is simply lying against the wood wall or floor, the force of the blow will dent or splinter the wood beneath the chain, and the iron will not take any damage. 

[li]The ends of the chains are imbedded so deeply into the wood that any attempt to loosen them by chopping away at the wall or floor will be futile.

Sets of chains and shackles are [mark]spaced about 20 feet apart[/mark] along both the outer and inner walls (at the places marked on the map), staggered so that [mark]no two prisoners are closer than about 10 feet[/mark] from each other. There are a total of [mark]17 sets of shackles, and 10 of them are currently in use[/mark].[/ul]

[mark]Seven of the other nine prisoners[/mark] that characters will encounter on their way through this corridor are almost as bad off as the first victim – [mark]able to do nothing but scream and wail, begging for their release[/mark]. They are unarmored, in very poor health, and would not be of any measurable benefit to the party if they were released. Some of them may actually beg the adventurers to end their suffering, instead of screaming to be freed.

[mark]The two healthy prisoners[/mark] – captured most recently of all – are shackled in the last two spots in the corridor, at the foot of the ramp that leads to area 19. 

[ul][li]The [mark]first is a female dwarven fighter[/mark]  who is armored but weaponless. If freed, she [mark]will follow the adventurers and will be willing to assist[/mark] them in any way she can that does not involve a direct threat to her life. 

[li]The second is [mark]a 3rd level female human wizard[/mark]  whose only remaining spell at this time is magic missile. If freed, [mark]she will also follow and assist[/mark].
[/ul]
[h2]19.	Guard Room[/h2]
This sparsely furnished, irregular chamber, located behind an [mark]unlocked door[/mark], is occupied by [mark]a 2nd level male drow fighter[/mark] who is “guarding” the prisoners. 

[ul][li]The captives in the corridor don’t need to be looked after closely; basically, the guard in this area is only responsible for making [mark]sporadic checks of the corridor to see if any prisoners have died.[/mark] When he finds a body, he unlocks the shackles holding it and arranges for the remains to be transported to the fertilizer pit. A key, which fits any of the shackles in the corridor, is on a cord around his neck.

[li]The [mark]guard is accustomed to hearing the delirious moans and groans of the prisoners[/mark], and will not be alerted to the presence of intruders by any sounds or statements the captives might make. He will be [mark]automatically surprised if the party approaches the door with caution[/mark] and pulls it open quickly.

[li]The guard [mark]will be able to hear if any attempt is made to release a prisoner from any of the five sets of chains closest[/mark] to the door of this room. If this occurs, he will burst from the room, attacking only if a lone character is the adversary. [mark]If outnumbered[/mark], he will try to flee in a counter-clockwise direction, heading back out of the corridor and toward the first level to sound an alarm. He will [mark]only engage in combat if an opponent stands in his way[/mark], and under no circumstances will he lead the party clockwise through the corridor and deeper into the fortress.
[/ul]
[h2]20.	Shadow in the Shadows[/h2]
This roughly square chamber lies behind [mark]a heavy wooden door[/mark] that can be locked from the outside but is [mark]not locked at the present time[/mark]. 

[ul][li]The [mark]room is pitch dark[/mark], and even if the chamber is illuminated by light sources carried by characters, the [mark]northeast corner will remain shrouded in shadow[/mark]. This is because a [mark]four-foot-high partition of wood[/mark] (indicated on the map by dotted lines) has been built up, closing off access to the corner except through a narrow passageway along the north wall.

[li][mark]Characters who advance close enough[/mark] to the partition to see over the top of it will spot a gem in the corner of the chamber, glowing with a faint red color and seemingly suspended in the shadows a few inches off the ground.[/ul]

The shadows cast by the [mark]partitions are designed to conceal the presence of a shadow demon[/mark] whose inert body lies in the corner (at the spot marked “x”). 

[ul][li]The demon has cast a [i]magic jar[/i], causing its life force to be contained within the gem that is hung around its neck.

[li]The demon will try to [mark]take over the mind of the first character who approaches[/mark] to the outer edge of the partition, or who comes through the narrow passageway and moves toward the gem. 

[li][mark]If no one heads for the northeast corner[/mark] and characters decide instead to move to the opening to [b]area 21[/b], the demon will attempt to magic jar into the mind of [mark]the first character who moves through the narrow opening[/mark].

[li]If the [mark]victim of the [/i]magic jar [i]makes a DC 13 Charisma save[/mark] after being possessed, the demon’s life force will be cast out of the character’s mind and back into the gem, and the demon will not be able to posess that victim for 24 hours.

If a player is successfully posessed, give them the shadow demon's stat sheet and the following instructions to play the demon as described.

[container:tablebox-90]
[center][b]Instructions for the Player whose Character was Posessed[/b][/center]

You have been posessed by a [i]shadow demon[/i] via a  spell.  The demon, whose body was behind the partition, cast their life force into the glowing gem (behind the partion) and from there, waited for a victim - you.  Your character's life force is now inside the gem and can see/sense what is happening nearby but cannot interact with the world outside the gem.

You are now playing as the demon inside your head.

Without revealing the demonic presence inside the character's mind, do your best  to foil the party’s attempts to proceed further inside the fortress.  Start gently but slowly, follow along with the party but the further the party goes the more insistent/aggressive you should get.

Notwithstanding the previous point, the gem that was (is still?) behind the partition is to be protected at all costs.  It is your "magic jar" and you do not want to get very far from it or risk death (being more than 100' from the gem prevents returning to it and thus to your body).
[ul][li]If the party takes the gem, it must be carried by [b]you and only you[/b]. 
[li]If necessary to protect the gem - from your party or someone else - you will fight to the death.[/ul]

You have access to your character's physical weapons and attacks. You do [b]not[/b] have access to your character's skills or magic except for what comes from an item you carry.  You have however, gained the  innate capabilities of the demon: 
[ul][li][b]Light Sensitivity.[/b] While in bright light, you have disadvantage on attack rolls, as well as on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight.
[li][b]Shadow Stealth.[/b] While in dim light or darkness, you can take the Hide action as a bonus action.
[li][b]Subtle Nightmares.[/b] Cast the following spells (as an action) without any outward indication: [i]fear[/i], [i]darkness[/i]. [/ul]

In addition, some of your ability scores have changed to that of the demon: INT - 14, WIS - 13, CHA - 14.
[/container]

[mark]If the party is somehow able to neutralize or immobilize the possessed[/mark] character, the demon will attempt to abandon the possessed character.   
[ul][li]If the gem is within 100', the demon’s life force will travel instantaneously back into the gem, and the once-possessed character will regain control of their mind. 
[li]If the gem is not within 100' the demon will bide it's time.
[li]If the demon successfully returns to the gem and its body is < 100' away, it will jump to its own body and re-animate. Otherwise it remains in the gem until the distance is < 100' at which time it jumps back to it's own body.
[li]If the host body dies, the demon must make a DC 14 Charisma saving throw. On a success, it returns to the container if it is within 100'. Otherwise, the demon dies too.
[li]If for some reason the posessed character remains in the gem but the demon is dead, the character may return to its own body once the gem is withing 100'.[/ul]

Upon a successful return to its own body, the demon will lie in wait for the party to return.

[h2]21. Farther from the Jar[/h2]
[aloud]This chamber is empty and has no distinguishing features.[/aloud]

[h2]22. Perilous Pool[/h2]
[aloud]The only noteworthy feature of this oblong chamber is a shallow pool containing what appears to be a shimmering liquid.[/aloud] 

[ul][li]Any and all characters who move to within 5 feet of the edge of the pool while facing toward the pool will [mark]see their reflection in the surface, and will soon discover that the pool is actually a type of mirror of opposition[/mark]. 

[li]Duplicates of characters who look into the pool [mark]will come into being one round later[/mark], so that the mirror’s true nature will not be immediately evident to the party, and more than one character might approach the pool before its delayed-action magic goes into effect.

[li]Any [mark]duplicate will attack only the character who was duplicated[/mark], ignoring the presence of any other party members who may be in the room, [mark]unless or until another party member joins[/mark] in the attack against someone else’s magical adversary.[/ul]

[h2]23.	Cleric’s Chamber[/h2]
This room, located behind an unlocked door, is the study and living quarters of [mark]a female drow cleric of 4th level[/mark]. She will be [mark]oblivious to the presence of characters in the area outside her chambers[/mark], because she is deep in meditation, praying for replenishment of her spells. Her meditation will end at precisely the moment a character pulls open the door to her quarters (but she is susceptible to being surprised).

[ul][li][mark]In addition to the innate magic abilities[/mark] possessed by all female drow, this cleric has the following spells: [ul][li]cure light wounds, [li]protection from good, [li]sanctuary, [li]silence 15' radius, [li]and spiritual hammer. [/ul]

[li]If outnumbered, she will attempt to escape into [b]area 22[/b] and from there into and through [b]area 24[/b], using her superior movement rate to outdistance the intruders and warn the occupants of the uppermost level.[/ul]

The room contains ordinary furnishings of no particular value, plus a [mark]wooden chest that is locked and protected with a poison-needle trap[/mark]. 
[ul][li]Anyone who opens the chest without finding and removing [mark]the trap will take 2-8 points of damage[/mark] from the needle and must save vs. poison or fall unconscious for 2-5 turns. 
[li]The chest contains [ul][li]ordinary clothing and personal items, [li]a suit of +1 chainmail sized to fit a female elf, [li]20 gold pieces in a leather pouch, [li]and three gems worth 10, 25, and 125 gp.[/ul][/ul]

[h2]24. Catwalk Challenge[/h2]
[aloud]After going up the ramp leading from [b]area 22[/b], characters will find themselves on a narrow catwalk elevated slightly off the floor of the chamber and set off from the surrounding area by a wooden ledge three feet high and six inches thick. The catwalk is too narrow to allow anything other than single-file passage through this chamber. The terrain on either side of the catwalk appears to be overgrown with mushrooms and other sorts of fungus. The catwalk twists around, following the path indicated by the dotted line on the map.[/aloud]

[ul][li]The area [mark]around and beneath the catwalk has been magically altered [/mark]by a [i]hallucinatory terrain[/i] spell. The surface of this chamber is [mark]actually covered by a series of jagged wooden spikes[/mark] that have been chiseled out of the floor. The spikes are all between 2 and 3 feet tall and are very closely packed together. 

[li]If a [mark]character vaults over the ledge framing the catwalk[/mark], the hallucinatory terrain will be dispelled as soon as that character drops down on the outside of the ledge and falls onto the spikes below. A character will always take damage from the spikes, but has a chance to make a saving throw for half damage. To avoid taking full damage [roll:4d4], the character must make [mark]a DC 15 Dexterity save.[/mark] 

[li]Any [mark]character who lowers himself carefully[/mark] into the area of the spikes, once they have been "discovered,” will not take damage from the descent.[/ul]

[aloud]Twenty feet from the foot of the ramp leading up to the third level, the catwalk opens out into a clearing.[/aloud] 

Right at the edge of this clearing (at the location marked “x” on the catwalk), the party’s [mark]progress will be impeded by a powerful (32 hit points) giant spider[/mark] that has been trained to attack any non-drow character or creature who advances this far into the chamber. 

[ul][li]The spider will [mark]begin by dropping a bundle of webbing[/mark] on the first character in line, and on the same round will then drop itself down from the darkness above and attack either the character ensnared in the webbing or the next character in line (50% chance of each). 

[li]A character holding a light source above his or her head and actively scanning the ceiling of the chamber has a 20% chance of detecting the presence of the spider just before the creature drops its webbing, thus negating the spider’s chance to surprise the invaders.[/ul]

Any character in the area of the spikes must move slowly (at a 3" rate). In addition, characters in the spikes must save vs. dexterity on d20 every round to avoid stumbling and taking 1-6 points of damage.

articles.title: FoD: The Forest
articles.content:
The Forest of Doom is a thick, dark curtain bisecting a peninsula, cutting off the Lands of Launewt (located at the tip of the peninsula) from the rest of the continent. It is a band 30 miles wide and more than six times that long. There is no way to go anywhere on foot from the Lands of Launewt without passing through the forest. (Player characters and other adventurers new to the Lands of Launewt are assumed to have approached the country by sea.)

The forest, in addition to providing the surrounding environment for the drow fortress, can also be built into an extended wilderness adventure. This expansion is particularly appropriate if this adventure is being integrated into an ongoing campaign – several parties of adventurers may go into the forest, but perhaps not all of them will come out.

[container:tablebox-40]The original module provided a random encounter table  based on AD&D but this has been omitted. If desired, choose your own encounters of appropriate level.[/container]It is a thoroughly evil place. No good-aligned creatures will be encountered inside the forest; otherwise, treat it as a faerie/sylvan forest setting in a temperate climate. A suggested random encounter table is given below. The chance of a random encounter is a roll of 1 on d6 and should be checked every hour.

The forest springs up abruptly at the edge of the Lands of Launewt. It is thick and dark, with no patch of land within it that could be called a clearing. There are no paths, and the forest floor is covered with a thorny underbrush that inhibits movement (treat as difficult terrain).

The center of the forest’s evil activity is located, logically enough, in the exact center of the forest. If the adventurers are actively seeking the drow fortress but have wandered far from its location, the DM can point them in the right direction rate for overland travel). Negotiating the underbrush entails a great deal of noise, so that any creature's or party’s chance of being surprised is halved – unless those attempting to surprise remain motionless and quiet.

The thick overhead growth starts to blot out daylight almost as soon as one sets foot inside the forest. At a point only a few hundred feet inside the forest perimeter, the light conditions are similar to twilight on a cloudy day. The darkness gradually deepens as one moves closer to the center of the forest until, for a radius of five miles around the drow fortress, the forest is as dark as night. Even on the brightest day, the sun produces no more light than a full moon.

The center of the forest's evil activity is located, logically enough, in the exact center of the forest. If the adventurers are actively seeking the drow fortress but have wandered far from its location, the DM can point them in the right direction by the use of subtle clues: a deepening of the darkness as they move toward the center, and vice versa; the fact that encountered creatures (intelligent ones, at least) will tend to retreat in the general direction of the fortress; and other indicators of this sort.

Any time the adventurers are within one mile of the fortress, there is a 20% chance per turn of movement that they will discover one of the many trails that radiate into the forest from the drow fortress. There are 18 such radiating trails, spaced at roughly 20-degree intervals. They have been cleared to make it easier for slaves to forage raw materials (for the purpose described below) from the immediate area around the fortress.

articles.title: FoD: The Fortress
articles.content:
The headquarters for the house of Erihn’s evil campaign is a phenomenally huge tree, with a trunk measuring 180 feet in diameter at the base (see cross-section, at right). The tree, most likely related to the sycamore family, is made of relatively soft wood. Large sections of the inside of the trunk have been hollowed out to form rooms and passages, but the tree is so massive that these cavities have not weakened it structurally. The tree is alive; it is nourished by a wide, shallow river that passes within a few hundred feet to the south of the trunk, and in addition it is being continually fertilized by a procedure devised by the drow which is carried out through the efforts of several dozen slaves.

On the north side of the tree, opposite the river, are two semicircular trenches, each 15 feet wide and 10 feet deep and separated by a 50-foot-wide walkway. These trenches are always kept at least half full of fertilizer, which consists of decaying organic matter gathered from the surrounding forest as well as a compost-like mixture that is manufactured inside the tree. For most of every day, all except for the darkest hours of the night, slaves are kept busy gathering raw materials from the forest, hauling water in buckets from the river to inside the tree, and bringing out buckets of sludge to deposit in the fertilizer trenches.

If a party approaches the fortress by way of any of the foraging trails, there is a 10% chance every 3 turns, cumulative, of encountering a group of 3-6 slaves. The slaves will be supervised by 1 bugbear, if there are 3 or 4 slaves in the group, or by 2 bugbears if 5 or 6 slaves are working together. (For detailed descriptions of these creature types, see the notes following the Fortress Encounter Table below.) The bugbears are not especially bright, and not overly anxious to engage in combat. They will probably (60%) believe any plausible reason for the party being in the vicinity of the tree, and in that event they will be willing to let a party advance toward the fortress unopposed.

The chance of a random encounter within the tree is a roll of 1 on d6, with checks made every turn after the party gets inside. If an encounter is indicated, refer to the table at right and notes below to determine the creature type involved:

[container:indent-40] Quaggoth groups encountered inside the tree will be unarmed. These creatures are servants of the drow whose main purpose is to shepherd and supervise slaves working inside the fortress.

Drow random encounters inside the tree will be with groups of 2-4 plus a leader, with a 30% chance that each character in a group is a female. The non-leader types are all 2nd level fighters (AC 2, MV 12" or 15", HP 11 each, #AT 2, D sword 1-6, dagger 1-4). The leader of a party who is male will be a 3rd level fighter/magic-user (AC 2, HP 13) who can cast burning hands, shield, and mirror image in addition to the innate magic ability possessed by all dark elves. The leader of a party who is female will be a 3rd level fighter/cleric (AC 2, HP 20, D mace 2-7) with the ability to cast cure light wounds, remove fear, and chant in addition to the innate spell abilities possessed by all female dark elves. Any drow encountered inside the tree, randomly or otherwise, will be using the special drow weaponry and armor.

Slaves are various human, demi-human, and humanoid creatures who have been captured and put to work maintaining the fertilizer system, serving food, and doing other sorts of maintenance. Slaves encountered randomly inside the fortress will not be working on the fertilizer pit (see the descriptions of area 6 and area 7 in the first level), but will be engaged in other sorts of activities. Slaves are all AC 10. MV 9", HD 1-1, HP 3, and unarmed (usually carrying buckets or sacks, if anything) and connected at the waist by 5-foot-long chains. A group of 4 or fewer will be guarded by an unarmed quaggoth; a group of 5 or more will be guarded by a single bugbear with a wooden club.

Bugbears will only be encountered on the first level (ground level) of the fortress; for random encounters on the other levels or in the passageways between levels, roll d8 instead of d1O to determine the creature type. Each bugbear in a random encounter will be armed with a long sword or a mace (50% chance for each).[/container]

There are no stationary light sources inside the fortress. Servants and slaves will carry lanterns or torches for illumination when such equipment is necessary. The fortress has no stairs as such; chambers located at different elevations are connected by rough-hewn, sloping passageways.

articles.title: FoD: Third Level
articles.content:
[u][i]Elevation ranges from 300 feet to 350 feet above ground level.[/i][/u]

[h2]25. Male Drow Barracks[/h2]
The door to the area is unlocked.

[aloud]This chamber is appears to be a barracks of some. It contains six cots and the same number of wooden chests, plus a small table and two chairs. Two of the six places are currently in use but the room is unoccupied.[/aloud]

Only [mark]two of the six living areas are presently being used[/mark]; each of the (locked) wooden chests in those two areas contains ordinary clothing and personal items, plus (in one) 112 gp and (in the other) two gems of 25 gp value apiece. 

[h2]26. Female Drow Barracks[/h2]
The door to the area is unlocked.

[aloud]This chamber is appears to be a barracks of some. It contains six cots and the same number of wooden chests, plus a small table and two chairs. Two of the six places are currently in use but the room is unoccupied.[/aloud]

Aside from the to-be-expected clothing, the only items of value in the chests in those areas are (in one chest) 11-20 gold pieces and (in the other) a pair of non-magical necklaces worth 30 and 50 gp apiece. 

[h2]27. Erihn’s Chamber[/h2]
This is the living quarters for the drow noble Erihn, the commander of the fortress, and his henchman Tarmar. 

The [mark]door to the room is wizard locked[/mark] ([i]arcane lock[/i] spell).
[ul][li]Casting [i]knock[/i] on the object suppresses arcane lock for 10 minutes.
[li]The door is more difficult to break or force open (DC 20 on Strength). 
[li]Similarly, DC 20 to pick the lock.[/ul]

[aloud]The room much nicer that any living querters you've seen here. It contains two beds strewn with silk coverings, one of them set off from the rest of the chamber by silk curtains; two desks north ans west walls); and a single wooden chest.[/aloud]

[ul][li]The [mark]desk on the north wall[/mark] belongs to Erihn. In its compartments and drawers are [mark]material components[/mark] for Erihn’s spells, [mark]plus a set of ledger books[/mark] in which the commander keeps track of plundered goods that have been transported back to the drow underworld. 
[ul][li]The books are written in drow language, which is unintelligible to anyone not familiar with the dark elves’ secret writing. 
[li]Inside a [mark]drawer that is wizard locked[/mark] are Erihn’s spell books, plus a letter signed by Erihn and bearing his personal seal. The spell books contain instructions for all the spells Erihn currently carries (see the description for [b]area 29[/b]) plus[i] jump[/i] and [i]fire trap[/i].[/ul]

[li]The [mark]desk against the west wall[/mark], belongs to Tarmar. [mark]In its drawers[/mark] are a jeweled dagger (non-magical) worth 200 gp, some writing implements, and a partially completed report on the recent activities of the drow that includes a section relating how three promiment members of the Council of Launewt have been bribed into siding with the drow and trying to discourage concern on the part of other council members.[/ul]

[mark]The wooden chest[/mark] bears a [i]fire trap[/i] which was cast upon it by Erihn. Inside are 200 sp, 150 gp,  a scroll with [i]jump[/i] inscribed on it, a small pouch containing 500 gp worth of gems, and a +1 dagger of drow manufacture. 

[h2]28. Meronal’s Chamber[/h2]
The [mark]door to this room is trapped[/mark] with a [i]glyph of warding[/i] that will explode for 16 points of fire damage if it is not bypassed. 
[ul][li]The glyph is nearly invisible and requires a successful Intelligence (Investigation) check vs. DC 14 to spot.
[li][i]Dispel Magic[/i] will remove the glyph.[/ul]

[aloud]This room,contains a large, round bed with silk coverings in the center of the floor; a desk and chair along the east wall; a small wooden altar in the southeast corner; a small, free-standing closet along the north wall; and tapestries of silk on all the walls. [/aloud]

This is the living area of [mark]Meronal, a female who is Erihn’s consort[/mark] and (for the moment, at least) his second in command.

[ul][li]The[mark] closet contains[/mark] ordinary clothing and clerical vestments owned by Meronal. 

[li]The [mark]desk holds[/mark] incense and other sorts of clerical items of no particular value. 

[li]The tapestries are all of no great value, [mark]except for one behind the altar[/mark] bearing a portrait of a huge spider with a female head that is worth 200 gp. 

[li]The [mark]altar has a secret compartment[/mark] in the front (DC  10 Intelligence (Investigation) to spot) that contains a gold statue of Lolth, the drow spidergoddess, that is studded with gems and worth a total of 3,000 gp[/ul]

[h2]29. Hall of Homage[/h2]
This spacious chamber is located at the top of a winding ramp that leads up from the second level. The double doors at the top of the ramp are[mark] not locked, but they have a Leomund’s Trap[/mark] cast on them, and they are so massive that a [mark]strength of at least 13 is needed[/mark] to pull one of them open.

[ul][li]The room is principally used for worship ceremonies and celebrations. 

[li]It is mostly open space, with [mark]massive pillars of wood arranged in a rough circle[/mark] around a center clearing where the activities are conducted. These pillars are integral parts of the tree that were not chopped away when the cavern was hollowed out, primarily so that the tree would not be overly weakened by a fullscale excavation of the chamber. They are gently curved and tapered at the top and bottom so that they blend smoothly into the ceiling and floor of the chamber.[/ul]

In the center of the chamber are [mark]two ornately carved and engraved wooden thrones[/mark]. 

[ul][li]The one to the west is Meronal’s, and the one to the east is Erihn’s. [mark]Both thrones are currently occupied[/mark] by their owners, and each seat is flanked by a pair of guards. 

[li]Under no circumstances will the dark elves in this chamber be surprised by the arrival of invaders; as soon as even a single character advances to a point between any of the surrounding pillars, the [mark]drow guards will stand at the ready, prepared to beat off any assault[/mark] directed toward them or the occupants of the thrones.

[li]The guard on Erihn’s left is his henchman Tarmar, a 5th level fighter, his chainmail, sword, and dagger are all +2 items of drow manufacture. 

[li]The [mark]other three guards[/mark] are all 3rd level male fighters ( with +1 weapons and +1 chainmail of drow manufacture).

[li][mark]Meronal is an 8th level cleric[/mark]  who has the following spells in addition to her innate magical abilities: [i]bless, command, cure light wounds (x2), protection from good, chant, detect charm, hold person (x2), snake charm, resist fire, cause blindness, feign death, remove curse, cure serious wounds, and speak with plants[/i]. 

[li][mark]Erihn is a fighter/magic-user of 7th level in each class[/mark]  who has the following spells in addition to his innate magical abilities: [i]friends, hold portal, magic missile (x4), message, feather fall, detect invisibility, ray of enfeeblement, shatter, lightning bolt, protection from normal missiles, and wall of ice[/i]. 
[ul][li]He wears a ring of wizardry which doubles his first-level spell ability. Erihn wears +2 chainmail and uses +1 weapons, all of drow manufacture. 
[li]Around his neck is a pouch, attached to a string, which con-tains 5 platinum pieces, a gem worth 100 gp, and a metal stamp that produces a seal identical to the one found on the letter inside his desk (see the description for area 27).[/ul][/ul]

[mark]Starting on the tenth round after a conflict breaks[/mark] out in this chamber:
[ul][li]Any and all dark elves from the lower levels who are still alive and able to fight [mark]will arrive on the scene[/mark], entering the room at the rate of [mark]one per round thereafter[/mark]. 

[li]The [mark]“rear guard” will attempt to block off the exit back to the ramp[/mark], while at the same time mounting enough of an offensive to direct the invaders’ attention away from the occupants of the thrones. 

[li]Erihn and Meronal will [mark]refrain from physical combat as long as possible[/mark], staying behind their guards and using their spell-casting powers to complement the fighters’ attacks. [/ul]

[li][mark]If the battle goes against them, the dark elves will try to flee[/mark] through the double doors and down the ramp – but [mark]in no case will they surrender[/mark].[/ul]

articles.title: GT: Scene 1
articles.content:
[h1]Forest Clearing[/h1]

[aloud]
Another day on the road which leads you through an ancient forest. You all look forward to soft beds and good food, not to mention the companionship of other people. Mighty trees bend thick branches over the path, as if they wanted to check what brave—or foolish—creatures dare enter their domain. Colorful birds perch on twigs, their songs chasing wind among leaves. 

Suddenly you hear the sound of a horn—someone needs help! The forest goes silent and now you can clearly discern the sounds of a nearby battle: groans of the wounded, whistling arrows, steel on steel!”
[/aloud]

Upon investigating the sounds...
[aloud]You arrive to the edge of a small clearing. A large tree grows in the middle and underneath a bearded man leans against the trunk. Several green and yellowish creatures—goblins—are attacking him with wicked swords and bows. A number of dead assailants lie on emerald green grass in pools of black ichor. 

The man is almost done for—an arrow has pierced his side. Upon seeing you, he regains hope and shouts ‘Help!’ The goblins turn toward you and charge! 

Roll initiative!”
[/aloud]


[h2]The Fight[/h2]
The fight continues until all the [spoiler][block:1464460]|goblins[/spoiler] are dead. If the goblins gain an upper hand, the lumberjack joins the fight and one round later those goblins who are still alive run away into the forest using their Nimble Escape ability.

Apart from low-quality short swords and bows, they collectively have [roll:1d10+3]  silver.

[h1]Aftermath[/h1]
After the combat the lumberjack falls down, clutching his wounded side. If it is left untended, the man is not able to return to the village on his own. 

[ul][li]The lumberjack's name is Yuri and he lives in Oakenville. 
[li]He was scouting the woods when the goblins attacked. 
[li]He doesn’t know where they came from. 
[li]Until now this particular part of the forest was fairly peaceful. 
[li]Yuri will thank the party and ask them to escort him back to the village.
[/ul]

[h2]Tracking the Goblins[/h2]
The tracks lead the party deep into the forest, in the direction opposite of Oakenville, then stop at a river. If you want, use a random encounter here (see p. 22). The journey takes many hours and the party does not arrive to the village. 
 
Yuri will point out that someone should warn Oakenville and he might not be able to get back on his own.

[h2]Captured Goblin[/h2]
If a player character knows the Goblin tongue, they may try to interrogate the prisoner. This requires a successful [u]DC 12 Charisma (Persuasion or Intimidation) check[/u]. 

While pleading, threatening, and cursing the goblin admits:
[ul]
[li]he belongs to the Toothmouth tribe, lead by Mighty Toothwrencher, also known as Toother. 
[li]his group arrived to the forest because “a powerful master” called and they were going for “an important meeting.” 
[li]he is an unimportant grunt and doesn’t know much more.
[/ul]

articles.title: GT: Scene 2
articles.content:
[h1]Oakenville[/h1]
This village, located in the heart of the Green Forest, is famous for its carpenters and lumberjacks. The high-quality timber harvested here is floated down to the sea. There, it is used as masts on great ships or beautiful furniture adorningroyal chambers. About 300 people (50 families) live in Oakenville. 

[h2]Important People[/h2]
[h3]The Headman - Mandyk Bor[/h3]
Lawful Good male human commoner. A slightly limping man with grey hair and magnificent beard with bushy and very wellgroomed moustache and perceptive, intelligent eyes. During a conversation he often strokes his moustache and straightens his back. He also favors the right leg when walking—years ago an owlbear almost took the left leg off and it still pains Mandyk. He’s always eager to retell the story! If you need Mandyk’s stats, use the commoner. 

[h3]The Healer - Elenya Silverbraid[/h3]
Lawful Good female half-elf priestess of Lohi, recently arrived from Haven. At first she was deeply unhappy for the “punishment,” but she’s slowly realizing she actually likes village life. Typically, she can be met at a charming wooden temple serving all deities. If the party shows their devotion (and puts some gold into the alms box) she can cast a spell from the cleric spell list, up to level 2. 

[h3]Shopkeeper - Bartholomeo Bandau[/h3]
Chaotic Good male halfling commoner is a plump halfling and probably the wealthiest person in the village. He has multiple chins and sweats easily. With the help of his many children, siblings, and cousins he runs “The Oakenville General Store,” a shop with anything the villagers might need. The party can sell him valuable items and buy anything they might need during their adventures. He doesn’t have any magic objects, though. 

[h3]Yokasta, the Innkeeper[/h3]
Chaotic Neutral female human commoner inherited the inn after her father, who now spends days on the porch or at the place of honor at the fireplace. The inn is called “The Old Oak” and a giant tree actually forms a part of a wall. Yokasta excels at her job and brews delicious beer but she always looks tired. She knows everyone in the village. If the heroes impress her, they’ll never go hungry.


[h1]Further Information[/h1]
Any info not already shared by Mandyk already, can be obtained at the Inn.

[b][i]Since when have you had trouble with goblins? Where did they come from?[/i][/b]
[ul][li]“Well, there are always around but not so many and not so organized. But this troubles now? I think it all started a few months ago. After that huge storm that fell a lot of trees.”[/li][/ul]

[b][i]Can we get some support from you?[/i][/b]
[ul][li]“We have families, children. We can fight, sure, but only if we really must.” [b]Note:[/b] on a successful Persuasion check (DC 15), the town headman (Mandyk Bor) will assign a village guard as a guide. [/li][/ul]

[b][i]What else can you tell us?[/i][/b]
[ul][li]“Rest at the inn and depart in the morning. Visit Marsha, our herbalist. She’ll give you a few healing potions—I feel you’ll need them."[/li][/ul]

[b][i]How many goblins are there?[/i][/b]
[ul][li]“Hard to say, but not an army for sure. Probably only a few left after you’ve killed so many with Yuri!”[/li][/ul]

[b][i]How far is the new clearing?[/i][/b]
[ul][li]“Three or four hours on foot.”[/li][/ul]


[h1]Meeting Marsha[/h1]
[aloud]Marsha’s cottage is a small, nicely shaped building at the edge of the village. Carefully arranged patches of aromatic herbs surround it and a beautiful apple tree lends its shade to the plants that shun the sun. An intense aroma of various flowers and herbs permeates the air. Two cats—one black, one tabby—watch you from the porch, clearly unhappy to see you. A moment later the cottages door opens and a young black-haired woman in simple clothing exits the building. Your eyes are drawn to her apron with dozens of sewn-on pockets. The woman is holding a small wooden box. 

When Marsha quickly approaches you, the box’s contents quietly jingle.
[/aloud]

As Mr. Bor promised, Marsha gives the party a few (one per character) healing potions. Each potion restores [roll:2d4+2] hit points. 

The party may ask for more potions but it will require a [u]successful DC 15 Charisma (Persuasion or Intimidation)[/u] check. 
[ul][li]If the check succeeds, Marsha says “Well, you are helping us with the goblins,” and adds two more potions (she doesn’t have more). [li]If the check failed, Marsha says that she can’t just give the potions away, but she can lower the price from 50 to 30 gp for each. [/ul]

articles.title: GT: Scene 3
articles.content:
[h1]In the Forest[/h1]
As the party travels through the forest, one or more of the following encounters may occur.[br][br][br][br]
[container:tablebox-100]
[table]
[tr][th][roll:1d8][/th][th]Event[/th][/tr]

[tr][td]1[/td][td][b]Bloody paw prints cross the path.[/b] If the party follows them, they find a wounded wolf hiding in bushes. It’s a wild animal. If the characters want to help, they need to [b]calm it down[/b] first, which requires a successful [u]DC 12 Wisdom(Animal Handling) check[/u]. A goblin trap wounded the wolf and it is bleeding from its leg. It has only 1 hit point. [b]Tending the wound[/b] requires a successful [u]DC 12 Wisdom (Survival) check[/u]. The check has [u]disadvantage if the characters did not calm the wolf[/u]. 

Once healed, the wolf escapes into the forest. You can also decide that after a particularly good Animal Handling roll (e.g. nat-20) and healing the wolf completely, the wolf accompanies the characters until dusk. [br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]2[/td][td][b]A sack hangs on a tree branch[/b], about 30 feet above the ground. It looks stuffed with—perhaps valuable—things. It is well stuck and shooting arrows at it is pointless. [b]Climbing the tree and dropping the sack[/b] requires a successful [u]DC 13 Strength (Athletics) check[/u]. [b]Carrying the sack down[/b] requires a [u]DC 15 Dexterity (Acrobatics) check[/u].

[b]Failing either check[/b] means the character falls down and takes 11 [roll:3d6] points of damage. The damage can be halved with a successful [u]DC 11 Dexterity saving throw[/u].

[b]The sack contains[/b] food rations, a silver brooch shaped like a butterfly (worth 10 gp), five blank parchments, a potion of climbing, and dirty travelling clothes.[br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]3[/td][td][b]A goblin trap.[/b] A thin rope has been stretched across the path and tied to a pulled-back branch with sharp spikes. [u]Passive Perception (DC 13) of the character leading the party[/u] means they spot the trap. 

On a failure the trap activates - use [roll:1d20+4] attack roll vs. the character’s AC. The trap deals [roll:1d4 + 1] piercing damage.[br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]4[/td][td][b]Signs of battle.[/b] The grass is trampled and bushes crushed. A successful [u]DC 14 Wisdom (Survival) check[/u] reveals that a group of small creatures, perhaps kobolds, attacked a large animal here, most likely a deer.[br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]5[/td][td]The party is traversing a hillock without any trees when a shadow of a [b]large winged beast covers the sun[/b]. A [spoiler][block:1464463]|griffon[/spoiler] is circling above, searching for prey!  To avoid attracking the griffon's attention, a DC 15 Dexterity (Stealth) check is required. Since there are plenty of places to hide in the forest, the characters make the check with advantage. However, characters wearing heavy armor make the check with neither advantage nor disadvantage (advantage and disadvantage cancel each other). 

[b]This is a group check.[/b] If at least half of the group succeeds at their checks, the griffon flies away and looks for food elsewhere. If the majority of the characters fail their checks, the griffon attacks! 

The griffon is 300 feet in the air and at full speed (making a move and the Dash action) moves 160 feet per round. The party members have two rounds to hide again or reach the tall trees, which are 150 feet away. If they succeed, the griffon’s frustrated scream pierces the air and the creature flies away. If the party wants to fight… They have a tough opponent before them.[br][br] [/td][/tr]

[tr][td]6[/td][td]The party hears l[b]oud squealing and a frightened pig[/b] bursts out from the bushes. An invisible, high-pitched voice shouts: “Go forth, you pig! Next time you’ll remember your manners!” 

The voice belongs to a pixie named [spoiler][block:1464506]|Lalalna[/spoiler], who polymorphed a lumberjack from Oakenville into a pig. It was a punishment for polluting their house. The lumberjack accidentally defecated on the faerie’s house. 

[b]Calming Lalalna’s wrath[/b] and convincing them to change the lumberjack back requires a successful [u]DC 13 Charisma (Persuasion) check[/u]. Lalalna can speak Elvish and Sylvan. The lumberjack thanks the party and runs to the village as fast as he can. 

If the characters are aggressive or threatening, on their initiative Lalalna casts sleep at the party member attacking them and flies away. The lumberjack returns to his normal self after 1 hour.[br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]7[/td][td]A recent storm changed this part of the forest into a [b]swamp[/b]. Each party member must succeed at a [u]DC 13 Strength saving throw or must make another saving throw, this time DC 11 Constitution[/u]. If the character also fails this saving throw, they receive one level of exhaustion. Until they finish a long rest, all ability checks (not attacks!) are made with disadvantage.[br][br][/td][/tr]

[tr][td]8[/td][td][b]A pit trap.[/b] Near the new clearing, goblins dug out and camouflaged a pit trap. [u]Passive Perception (DC 13)[/u] for party member in front to spot the danger. If not, they enter onto the masked cover and start to fall down! 

The character can make a [u]DC 13 Dexterity saving throw[/u]. If it is a success, they jump away, safe and sound. 

If they fail, the character falls into a 15-footdeep pit and takes [roll:1d6] bludgeoning damage.[br][br][/td][/tr]
[/table]
[/container]

articles.title: GT: Scene 4
articles.content:
[h1]New Clearing[/h1]
After a few hours the party reaches the new clearing. Two goblins are hiding there and a secret entrance to a dungeon is hidden under the shed.

[aloud]
After a couple hours journey through the forest you come across a fairly large clearing. In the middle is a woodshed, likely used by lumberjacks for rest and/or storage. Against one side of the shed is a stacked pile of logs and another out front.  Several stumps, logs, bushes and rocks litter the clearing.
[/aloud]

[h1]The Setup[/h1]
[container:tablebox-30]Goblin passive Perception is 9, and on a failed check the party won't be seen unless the go out into the open.[/container]There are goblins in hiding, one behind a clump of bushes and another off to the side in the woods (add more?). A PC can find them by sneaking through the woods (making appropriate Stealth checks if they want to remain unseen).


Upon spotting the party, the goblins will attack and sream out a warning.

[h2]Using the Terrain[/h2]
[container:tablebox-30]
 [center][b]Cover Table[/b][/center]
[table]
[tr][th]Item[/th][th]Small[/th][th]Medium[/th][/tr]
[tr][td]Logs[/td][td]1/2 cover[/td][td]no cover[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Bushes[/td][td]3/4 cover[/td][td]1/2 cover[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Log Piles[/td][td]full cover[/td][td]full cover[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]Shed[/td][td]full cover[/td][td]full cover[/td][/tr]
[/table]
 [center][b]1/2 cover:[/b]  AC +2[br][b]3/4 cover:[/b] AC +5[/center]
[/container]
[h3]Cover[/h3]
Logs, log piles, bushes and the shed can be used for cover, see table at right.

[h3]Toppling the Piles[/h3]
A log pile can be pushed over. A single PC requires a [u]DC 20 Athletics check[/u] but two or more PCs can combine efforts if their total STR is 28 or more[/u].

Anyone on the other side is dealt [roll:2d10] bludgeoning damage [u](DC 15 Dexterity check for half)[/u].

[h2]Last Goblin[/h2]
The last standing goblin near the shed will use it's Nimble Escape feature to disengage and run inside where it becomes "hidden".  A successful DC 15 Wisdom (Perception) check allows a character to hear shuffling and the goblin muttering something in the Goblin tongue: “I must get help. Ouch, careful with the trap.” 

If the characters allow that last goblin to run away, it warns the patrol in the Old Dungeon and they all set up an ambush in Area 5.

[h1]After the Fight[/h1]
The goblins don’t have anything interesting to take, but the players may guess that they guarded something here and the shed is the only logical place to hide anything. After taking a look, the party discovers the entrance in the shed’s floor (it’s quite obvious, if an escaping goblin managed to open it). 

If none of the goblins managed to get inside the shed, the trap door will stil be closed and it is trapped.  In order [b]to detect it[/b], a character searching the shed must succeed on a [u]DC 13 Intelligence(Investigation) check[/u]. If the character knows that the trap is here (for example, by overhearing the goblin), they make the check with advantage. [b]Disarming[/b] the trap requires a successful [u]DC 10 Dexterity check using thieves’ tools or DC 13 Dexterity (Sleight of Hand)[/u] check. If the check fails, the trap activates.

The trap is a small pot filled with alchemist’s fire hidden under the ceiling. When a character lifts the trapdoor, a thin string connected to the pot pulls it off the beam and it falls down, smashes on the ground, and causes a small explosion. Everyone in the shed takes [roll:1d4] fire damage. They must also make a [u]DC 11 Dexterity saving throw or start burning[/u]. 

[b]Once on fire[/b], they take an additional [roll:1d4] fire damage at the [u]start of each of their turns[/u]. [b]Then[/b] they can make another [u]DC 13 Dexterity saving throw[/u] to put the fire out. If they roll on the ground or receive assistance (via the Help action) they make the [u]saving throw with advantage[/u]. 

The fire in the shed quickly goes out since the ground is earth and it’s quite humid inside.

articles.title: GT: Scene 5
articles.content:
[h1]Old Dungeon[/h1]
[h2]Information[/h2]
[b]Lights.[/b] The dungeon is dark and there are no sources of light unless noted otherwise. 

[b]Rooms.[/b] Unless a room’s description states otherwise, the ceilings are at least as high as the room is wide. The ceilings in the Old Dungeon are flat.

[b]Corridors.[/b] Unless a description states otherwise, the ceilings of corridors and tunnels are as tall as they are wide. 

[h2]Opponents[/h2]
[spoiler][block:1464460]|goblins[/spoiler]
[spoiler]|Goblin Boss[/spoiler]
[spoiler][block:1464513]|Grug, the half-ogre[/spoiler]
[spoiler][block:1464519]|giant spiders[/spoiler]
[spoiler][block:1464514]|swarm of spiders[/spoiler]

[h2]Situation[/h2]
The Old Dungeon was made centuries ago. Originally, it was the cellar of a large elven guard tower, which was disintegrated by Bel’Quath’s powerful magic. Later human settlers arrived to the forest, failed to notice the hidden trapdoor, and recently built the shed over the entrance to the dungeon. Meanwhile, Bel’Quath, acting through Pabinus, began to organize his troops. Naturally, he remembered the old battlefield; the goblin tribe needed a base of operations and so the Old Dungeon became their new lair.

The goblins belong to the Toothmouth tribe. Their boss is named The Great and Magnificent Toothwrencher, but everyone calls him Toother, or Tooth Fairy behind his back. The tribe members collect the teeth of creatures they have killed and make necklaces and bracelets out of them. Some attach the teeth to their helmets and shields. Toother and his band came to the Green Forest on Pabinus’s request but he always concealed his features with a spell so the goblins don’t really know who their master is—they don’t really care, as long as they can raid humans and count on the wizard’s support.

When the party enters the dungeon, a meeting between kobolds and goblins is taking place in Area 8. Their leaders try to determine their spheres of influence in the forest and Bel’Quaths spectral projection oversees the talks. 

[quote]Now, you must decide: do you want your party to witness the meeting’s finale or is your dungeon “alive” and, for example, if the party takes a long rest after the fight at the clearing, the meeting concludes and the heroes won’t know about it? Or, not unlike in video games, an “animated cutscene” launches upon the heroes entering Main Hall, which will be more interesting, but may challenge the player’s suspension of disbelief.[/quote]


[h1]1. Entrance[/h1]
[aloud]
Slowly, one by one, you descend a rickety ladder. Mud covers the walls and roots poke out in odd places. It seems that a stairwell once ran around the vertical tunnel, but it collapsed a long time ago and became rubble at the bottom. A dark and dank corridor leads further into the dungeon.
[/aloud]


[h1]2. Narrow Corridor[/h1]
[aloud]
Goblin tracks crisscross the muddy floor and foul-smelling puddles have formed to one side. The corridor goes straight for a few dozen feet and then turns right at a small pile of rubble. At one place the stones forming the wall fell off, revealing a natural tunnel. The stench wafting from it is particularly vile and sticky, white strands cover its walls. The main tunnel leads further on.
[/aloud]

The narrow corridor is safe—there are no enemies or traps here. 

[ul]
[li]A successful [u]DC 12 Intelligence(History[/u]) check reveals that the architecture here is elven.
