[li]A successful [u]DC 13 Intelligence(Nature)[/u] check reveals that the white substance on the side tunnel’s walls is spider webbing. And the spider that made it must be particularly large.

[li]A successful [u]DC 15 Intelligence(Investigation)[/u] check reveals that there are doors behind the pile of rubble. A successful Ucheck is required to move the rubble away, but it takes time and the noise may draw the goblins patrolling Area 5 (your decision). 

The doors are thick, solid, and locked. A hero may try to[b] smash it[/b]: U. A character proficient with thieves’ tools may try to quietly [b]open the lock[/b], which requires a successful DC 10 Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check using thieves’ tools. Area 6 is behind the door, including an angry half-ogre, annoyed by the noise.
[/ul]


[h1]3. Spider Cave[/h1]
[aloud]A short and low tunnel leads down and ends in an irregular natural cave. You can see a wooden bridge a few dozen feet above the floor and along the back wall. Thick webs cover the walls, floor, and even the ceiling. In a corner you see bulbous shapes wrapped in the webs—cocooned victims! Round, opalescent spider eggs protrude from a particularly thick tangle of webs stuck to another wall. Your eyes go up and meet the gaze of eight spider eyes! The beast hisses and attacks![/aloud]

The [spoiler][block:1464519]|giant spider[/spoiler] is on the ceiling, about 12 feet above the ground and most melee weapons can’t reach it. Moreover, the [b]whole cave is difficult terrain[/b]. It means that the heroes spend 2 feet of movement for every 1 foot traveled, but spider doesn’t, thanks to its web walker trait.

The giant spider won’t chase characters fleeing the cave—it is protecting the eggs. If someone [b]breaks the eggs[/b] (accidentally or on purpose) a swarm or nasty little [spoiler][block:1464514]|swarm of spiders[/spoiler] spills out. 

[b]The webbing is flammable.[/b] For example, if a hero drops a torch, the fire immediately spreads across the room. All the creatures inside take [roll:1d4] fire damage and must make a [u]DC 10 Dexterity saving throw[/u]. On a failure, they start to burn and take an additional [roll:1d4] [u]fire damage at the start of each of their turns[/u]. A successful [u]DC 10 Dexterity saving throw[/u] puts out the flames. [u]The cave stops burning after 1 round.[/u] The [b]fire also collapses the bridge[/b], which may make reaching Area 4 more difficult. 

[h2]After the Fight[/h2]
Once the spider is dead, the party can look around. If they investigate the beast, they discover a tooth-studded collar around its neck—it seems that goblins tamed the creature!

If the [b]spider eggs survived the fight[/b], the party may try to harvest them, which requires a successful DC 14 Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check. This allows the character to harvest [roll:1d6+2] eggs, which can be sold in the village for 10 gp each, if they’re intact. 
If the check fails, the character harvests only 2 eggs.

[b]The bodies in the cocoons[/b] are goblins. If someone takes a closer look and succeeds on a [u]DC 13 Intelligence (Investigation) check[/u], they discover that one of them clutches something in its hand. It’s a small piece of chalcedony, worth 50 gp.

Someone may also [b]try to climb[/b] the bridge or the rock outcropping near Area 4 (although it’s impossible to see from the cave what’s there). This requires a successful [u]DC 13 Strength (Athletics) check[/u]. 

However, the [b]bridge is ancient and can’t bear such a weight[/b]. It collapses as soon as a medium-sized or larger creature stands on it. The climber may make a [u]DC 11 Dexterity saving throw[/u] and jump to the left or right (decide quickly!) ending up in Area 4 (see below) or near the blocked door to Area 5. 

If the character fails the saving throw, they fall and take [roll:1d6] bludgeoning damage. Naturally, if they climbed up, they might want to climb (DC 10 Strength(Athletics) check) or jump down (DC 10 Dexterity(Acrobatics) check).


[h1]4. Old Warrior[/h1]
[aloud]The tunnel curves sharply and ends in rubble. Wherever it led, it must’ve collapsed a long time ago. There is a human skeleton in a rusty chainmail lying on the stones. One bony hand reaches to the rubble as if it was still trying to dig through, the other clutches a sword. Interestingly, a cloak under the skeleton looks completely new.[/aloud]

If someone tries to take the cloak, the [spoiler][block:1464515]|skeleton[/spoiler] stands up and attacks! A player may simply declare that their character grabs the cloak and runs away, in which case they will be out of melee range when the skeleton animates.

If they entire party immediately retreats to the bridge or further, the skeleton will return to the rubble and start digging; after a minute it de-animates.

[h3]Treasure.[/h3] The cloak is a [spoiler][block:1316735]|cloak of protection[/spoiler].  


[h1]5. Main Corridor[/h1]
[h2]If the goblins have NOT been alerted[/h2]
[aloud]You enter a wide corridor. There is a collapse in the north, and in the south the passage makes a 90-degree turn west. Old, faded, and incomplete paintings cover the walls. Rubble and pieces of decorative columns lie on the floor. The air smells of dust and time. A sudden bang of large doors being shut shatters the almost sacred atmosphere. Then you can hear the approaching footsteps of goblins, who chatter something in their screeching tongue.[/aloud]

If the party [u]kept quiet, and is not carrying a light[/u] they can ambush the goblins. The party members make [u]Dexterity (Stealth) checks against DC 4 (the goblins’ passive Perception in dim light)[/u]. If half or more of the party members succeed, they can quickly take positions and attack the goblins, making the first attack (goblins have "surprised" condition).

If the party is carrying a light and/or making noise the patrol will be aware and attack once they party is in the corridor.

[h2]If the goblins HAVE been alerted[/h2]
[aloud]You enter a wide corridor. There is a collapse in the north, and in the south the passage makes a 90-degree turn west. Old, faded, and incomplete paintings cover the walls. Rubble and pieces of decorative columns lie on the floor. The air smells of dust and time. A sudden bang of large doors being shut shatters the almost sacred atmosphere.[/aloud]

The patrol will NOT be in the corridor as they are waiting around the corridor in Area 7.

If the fight with the goblin patrol takes too long or is particularly noisy, Grug (see below) will burst from his lair shouting “Be quieeeet!”.  The goblins then tell Grug that if he helps them the noise will end sooner.  The party may at this point jump in and try to convince Grug to join their side. The goblins and the party make an contested Charisma (Persuasion) check to see whom the half-ogre helps. Whomever rolls higher wins!

[h1]6. Half-Ogre's Room[/h1]
[h2]If the goblins have NOT been alerted[/h2]
The door to this room is closed but unlocked. 

Once they open the door...
[aloud]Sour smells of old sweat and rot permeates the air in this room. The floor is strewn with gnawed bones, shredded clothes, and rusted pieces of armor. A small, smoky torch casts dancing shadows on the walls. Suddenly one of them rises from a corner, growing larger and larger, and finally you see a 10-foot tall half-ogre, who grabs a huge axe and growls: ‘I told you to leave me alone, you filthy maggots![/aloud]

A half-ogre named Grug lives here and wants to be left alone most of all. Theoretically, he agreed to work with Toother and his goblins, but he’s lazy and, after a few first tasks, he’s fed up with following Tooth Fairy’s orders. If the party doesn’t attack him at once, they may try to talk to him. 

[ul]
[li]After a successful [u]DC 9 Charisma (Persuasion) check[/u], the party may convince Grug to abandon the goblins and go explore the world. If Grug is annoyed because, for example, the party made a lot of noise clearing the rubble at the door in Area 2, the check is made with disadvantage.

[li]After a successful [u]DC 14 Charisma (Persuasion or Intimidation) check[/u], the party may convince Grug to help them fight the goblins. Again, if Grug is angry, apply is advantage to the check. But if the party does something to win his favor, for example, paying him (which you can suggest as you roleplay Grug), apply advantage to the check. 
[/ul]

[h2]Moral quandry[/h2]
If Grug is convinced to assist the party Grug may admit he robbed or even killed villagers. He may even show a trophy.

[h2]Treaure.[/h2] Searching the litter in Grug’s room requires a successful [u]DC 13 Intelligence (Investigation) check[/u]. If the check succeeds, the party finds a scrap of a jacket with a pouch sewn into the layers. The pouch contains 15 gp and a [spoiler][block:1402256]|potion of growth[/spoiler].


[h1]7. East Corridor[/h1]
[aloud]
The corridor ends in a pile of rubble. Double doors with metal fittings block your way to the right. You can hear multiple muffled voices behind them.
[/aloud]

If the party let the last goblin from the abovegound fight escape, then there is an ambush set up here consisting of several goblins.  If Grug hasn't been met yet then at some point in the fight he will come out complaining of the noise (see Area 5).

Once the goblins and Grug have been dealt with the following applies.

[h2]Collapsed end[/h2]
The characters with [u]passive Perception of 13 or higher[/u] notice signs of work at the collapsed end. If they check them out and make a successful DC 14 Intelligence (Investigation) check, they find a hidden door in the wall. Opening it requires a successful [u]DC 15 Strength (Athletics)[/u] check or a more subtle approach and a [u]DC 12 Dexterity check using thieves’ tools or DC 15 Dexterity(Sleight of Hand) check[/u]. 

Behind the doors there are narrow stairs up. There is another secret door at the end, which is spotted and opened automatically. They’re unlocked and lead to the gallery in Main Hall (Area 8a).

[h2]Large Double Doors[/h2]
The large double doors lead to the main hall (Area 8) and are unlocked. It’s impossible to open them subtly. If someone wants to listen to the voices inside, they must succeed on a [u]DC 13 Wisdom (Perception) check[/u]. If the check succeeds and the kobold-goblin summit is occurring inside:

[aloud]scratchy goblin voices and hissing kobolds. Both groups quarrel over who should rule the forest. They speak in the Common tongue. Suddenly a vile, icy voice breaks into the discussion. “Your achievements shall determine that,” he says. Both kobolds and goblins go quiet for a moment and then they start quarreling again: this time whose achievements will be greater.[/aloud]


[h1]8. Main Hall[/h1]
[aloud]
Columns line this spacious area that once was a large storeroom. For the goblins though it has become a throne room, bedroom, dining room and—judging by the smell—a toilet. Old chests and crates were pulled together to form primitive tables and the pile of them at the far end serves as the goblin boss’ “throne.” Toothwrencher, or Toother (and Tooth Fairy) preens on the rickety piece of furniture. Other goblins surround him and a group of small reptilian creatures—kobolds— stands a few feet away. Their leader wears a headdress made of magnificent feathers. Above them a spectral shape floats in the air, its cloak billowing in winds of magic. When you enter the room, the specter’s red eyes flash with anger. 

‘Kirich, you know what to do. Toother, kill the intruders!’ 

The apparition disappears and the kobolds run out through the far exit. As the massive portcullis closes behind them, the goblins are almost upon you!”
[/aloud]

[h2]8A. Gallery[/h2]
Two ways lead to the gallery: a rickety ladder in the main hall and a secret passage from Area 7. It is a good position for an archer. It also offers access to rafters and, after a successful DC 12 Dexterity (Acrobatics) check and then a DC 10 Dexterity (Stealth) check (with advantage because it’s dark under the ceiling), a character can sneak to a place directly over Toother and launch a surprise attack (gaining advantage on their first attack roll)—the possibilities are endless!

[h2]The Fight[/h2]
If Toother loses at least 20 hit points, he attempts to run away. He just sprints through the dungeon, covering 60 feet per round (12 squares). Remember, as an agile goblin he does not provoke opportunity attacks, but take into the account difficult terrain and obstacles.

If Toother dies or starts running, the other goblins try to escape as well.

[ul]
[li]If someone [b]cuts the rope[/b] holding the chandelier, it falls down dealing [roll:3d6] bludgeoning damage to everyone directly underneath (look at the shadow on the map). The targets can make a [u]DC 13 Dexterity saving throw to take half the damage[/u].

[li][b]Chests and columns[/b] provide half cover (+2 AC and Dexterity saving throws)

[li]The [b]pile of crates[/b] forming Toother’s throne is unstable. If a character pushes it and makes a successful [u]DC 10 Strength (Athletics) check[/u], the whole contraption falls apart, creating a 10-foot-by-10-foot area of difficult terrain. Moreover, [u]anyone within 5 feet of the crates must succeed on a DC 11 Dexterity saving throw or fall prone[/u].

[li]A [b]pot full of odd-smelling stew[/b] bubbles in a corner. A successful [u]DC 15 Intelligence (History) or DC 17 Intelligence (Medicine) check (doesn’t require an action)[/u] reveals that if a lid is placed on the pot, the strange substance [u]becomes unstable and should explode after 1d4 rounds[/u], throwing the pot off the fireplace. The hot, brass container bounces in a straight line at the start of each round moving towards the nearest obstacle. All creatures in the line take [roll:1d4] bludgeoning damage and must succeed on [u]DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or fall prone[/u]. Moreover, the area the pot traversed is covered with gooey soup and counts as difficult terrain.

[li]Near the throne stands a [b]large keg[/b] giving off the distinctive smell of strong alcohol. A successful [u]DC 13 Intelligence (History) check[/u] reveals that it contains grog, a beverage all goblinoids love. Grog is highly flammable. If someone sets it on fire, the keg explodes dealing [roll:2d10] fire damage to everyone within 10 feet, or half with a successful [u]DC 11 Dexterity saving throw[/u].

[li]A massive portcullis blocks the exit through which the kobolds escaped. Raising it with a nearby wheel takes 3 rounds.
[/ul]

[h2]Toother get Captured[/h2]
If the party managed to capture Toother alive, he begs for mercy and talks about a terrifying sorcerer who wants to burn the forest to the ground, and Toother in particular because he failed. Toother knows that the sorcerer is organizing the monsters in the forest, but doesn’t know exact plans nor reasons for that. He doesn’t know where the kobolds are or even what the Dark Master (as he calls him) looks like, because he always contacted him wreathed in illusions. Other goblins know even less.

[h1]Aftermath[/h1]
[aloud]Suddenly the air around the throne darkens and a spectral shape, woven of shadows and malice, appears. Its fiery eyes take a long, hard look at each of your faces, as if burning them into memory. Then, as abruptly as it appeared, the specter disappears.[/aloud]

[h2]Treasure[/h2]
Toother hid his loot in a chest behind the throne. The chest is closed. If the party doesn’t have the key (Toother has it on him), opeing it requires a successful [u]DC 13 Dexterity check using thieves’ tools[/u]. There are two magic spell scrolls inside: [i]false life[/i] and [i]identify[/i]. There is also a small pouch with [i]jewels, worth 120 gp[/i].

articles.title: HBG: Epilogue
articles.content:
[h1]Back to Oakridge[/h1]
If the party gives the poison to the boys, and the boys subsequently perish, the local reaction will be shock, grief, and outrage.

The villagers of @[Oakridge](settlement:7ea6d7eb-ee01-4ebb-9e88-226dca2f1246), who had placed their hope in the adventurers, will be devastated by the loss of Thomas' children. The news will spread throughout the village, and anger will surge among the townsfolk. The mood will become hostile, and the blame will fall heavily upon the party for their actions unless they prove innocent. In that case, the village will demand the death of the hag.

The locals may demand justice for this grievous mistake, and there's a possibility that they will form a mob to seek retribution. Whether this takes the form of an angry mob confronting the party or an organized group seeking vengeance against the Hag depends on the temperament of the villagers and the party's actions in the immediate aftermath.

As the Dungeon Master, you can decide the course of action based on the players' choices, the temperament of the villagers, and the overall narrative direction you wish to take in response to the tragic outcome. The consequences of the party's decisions will shape the direction of the story and its ultimate resolution.

category.title: Module: The Hag, The Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HBG: NPCs
articles.content:
[anchor|thomas]

[h1]Thomas (farmer)[/h1]
[container:tablebox-30][img:7012401|nolink][/container]Thomas, a farmer, is a straightforward and warm-hearted individual. He is terrified that his children might never wake up again, and he is willing to do whatever it takes to save them. In his desperation, he even considers facing the Hag alone to secure a cure for his kids. He earnestly pleads with the players to lend their assistance in this dire situation.

Thomas is a tall, broad-shouldered man, and the constant field sunlight weathered his skin. He wears a small hat, brown trousers with a suspender and a shirt, complemented by a modest grey moustache.





[anchor|lanita]

[h1]Lanita (priestess of Ariel)[/h1]
[container:tablebox-30][img:7012400|nolink][/container]Lanita, a priestess of Ariel, is a warm and smilingwoman in her thirties. Her skin tone suggests she comes from the south, perhaps the Endless Dunes. Sold as a slave in her youth, she grew up in a noble's house. There, she encountered a priestess of Felicida who noticed Lanita's ever-bright nature. Recognizing her potential, the priestess took Lanita to the temple, where she devoted herself to spreading joy and love to everyone she meets.

Standing at around 5 feet 5 inches tall, Lanita has darkish skin and long black hair with streaks of grey. She willingly heals the party for free if they ask, using her powers as a priestess.








[anchor|rico]

[h1]Rico (inkeeper)[/h1]
[container:tablebox-30][img:7012394|nolink][/container]Rico, the innkeeper, is a sizable human who delights in eating, talking, and drinking. He's cheerful and always ready for a lively conversation or a drinking challenge. An intriguing offer hangs on the inn's wall: if anyone can devour a hefty 4-pound steak accompanied by a loaf of bread within an hour, they can enjoy a complimentary day's stay on Rico's tab. (Constitution roll DC 15) .

category.title: Module: The Hag, The Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HBG: The Bear
articles.content:
[h1]Great Black[/h1]
Following the Hag's directions, the party ventures to the northern part of the forest. They come upon a dark cave where [mark][url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot/]Great Black[/url], the bear[/mark], lives. The Hag wasn't lying; the bear is indeed colossal. Surrounding its cave are numerous bones, carcasses, and dried blood, creating an ominous and unsettling atmosphere with a terrible smell.

Upon closer inspection with a successful [u]DC 14 Wisdom (Perception) check[/u] the party notices that the bear's fur is covered with dried blood. It has arrows and spears sticking out from its flesh, many shattered. It becomes [u]apparent that Great Black has been the victim of repeated attacks[/u], and these open wounds have driven it to madness.

The party has two choices: 
[ul][li]end the bear's suffering by slaying it and then return to the Hag, or [li]attempt to help the bear by treating and cleansing its wounds.[/ul] 

In its frenzied state, [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot/]Great Black[/url] is generally aggressive and will attack anyone approaching unless the party can find a way to calm him (through an [u]Wisdom (Animal Handling) check with a DC of 16, using spells, or offering food[/u]).

As the party investigates the bear and its surroundings, they discover crude weapons and arrows bearing goblin craftsmanship marks. This finding might prompt the party to consider tracking down the goblins responsible for tormenting Great Black. They can do this by making a successful [u]DC 14 Wisdom (Survival) check[/u], following the bloodstains on the ground to locate the goblin's settlement. Failing the check, they could encounter some mishap like a goblin ambush in a swampy area; you can decide as a DM.

articleParent.title: Key: The Hag, the Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HBG: The Goblins
articles.content:
[h1]Goblin Camp[/h1]
As the party approaches the goblin's settlement, they can hear the noisy creatures from a mile away. The goblins have made their home within the forest's depths, constructing crude fences and makeshift lookout towers. They are constantly on high alert and do not try to remain quiet. Their chaotic voices echo through the trees, making it impossible for anyone not to notice them. 

They are immediately aggressive towards anyone who dares to enter their territory, launching an attack without any intention of negotiation. The goblins are fiercely territorial and hostile to any intruders, including the party. 

There are [mark]7 goblins[/mark] in the camp: one in each lookout tower (and in half cover), and four on the ground. Their leader is a bugbear named [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#pimp]Black Pimp[/url], who has two orcs as guards. [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#pimp]Black Pimp[/url]  usually is in his tent (the biggest one), while the orcs are in the other, slightly smaller tent. This encounter is supposed to be easy, however, the archer goblins in the towers could pose a threat to the party. 

[h3]The Loot[/h3]
As the party searches the camp, a successful [u]DC 12 Intelligence (Investigation) check in the bugbear's tent[/u] uncovers a crude chest. Inside, they find 12 gold, 7 silver, 42 copper coins, and a lump of basalt-encased gold ore worth around 20 gold. The basalt triggers memories of similar rocks used in the village's houses.

articleParent.title: Key: The Hag, the Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HBG: The Hag
articles.content:
[h1]Initial Meeting[/h1]
Following [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-npcs-plot#thomas]Thomas'[/url] guidance, the party sets off to the west. After a half-day journey through the forest, they reach the edge of the Hag's territory. It's a captivating clearing in the woods where the Hag lives in a cave. The clearing is a scream of colors, decorated with vibrant flowers, luminescent mushrooms, and trees that emit a supernatural glow. The entire scene feels like it's been taken from the heart of the Feywild. As the party approaches, they spot [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#ludmilla]Ludmilla, the Hag [/url] waiting for them, guarded by two wolves: [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#toothache]Toothache[/url] and [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#bonemeal]Bonemeal[/url].

[b]If the party chooses to engage in conversation[/b], the Hag reveals that she is responsible for the magical disease that has happened to Thomas' boys. The children disrupted a ritual she was performing, an attempt to get rid of a bear known as [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#bear]Great Black[/url] of its madness. Unfortunately, the disturbance caused by the boys resulted in her losing crucial components for the spell, and now she cannot repeat the ritual. 

In general, [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#ludmilla]Ludmilla[/url]  proves to be a bitter and uncooperative character. If the party persists and pleads for her help, she agrees to help them, but on one condition: they must deal with [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#bear]Great Black[/url]. The bear has recently gone mad, rampaged through the forest and destroyed the Hag's herbal garden twicealready. It's an increasingly dangerous and chaotic presence in the woods that must be dealt with before [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#ludmilla]Ludmilla[/url]  provides the cure to heal the farmer's boys.

[h1]After the Bear[/h1]
After dealing with [url:https://www.worldanvil.com/w/antheros-phre3d/a/hbg3A-monsters-plot#bear]Great Black[/url], the party returns and she keeps her promise and hands over two small potions, as agreed. 

The [u]potions however are not what they appear to be; they are filled with poison, not a cure[/u].  The party can sense this trickery through a successful [u]Wisdom (Insight) check (DC 15)[/u]. If they notice the deception, they have two main few options:

[ol]
[li][b]Negotiation:[/b] The party can confront the Hag and demand a genuine cure. She carries it in her pockets, and she'll give them to the party with an evil laugh.

[li][b]Conflict:[/b] If the party doesn't wish to negotiate or believes they can't reason with the Hag, they can attack and kill her. This, however, might have consequences in the broader context of the adventure and the party's reputation in the area. If the Hag is dead, the party will find another two potions in her pockets (these are the cures, but the party won't know that).
[/ol]

How the party deals with this situation will significantly impact the adventure's outcome and Thomas' children's fate.

[h2]Loot[/h2]
In case the party kills her, the following can be found in her home: 
[ul]
  [li]120 gp,  
  [li]2 [i]potions of greater healing[/i], 
  [li]1 [i]potion of mind-reading[/i], 
  [li]1 [i]potion of gaseous form[/i] and 
  [li]a [i]Wand of Wonder[/i]
[/url]

articleParent.title: Key: The Hag, the Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HBG: The Village
articles.content:
[h1]Arriving Back in the Village[/h1]
This story starts when the players arrive back in  @[Oakridge](settlement:7ea6d7eb-ee01-4ebb-9e88-226dca2f1246) after completing the events of @[Mine Your Own Business](plot:ebca0f33-09a4-4b39-85a0-992137e2152c). After completing any fallout from that quest they learn about a strange sickness that has left Thomas, a local farmer's, children in a deep sleep. Either Thomas or the village mayor asks the party for help.

The village includes a temple devoted to @[Ariel](person:dbb2eafb-b59f-42aa-9c8b-f4caea122fbc), the goddess of love and beauty. Her followers often visit @[Oakridge](settlement:7ea6d7eb-ee01-4ebb-9e88-226dca2f1246), bringing happiness and celebrations to the village. @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d) is a priestess there. 


[h1]Thomas' Children[/h1]
Once the adventurers accept the invitation to Thomas' house, they find the two boys lying in a deep slumber. No matter what they try, the children cannot be awakened.

Thomas shares his concern, explaining that not even @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d), a priest of Felicida, could awaken the boys. @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d) suspected some sort of magical disease, spell or curse had fallen upon them.

Thomas reveals that the day before the boys fell into this mysterious sleep, they had been playing in the nearby forest. The boys mentioned they had crossed the meadow where the Hag resides, and she had chased them away.

In the past, the villagers of @[Oakridge](settlement:7ea6d7eb-ee01-4ebb-9e88-226dca2f1246) had maintained a friendly relationship with the Hag. She had been known to offer cures and even help to control the weather for the crops when needed. With a worried expression, Thomas wonders if the Hag's involvement could be behind this puzzling disease.

Thomas can only provide a little in terms of gold, but he does offer the party food and a place to stay at the village inn. With a heartfelt plea, he implores the adventurers to seek an audience with the Hag and secure the cure to awaken his beloved boys.

[h1]Lanita's Flower[/h1]
Priestess @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d), as the party sets out to confront the Hag, asks for a favour. She requests them to sneak into the Hag's herbal garden and steal a flower from the  @[Sunstone Creeper](material:60f26c9b-dd44-49a2-b38d-5e474f78aa59) vine growing there. This flower is crucial for her alchemy, as she intends to brew a potion to cure a disease outbreak in a mountain village.

The flower is distinct with its large, meaty yellow petals adorned with white stripes. @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d) insists there's no mistaking it for any other flower. The party, understanding the urgency and importance of the task, agrees to help @[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d) with this discreet mission. [u]@[Lanita](person:76a411e0-b464-4da4-b588-626b50a54e4d) explains that Ludmilla, the Hag, has her garden hidden, and a magical item, tree, or rock can transport the players there.[/u] To help them find it, she gives them a detect magic spell scroll.

As a reward, she offers unlimited healing to the party and a [i]potion of cure disease[/i].

articleParent.title: Key: The Hag, the Bear and the Goblins

articles.title: HitW: Investigating the Woods
articles.content:
[h1]Investigate The Wood[/h1]
When the party ventures into the wood, read or paraphrase the following description aloud.
[aloud]The wood has no definitive entrance, and the confusing criss-cross of roots, trunks, and limbs makes it impossible to leave by the same way you came. The stench of wet dog hangs heavy in the air. The ground, covered in wet leaves and other forest debris soaked by rain, squelches softly underfoot.
[/aloud]
With dim light and only game trails that loop or stop at dead ends, the wood counts as difficult terrain. Ask the players to choose a navigator and have that player character make a [u]Wisdom (Survival) or Intelligence (Nature) check[/u], with the result determining the [u]number of times the player should roll on the Woodland Travel Troubles[/u] table below: 1-5 roll three times, 6-15 roll twice, 15-20 roll once, >20 roll zero times but read result 8 on the table.

[container:tablebox-90]
[center][b]Woodland Travel Troubles[/b][/center]
[table]
[tr][th][roll:1d8][/th][th]Trouble Faced[/th][/tr]
[tr][td]1.[/td][td][b]Footprints! Wait.[/b] Those are your own footprints. The confusing nature of the wood tricked you into following a loop, wasting two hours of travel.
[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]2.[/td][td] [b]Boar? Where?[/b] One wereboar in boar form charges from the brush! If parlayed with, it shapeshifts to humanoid form and complains about the werewolves invading its territory.
[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]3.[/td][td][b]Illusory Terrain.[/b] The party finds a large foggy pond blocking their path. Interacting with the fog and water, or making a successful DC 11 Intelligence (Investigation or Nature) check reveals it to be an illusion.
[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]4.[/td][td][b]The Howling.[/b] The party hears a distant howl echoed by several howls, yelps, and growls from uncomfortably close by.[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]5.[/td][td][b]Quicksand![/b] The player character with the lowest passive Perception drops knee-deep into a leaf-covered pit of stinking muck. Roll initiative! In three rounds the character is submerged and suffocating. Any player character can use their action to make a DC 15 Strength (Athletics) check, saving the sinking player character on a success. Any three characters working together automatically succeed.
[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]6.[/td][td][b]Dead Drop.[/b] The party comes to a long chasm, 50 feet deep and crossed by a 30 foot rope bridge. The first player character to cross the bridge must succeed on a DC 15 Dexteritysaving throw or stumble and destroy a section of planks, increasing the DC by 2, and so on. If the DC exceeds 20 and a player character fails, they fall. 
[/td][/tr]
[tr][td]7.[/td][td][b]All That Glitters.[/b] The player character with the highest passive Perception spots a set of ruined plate armor. Inside the armor is a leather pouch containing 30 silver coins. [/td][/tr]
[tr][td]8.[/td][td][b]Cream Was Here.[/b] The player character most concerned for Gredel’s cows finds a tarnished cowbell etched with the name “Cream.”[/td][/tr]
[/table]
[/container]

After using one result on the Woodland Travel Troubles table, [mark]two sanguine werewolves[/mark] begin stalking the party. As long as the [u]party does no serious damage[/u] to the wood or its inhabitants, these werewolves [u]follow about 60 feet behind[/u], unphased by any other challenges the party faces. 

A player character [u]looking for signs of werewolves or other tracks notices[/u] the followers with a successful [u]DC 15 Wisdom (Perception or Survival) check[/u], and any player characters with a [u]passive Perception of 15 or higher notice them immediately[/u].

[b]If the party does not confront the sanguine werewolves[/b] after two more hours of exploration, they arrive at HotW: The Ruined Lodge, and the werewolves remain hidden outside the lodge.

[b]If the party confronts the two sanguine werewolves[/b] the werewolves attack and fight to the death. If the [u]werewolves are too powerful[/u] and the GM decides to intervene, all the combatants may hear a distant howl that summons the remaining werewolves. Otherwise, the [u]dead sanguine werewolves shapeshift into their humanoid forms[/u], scarred and bent from years of beastial living. 

[aloud]They wear rags of clothing, and one carries a torn satchel with scraps of raw meat and a leatherbound journal.[/aloud]

Upon opening the journal: [aloud]The first page is signed “Melluria,” and its entries begin about one year ago with the writer buying a plot of land in the wood and building a lodge for travelers.

Then the journal covers many months of the day-to-day business of running the lodge, with a stand out entry listing the pros and cons of new expansions to the nearby city’s mercantile district: better trade goods, more competition, etc. Entries from the last month describe The Howling’s growing intensity, the lodge being stalked by werewolves, then being overrun. The final entries capture the writer feeling betrayed by her own body and impulses, and reeling with grief over the loss of her life as she knew it. In the last entry, the handwriting becomes totally illegible as she repeatedly scrawled about her hunger for raw meat. [/aloud]

After facing the werewolves, the party hears a howl, and upon looking in its direction, they see HotW: The Ruined Lodge through the dense forest.

articles.title: HitW: Items, NPCs and Monsters
articles.content:
[h1]Items[/h1]
[h2]Abjurer's Compendium[/h2]
[container:tablebox-30][img:7002204|nolink][/container]This tome, bound in white leather and penned in glittery blue ink, is a manual of abjuration magic.  It also contains an odd form of [i]Remove Curse[/i] that seems somehow specialized. It targets a specific creature type - sanguine werewolf - and can remove the curse that created them.

[h3]Effects[/h3]
If you spend 8 consecutive hours reading it, you become attuned to the book. While attuned:
[ul]
[li]you have advantage on ability checks and saving throws related to abjuration magic,
[li]you learn one abjuration spell of your choice, up to the highest spell level you can cast. This spell is always prepared and doesn’t count against your number of known or prepared spells.
[li]you can cast the specialied [i]Remove Curse[/i] spell as a ritual
[/ul]

[h1]NPCs[/h1]
[h2]Gredel[/h2]
[container:tablebox-30][img:7002627|nolink][/container]Formerly afflicted with lycanthropy, Melluria has overcome many struggles: “This trouble will pass, child. It must.” 

This hunched human woman looks aged beyond her years. She has auburn hair with streaks of gray, weathered and scarred skin, and somber gray eyes. Leaning on a cane, she wears a patched brown dress and green scarf.

She used to run a lodge in the woods south of Tarnsworth until becoming afflicted with a form of lycanthrope making her a sangunie werewolf.

[h2]Roleplay Notes[/h2]
[b]Love:[/b] “I love music, but the memory of playing escapes me. I spent hours pouring over my broken piano, attempting to force the notes into some semblance of harmony.”

[b]Dream:[/b] “I’ve long dreamed of visiting my sister and retiring near the coast.”

[b]Fear:[/b] “If I ever returned to that monstrous form, I don’t know how I would cope. It’s a terrible thing…being locked in another creature’s body.”

[b]Regret:[/b] “If only I had prepared for that attack…”

[h1]Monsters[/h1]
[h2]Sanguine Werewolf[/h2]
Sanguine werewolves are ferocious lycanthropes cursed to survive only in their hybrid form. Standing upright on dog-like legs, this creature’s humanoid frame is a mess of stinking gray-brown fur, matted with dirt and blood. Distinctly humanoid eyes peer over the werewolf’s snout, glinting like its claws and jagged, gnashing teeth.

[container:tablebox-30][img:7002604|nolink][/container] Sanguine werewolves are descendants of, or were afflicted by, lycanthropes who were cursed under the blood moon: a full moon during the lunar eclipse. It’s told that the blood god was so pleased with the lycanthrope’s brutality, that those turned under their moon were granted an especially ferocious transformation. This unique curse traps the werewolf in hybrid form, making them unable to shapeshift into their original human body or the form of a wolf. These werewolves live in small packs in forests, preferring older, darker woods with a steady supply of travelers, and they often coexist with ghosts and ghouls. 

[h3]Stats[/h3]
Medium, Humanoid (Shapechanger), Chaotic Evil, Level 3+[br]

[b]AC: [/b]12[br]
[b]HP:[/b] 40 [br]
[b]Resistances:[/b] nonmagical and nonsilvered bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing[br]
[b]Vulnerabilities:[/b] to silvered bludgeoning, piercing, slashing[br]
[b]Speed:[/b] 40 ft. walk[br]
[b]Prof. Bonus:[/b] +4 to STR saves, Athletics,  Perception, Stealth[br]
[b]Senses:[/b] darkvision 60 ft., passive Perc. 14[br]
[b]Languages:[/b] Common (unable to read or write)[br]
[b]Spells:[/b] +4, WIS, DC 12, at will: minor illusion

[h3]Special Abilities:[/h3]
[b]Lunge:[/b] (action) move >10 ft.;  +4 to hit, 1d6+4 bludgeoning, DC 12 DEX or prone[br]
[b]Claws[/b] (action) 5 ft. +4 to hit, 2d4+4 slashing[br]
[b]Leap[/b] (bonus action) jump 10 ft. without taking  opportunity attacks
[b]Bite[/b] (bonus action) 5 ft. +4, 1d4 piercing, DC 12 CON or become a sanguine werewolf on the next blood moon

[h3]Combat Notes[/h3]
Sanguine werewolves are stealthy predators that stalk their prey in pairs or small hunting groups. They usually attempt to surprise their quarry with a lunge and bite to take them down quickly, continuing to fight with their claws if necessary. Sometimes however, these werewolves merely taunt their targets from a distance with horrifying howls and simple illusion magic. 

Unlike most lycanthropes, these werewolves are particularly active around the blood moon, they are resistant, not immune, to nonmagical attacks, and they are in fact vulnerable to attacks from silvered weapons.

articles.title: HitW: Silencing the Howl
articles.content:
Tasked with ending The Howl, the party must decide whether to [ul]
[li]attack Melluria and commit themselves to slaying the sanguine werewolf pack, or 
[li]help Melluria break the curse. She wants to be cured, but in her troubled state, the hybrid sanguine werewolf Melluria sits helplessly until the party engages her.[/ul]

[h1]If the party attacks Melluria[/h1]
She defends herself, feebly fighting back and crying for mercy in a distorted, growling voice until fleeing out the window when reduced to half her maximum hit points. 

Player characters who hunt Melluria and the rest of the pack eventually encounter a total of [mark]thirteen sanguine werewolves[/mark]: [ul][li]seven are hostile and fight to the death, [li]three are indifferent and can be convinced to accept a cure with a successful DC 15 Charisma (Persuasion)check, and [li]three are friendly and wish to be cured, like Melluria.[/ul]

[h1]If the party approaches Melluria calmly[/h1]
Even after initially attacking her, she responds in kind. 

With a gravelly voice, Melluria 
asks for help and [ul]
[li]explains that she tried to study her condition with the Abjurer's Compendium, but lost the ability to read and write
[li]admits to eating some of Gredel’s cow, Cream, after other werewolves killed it and left the remains outside.
[li]still struggles to control her curse, she caused no harm to the nearby villages
[/ul]

[h1]Curing the Werewolves[/h1]
Player characters who [u]read the Abjurer's Compendium[/u] [b]or[/b] [u]succeed on a DC 13 Intelligence (Arcana) check[/u] determine that Melluria and other lycanthropes can be cured with the special [i]Remove Curse[/i] in the book, and that [i]Moonbeam[/i] can temporarily return a shapeshifter to their original form. While the book allows an attuned wielder of any class to [u]cast the spell in the book as a ritual[/u], the wielder must be [u]able to cast 3rd spells[/u].

After learning the cure, if the party is willing but currently unable to cure Melluria, she requests to join the party until they acquire the proper magical training. Otherwise Melluria insists on keeping the book so she may one day cure herself, even fighting the party to take it and flee if necessary. Once cured, Melluria and other former lycanthropes return to their homes, or find new ones far away if other werewolves remain in the wood.

[h1]Completion[/h1]
After killing or curing the sanguine werewolves, the villagers reward the party with a feast and commemorate this day for years to come as the day your heroes ended The Howling. At the gamemaster’s discretion, the god of chaos and blood who created the sanguine werewolves may seek revenge against the party.

articles.title: HitW: The Howling Call
articles.content:
Begin the adventure with the party entering one of these remote, forested villages and read aloud the following:

[aloud]The village’s buildings creak in the wind, and the near-empty streets smell of mildew with a metallic twang. The surrounding trees whisper and moan as the wind rushes through their branches. A few villagers work hurriedly, peeking over their shoulders with bloodshot eyes and squinting into the dark forest.[/aloud]

Give the party a chance to wander the street or ask a villager about the job postings, but the busy, muttering villagers pay them no mind. Player characters attempting to eavesdrop on any villagers may attempt a DC 13 Wisdom (Perception) or Intelligence (Investigation)check to overhear snippets of recent tragedies: livestock, neighbors, and relatives slain or taken “by The Howling.”

Then suddenly one halfling woman named Gredel scuttles up to the party, recognizing them as capable-looking adventurers, and asks, [aloud]“Hello! Have you come about the guard work?” [/aloud] She has pale, wrinkled skin, fading blonde hair, simple brown clothes, and one silver earring. Cautious not to scare off the party, Gredel offers limited information about the job and recent events. She speaks vaguely of missing livestock before asking the party to watch her two cows, Milk and Cheese, for 5 gold per night.

The player characters may coax Gredel into sharing more details with a DC 13 Charisma (Intimidation or Persuasion) check. On a success she whispers that another of her cows, Cream, was taken a few nights ago by “a beast from the wood.”

[b]If your party asks Gredel or another villager about The Howling[/b], they break down and plead for the adventurers to investigate the wood and kill the beasts responsible for their losses. They explain that attacks usually occur about twice per year, but the last few weeks have been a nightmare. If the party cannot stop The Howling, the terrified villagers must abandon their homes and move to the city.

[u]They can offer one silver dagger and one silver ingot[/u] that may be used as an improvised silver club. Alternatively, the ingot can be [u]smelted into 20 pieces of ammunition or used to silver one weapon with a successful DC 15 Dexterity check with smith’s tools[/u], which may be loaned by a villager.

[b]If your party insists on protecting Gredel’s two cows[/b], the player character with the highest passive Perception readily notices a third, empty barn stall labeled “Cream” and sets of parallel gouges, like claw marks, in the wood. 

Each night the party guards Milk and Cheese, they are terrorized by The Howling and unable to gain the benefits of a long rest. At the gamemaster’s discretion, clarify to the group that both their characters and these sweet, innocent cows are doomed unless the party investigates the wood and finds a way to stop The Howling.
articles.sidepanelcontenttop:
[b]Where Is Everybody?[/b][br]
At first, the villages of the wood may seem to be populated by only a few rude farmers. Normally the villagers are kind, hard working folk, but they have been worn thin by The Howling. It keeps them awake at night, and they must rush to complete each day’s tasks so they can lock themselves inside before the light fades. Those brave or foolish enough to be outside at night have been killed by sanguine werewolves, and many others have fled for the city.

articles.title: HitW: The Ruined Lodge
articles.content:
When the party approaches the lodge, read or paraphrase the following text.

[aloud]Crammed into a tight clearing, this decrepit two-story lodge watches the story of the wood unfold alone. The front door lies within the entryway, burst from its hinges and buried by wet leaves. Vines crawl through shattered windows, and the interior appears dark and damp. It’s quiet here, but a single discordant note tinks from somewhere inside.
[/aloud]

Besides the foyer (1), every room has a window, and [u]if any player characters look outside[/u], they spot a pair of orange eyes watching from the wood that disappear immediately. When [u]searching in any area, a successful DC 15 Intelligence (Investigation) check[/u] reveals a scant trail of shed auburn fur leading to the upstairs bedroom (8) at the end of the hall. The lodge is composed of the following areas.

[h2]1. Foyer.[/h2] The foyer contains two destroyed entry tables and passages to the office (2), the pantry (3), the kitchen (4), the den (6), and the second floor hallway (7). Deep gouges mar the hardwood floor, the walls, and the creaky stairs. 

[h2]2. Office.[/h2] The wrecked office has a small table, a bed, a desk, an empty chest, and an open safe. The floor is littered with the lodge’s business records and a mix of 40 gold and 10 silver coins.

[h2]3. Pantry.[/h2] A few rats skitter around the pantry’s shelves and cupboards, nibbling on what’s left of the lodge’s grains.

[h2]4. Kitchen. [/h2]In the kitchen, several rats tear at freshly butchered slabs of beef on the counter. The meat ends a smeared sticky blood trail that leads out the back door to the remains of a dairy cow missing its bell. 

Inside near the back door, a reading nook extends from the kitchen. It contains a mostly intact cushioned chair covered in long auburn fur and a single book. The book, Abjurer's Compendium, is a treatise on abjuration magic, and the lack of dust on it gives the impression that it has been read recently.

[h2]5. Dining Room.[/h2] The dining room, in a corner of the first floor between the kitchen (4) and the den (6), is dominated by an 8-person table split in two and several busted chairs.

[h2]6. Den. [/h2]This sitting room contains two clawed couches, a cushioned chair, and a ruined piano. [u]Searching this room reveals[/u] scratches on the piano keys and tufts of fur on the furniture. [u]Every few minutes[/u], a breeze from the window gently rocks the teetering piano, playing one eerie note.

[h2]7. Hallway.[/h2] Up the stairs from the Foyer (1), long scratches line the walls of the second floor hallway, and a soft whimpering can be heard from the bedroom at the furthest end of the hall.

[h2]8. Guest Bedrooms. [/h2]Each of these five guest bedrooms contain wrecked furniture and a few remnants of their final tenants. A successful [u]DC 13 Intelligence (Investigation)check reveals[/u] up to [roll:1d10] gold in each room. 

[h2]9. Last Bedroom.[/h2] In the furthest room from the stairs, the party finds one sanguine werewolf with auburn fur curled into the room’s far corner, wearing a tattered, bloodied dress and moaning sorrowfully. This fearful werewolf is [mark]Melluria, the lodge’s keeper[/mark]. Having caught the party’s scent, she lies still waiting for them to make the first move.

articles.title: In Search of Dragonsbane
articles.content:
[h2]Players Information[/h2]
The players are approached while in a tavern by a slender girl of rather average features, simply dressed, but carrying a lute. She has been performing for the patrons of the bar but appears to pay special attention to the players, particularly if they reveal themselves as adventurers, or if there is a bard in the party. She will join them at their table and offer them a proposition: that they help her find a valuable artifact that has been brought to Haven. If they appear interested, and seem trustworthy, she will ask them to meet her "employer", who will explain their task more fully.

[h2]GM's Notes[/h2]
The Mage’s Guild is very concerned over the rumored appearance in Haven of a rather notorious magical artifact, the harp Dragonsbane, made by the famed enchanter Estrado de Varyos, in centuries long past. The harp carries an array of powerful and subliminal spells that can be used to mold the subconscious wills of its hearers, to any end the harpist desires. As the Guild only legally functions if its members curb all misuse of magic, the possibility of Dragonsbane falling into the hands of an unscrupulous person who would take advantage of its powers is a real threat to the Guild's very existence. Now the Guild needs to unobtrusively find the harp - for if it were known that they sought it, surely the @[Thieves' Guild](organization:08dce3b2-80f7-468b-be7b-dd5fdbfac799) or @[The Black Hand](organization:d1a96263-4191-482e-ace6-360d25106f60) would take an unwelcome interest. 

Lady @[Rowena Geronde](person:c44f3336-7f83-4e89-a5c8-78896cc3f650) of Enchantments, Ltd., has sent the mage/bard @[Melisandre](person:3e36bdc2-4ec6-432d-b9bd-78ad5c800d64) to find some adventuring types to help find Dragonsbane. They should be trustworthy and preferably unknown in Haven. @[Melisandre](person:3e36bdc2-4ec6-432d-b9bd-78ad5c800d64) (see NPC's, Street of Harps) will take the players to meet Lady Geronde (see Enchantments, Ltd., Magic Street) who will tell them about the harp, although she will not detail its powers or how it can be used.

[container:indent-contain]If there is a bard among the player characters, he has an INT + 5% per level chance of already being familiar with the harps legend). [/container]

She can describe it as made of Elvish Heartwood, and that it is carved in the likeness of sleeping dragons. According to their informant, a street urchin, they believe the harp was brought in with other odd artifacts from an old dragon's hoard by a Free Trader. Rowena will offer a reward of 10,000gp for the Harp's return to her, but neither she nor @[Melisandre](person:3e36bdc2-4ec6-432d-b9bd-78ad5c800d64) can offer much help, as they are too well known in Haven.

The Harp was brought into Haven by the Free Trader Trevoris, and by the sheerest of luck, gotten past the guards, despite its heavy Magic (he played the harp to show how worthless it was, unaware that the harp's magic would convince the guards of that very fact). 
He sold it to another Free Trader, @[Ratchet](person:35916a89-7a9e-4dca-ba20-0ac6dcfe0fbd)  who had it for a few days. The-harp's use on @[Trade Island](settlement:eec2fc60-5565-40fa-9047-69924589c400) had some interesting side effects. 

[container:indent-20]
@[Ratchet](person:35916a89-7a9e-4dca-ba20-0ac6dcfe0fbd), lent it to one Dan Vedder to play around the campfire. Dan holds great bitterness in his soul, for his sister was kidnapped and raped by some young nobles in another duchy. His anger at this injustice was broadcast to all who heard him play leading to 
a riot and march on the gates of @[The Citadel](landmark:7b465292-ac23-4b4d-a13a-92af694b058b), where the crowd shouted anti-nobility slogans and waved torches. The Gates remained closed and they were ignored, save for a few dumped chamber pots from the battlements.  Some of those involved who could recount the event (though they do not know its cause) are @[Kazan the Mender](landmark:1119fcaa-fd35-4977-9b86-0a99c6a56918), @[Samoradat](person:69bb37b3-1191-43fe-ba4e-75713bab7da3), and @[Ratchet](person:35916a89-7a9e-4dca-ba20-0ac6dcfe0fbd) (see NPCs, Trade Island). Dan Vedder has long since moved on.

If the players were present and heard the music, they must save vs. magic resistance at -10, or be swept up into the emotional ambiance of those around them.  Even after the riot calms down, those who are present will still be less than cordial to any member of the nobility they encounter for several days.
[/container]

Subsequently, the harp was then sold to a young journeyman, @[Vernadot](person:70250451-96d9-441a-ba4b-3d3db516baf6) (see NPC's, Street of Harps) who took it to be restrung at the shop of @[Kelly O'Shea](person:4ec8a5f6-bf08-4260-8c29-e9145652a54c) (see Shops, Street of Harps) and is now being played at the bi-weekly services at the Temple of the Sky by @[Vernadot](person:70250451-96d9-441a-ba4b-3d3db516baf6), who is a recent convert. Attendance of the services has increased dramatically in the past two weeks, due to @[Vernadot](person:70250451-96d9-441a-ba4b-3d3db516baf6)'s unconscious broadcasting of his religious zeal by "harping on it", so to speak.

Information about the harp’s powers could be obtained through research in the @[Guild of Entertainers](organization:f4889184-3aed-47bb-b7cb-0beeec445e22)’ library, of from the old harper Turlough O’Carolan (see NPC's, Street of Harps). Helvia (see NPC's, Street of Harps) the Master of Journeymen of the @[Guild of Entertainers](organization:f4889184-3aed-47bb-b7cb-0beeec445e22), would know that @[Vernadot](person:70250451-96d9-441a-ba4b-3d3db516baf6) is now associated with the temple. Rumors about the temple's sudden prosperity, useful if the players have found out the harp's powers, could be heard on @[Believer's Lane](settlement:d61d64d3-42c0-42d5-a0aa-e141a9cc1ba0).
articles.sidepanelcontenttop:
[b]Locations/Start[/b] [br]
- Enchantments, Ltd.
- @[Trade Island](settlement:eec2fc60-5565-40fa-9047-69924589c400)
- @[Guildhall of Entertainers](landmark:25e4e595-8496-4eda-9132-424fcea4cb79)
- Temple of the Sky


[b]Key Participants[/b] [br]
- @[Melisandre](person:3e36bdc2-4ec6-432d-b9bd-78ad5c800d64)
- @[Lady Rowena Geronde](person:c44f3336-7f83-4e89-a5c8-78896cc3f650)

articles.title: In Search of Dragonsbane pt 1
articles.content:
[i][b]Note:[/b] This scenario is described more fully in the @[South Corridor](settlement:d684e9e8-9b69-45fc-8352-3de0e6e7f870), but should the players seek the Harp on the Island, there is some information the GM should know in order to provide clues for the players. Or, if they are not yet involved in the scenario, they may have been present when the Harp was played, and be affected by its power.[/i]

[h2]Players Information[/h2]
A number of nights ago, the @[Free Traders](organization:49945ba5-c9eb-4451-8ac9-773ea36d3c92) rioted, and marched on the gates of the Citadel, shouting anti-nobility slogans and waving torches. The Gates remained closed and they were ignored, save for a few dumped chamber pots from the battlements.

[h2]GM's Notes[/h2]
Many nights ago, the harp Dragonsbane was in the possession of the Free Trader @[Ratchet](person:35916a89-7a9e-4dca-ba20-0ac6dcfe0fbd), who lent it to one Dan Vedder to play around the campfire. Dan holds great bitterness in his soul, for his sister was kidnapped and raped by some young nobles in another duchy. His anger at this injustice was broadcast to all who heard him play (if the players were present and heard the music, they must save vs. magic resistance at -10, or be swept up into the emotional ambiance of those around them).

Even after the riot calmed down, those who were present will still be less than cordial to any member of the nobility they encounter for several days. Some of those involved who could recount the event ( though they do not know its cause) are @[Kazan the Mender](person:1dfec34a-1ab0-4e3d-ae41-e4439effd82e), @[Samoradat](person:69bb37b3-1191-43fe-ba4e-75713bab7da3), and @[Ratchet](person:35916a89-7a9e-4dca-ba20-0ac6dcfe0fbd) (see NPCs, Trade Island). Dan Vedder has long since moved on.

articles.title: Invitation to a ???
articles.content:
[h2]Players's Information[/h2]
As the players enter one of the @[South Corridor](settlement:d684e9e8-9b69-45fc-8352-3de0e6e7f870)'s many taverns, they will observe a small crowd gathered around the establishment's message board. If they press forward, they will see that the focus of attention is a small notice, emblazoned in flourishing script letters with the following words:

[container:indent-40]
@[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83)'s House of Wonders 17 Bloodstained Lane[br]
Come and indulge your wildest dreams of ____________ [br]
Any Hour, Night or Day Just Knock and Enter Only 50gp
[/container]

Where you see __________, the letters will seem to shimmer and dissolve and will form a brief description of whatever fantasy the player brings to mind.  Ask each person reading the notice to report what is sayd and agree that their reading is correct.

The on-lookers will be arguing spiritedly about the contents of the message (since everyone reads something slightly different) and its accuracy. Most will laugh it off as some sort of scam, and someone will almost certainly note loudly that the address listed lies in the heart of the @[Labyrinth](settlement:e05f9a94-a44d-4991-b419-28cae89db0ee), Haven's most unsavory neighborhood. Others will not be so sure that the matter is a hoax, citing the genuine skill that must have gone into the making of the card itself. There is a 20% chance that this argument may degenerate into a shoving match or (if no one intervenes) a full-scale brawl.

Should the players decide to investigate further, they will find @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83)'s house on a small street deep in the @[Labyrinth](settlement:e05f9a94-a44d-4991-b419-28cae89db0ee), in the midst of a number of squalid, overcrowded tenement dwellings.
[container:indent-contain]
GMs will have to decide whether the players' party is conspicuous enough in appearance to attract the unwelcome attentions of some of the neighborhood's more larcenous or desperate inhabitants en-route). 
[/container]

Except for the street address, the house is unadorned by any explanatory markings, signs, etc. There is a 60% chance that 1d3 [roll:1d3] other inmates of the tavern will also be investigating the message and will be either outside or inside the house. The windows to the building all appear securely barred, and it will take several minutes to open one up sufficiently to allow entry, except for one window in the rear alleyway, which has a loosely attached board (Perception check or careful search to notice) that can be removed to create a small crawlspace. The door is locked but if knocked on it will freely open outward to admit those waiting.

The room within is a sizable chamber littered with rugs and large pillows (all cheaply made stuff). There is a large box by the door with a slot marked "Place money here. Sit and wait." If any other visitors are in the room they will likely appear to be in a soporific state, but otherwise unharmed (5% chance of someone emerging from their dream while the players are entering). 

After about 5 minutes (whether or not the money indicated has been paid), players will sink into a similar state and experience the dream thought of earlier, as vividly as if they were physically acting it out. At the conclusion of their dream, each will awaken (probably not all at the same time), and may freely leave.

[h2]GM's Notes[/h2]
No one in the tavern seems to have any idea exactly how or when the card was placed on the message board, nor any recognition of the name @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83). A player examining the card closely has a chance (Perception check DC 17) to notice that the last line of its text appears to have been written by a different hand. 

Inquiries about @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83) made on @[Magic Street](settlement:7f47361e-705c-471d-9c89-8beaa6aad852) have a 40% per hour chance of yielding someone who can identify @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83) as an accomplished mage with an avid interest in the workings of the spirit world. He is said to have been absent from Haven for many months.

Indeed, @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83) the illusionist is still off exploring far beyond our fair city's walls. But the strangely possessed house he discovered here remains. @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83) located this building in his research, identifying it as an awesome source of otherworldly energies. He discovered, to his surprise, that the house itself appeared to be possessed by demons - somewhat like our modern-day Amityville Horror except that these spirits seem bound to seek the pleasure of their visitors instead of revenge.

The results are quite extraordinary, to say the least. After a few moments delay (in which the spirits apparently attune themselves to the subject mind), these forces will play out one of the player's deepest fantasies as noted above - whether it be sensual, action oriented, or materialistic. The dream state will last about as long as the equivalent real event. Furthermore, upon waking, the person will carry over the emotional and physical state experienced in the dream. This will usually mean euphoria and exhilaration. 

Conversely, a player whose fantasies involve battles with great monsters of legend will probably wake muscle-weary and gasping for breath as well. There is even some modest risk involved in such fantasies, for if the dreaming character is plagued by self-doubt, he may dream of losing, and perish of heart failure from the shock of his frighteningly realistic demise.

Under normal circumstances, it is impossible to bring any of the objects imagined during the dream state into the real world (thus, the pot of gold the adventurer finds will not be at his side when he awakens). However, a mage knowing ASTRAL PROJECTION spells may attempt to make such a transfer, with a saving roll against INT at a penalty of 5 for an object the size of a man's fist, and greater minuses for larger objects. It was in this manner that @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83) recovered the message card that now adorns the tavern wall.

The notice's sudden public appearance - and its new mercenary intent - is the result of the house's recent discovery by a down-on-his-luck street thief named @[Kerral Bramer](person:98fedeb3-d486-4cc9-b614-a99abe25ef7d). Kerral has been taking in a good haul (about 500gp a day) in the last few days from his little scam. He enters through the back window crawl-hole nightly, grabs the cash from the locked  box and quickly departs before the house's spell can take effect. He will be very unhappy if anyone spoils his scheme by spreading the word that no payment is required to activate @[Walyn](person:fd9cad38-6084-4337-b81d-9befacd75c83)'s "dream house” and may seek revenge on that person (though not face front, gods know). If caught red-handed collecting his loot, he will whine and wheedle, and eventually offer his captors a 1/3 split ("I found it, didn't I?") - he will go 50/50 it forced.

Unfortunately, the spirits are greatly disturbed by the perversion of their penance into a slick moneymaking scheme. In succeeding days, the house will take on an increasingly weather-beaten look, and the walls and floors will begin to creak and sigh incessantly. After 3-4 weeks, there is a 5% chance per day (cumulative) that it will collapse altogether, ending the spell forever, unless Bramer's scheme is exposed and ended beforehand.

articles.title: Is Mikyl Swiftsword the BBEG
articles.content:
[h2]What the Players Believe[/h2]
[ul]
[li]MS isn't happy that @[Lyza's](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) command exists 
    [ul][li][b]True[/b], he doesn't want her to succeed as it might rival his power base.[/ul]

[li]He argued against it at the city council 
    [ul][li][b]True[/b]. It was seen in a flashback when the party visited the @[CT: Council Chambers](plot:f8ec5847-9a50-4741-86c5-eb60047c10e6) as part of @[Key: Clockwork Tower](plot:5dab46dc-b84f-4f3f-b884-45a98b0ac6a6) .[/ul]

[li]He is behind the kidnapping of @[Marita Maggia](person:7c9dab1a-8221-41ca-83f6-0d3b49331e37) 
    [ul][li][b]True[/b].  He did it to discredit @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) (e.g. her team's involvement got her taken by @[The Black Hand](organization:d1a96263-4191-482e-ace6-360d25106f60)) - he paid the Hand to do it.[/ul]

[li]He is responsible for the kidnapping of Oliva Chronosmith and @[Seraphina Steamspark](person:16390999-39e0-4310-913a-308aec509a5d) 
    [ul][li][b]False[/b]. @[The Black Hand](organization:d1a96263-4191-482e-ace6-360d25106f60) did it for their own reasons.[/ul]

[li]MS is the leader of @[The Black Hand](organization:d1a96263-4191-482e-ace6-360d25106f60)
    [ul][li][b]False[/b]. To MS the Hand is a useful tool.  To the Hand, MS is just a different kind of tool.[/ul]
[/ul]

[h2]Support for Swiftsword[/h2]
[b]Supporters[/b]
[ul]
[li]The  @[ Council of Boroughs](organization:382855a0-8975-4580-8c45-37b214223a08)  generally backs him.  They aren't the most imaginative people and think he's doing a fine job with the Watch as it is.

[li]Of the major merchant houses,  only the @[Varrasch Family](organization:3947f0eb-ed12-44f7-a3f0-f04678bec261) and @[Ilorim Family](organization:fdb0d169-0e7d-407b-88f9-d9937f73e872) support Swiftsword. The latter, due to the party's raid on Adrielle's shop ( @[Fine Silks and Fabrics](landmark:123c418e-9d2e-4cf9-affe-234e2cb1317d) )

      [li]@[Guild of the Hearth](organization:64fcc4e5-eab3-45ee-9b58-125887237df6) (just focus on keeping our establishments safe)
    [/ul]
[/ul]

[b]On the Fence[/b]
[ul]
[li]Mayor @[Elsbeth Windermere](person:b2466c19-d35e-45af-b070-831261248185) was a supporter but is on fence since meeting the party.  She's beginning to see that maybe @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de)'s command has a place - [b]maybe under outside of the Watch and directly under her?????[/b]

articles.title: Key: Clockwork Tower
articles.content:
[h1]Foreward[/h1] 
What follows is based on the [b][i]Clockwork Tower[/i][/b] module from [url:https://pennybloodadventures.com]Penny Blood Adventures[/url] (full credits, below). It has been changed to fit the world of Antheros and the Haven Irregulars campaign (and to adjust some grammar that bothered me).

[h1]Overview of the Adventure[/h1]
The adventure begins when the characters are invited to the City Chambers by Mayor @[Elsbeth Windermere](person:b2466c19-d35e-45af-b070-831261248185). They might be well-known adventurers, experts in their fields, or simply opportunistic individuals who are willing to investigate the mysterious tower. The mayor asks them to find out why the tower has suddenly appeared and if it poses any threat to the city.

[h2]Finding the Key[/h2]
Clues will lead the party to @[The Watchmaker's Shop](plot:3d4f5d6a-d60f-4f59-97a2-c989bfc88849), where @[Olivia Chronosmith](person:17bc78d2-213b-481c-8a3b-f2dc64dafe23), a descendant of @[Constantine Chronosmith](person:4397abe5-31b6-4eab-b75f-ac79fd772e17), works. She has a music box that plays the "Song of the Gears". Once they acquire this, the party can approach the Clockwork Tower. When they play the song, the tower will reveal its entrance.

You can introduce some roleplaying encounters here as they try to convince Olivia to lend them the music box. Olivia is eager to learn
more about the tower and might ask to accompany the party or request they bring back any findings from the tower.

[h2]Entering the Clockwork Tower[/h2]
Inside the tower, the party can see that it appears very old, run down, and failing. This may lead them to understand that the tower needs to be repaired and completed. They will encounter the spirit of Constantine, who asks them to find blueprints hidden in his old residence to help complete his masterwork so that he may transport the tower to another time.

[h2]Objectives to the Game[/h2]
Constantine has three problems with the tower that the PCs need to resolve:

[ul]
[li][b]Incomplete Work:[/b] As the party delves deeper into the tower, they'll begin to find Constantine's notes and blueprints, hinting at the fact that his masterpiece, the time control device, was never fully completed. Unfinished mechanisms, half-assembled constructs, and erratic time anomalies give evidence to his  rushed work. To solve  this, the party will need to finish what Constantine started, possibly learning new skills or finding lost blueprints to do so.

[li][b]Wear and Tear:[/b] The age of the tower is apparent not only in its aesthetic but also in its functionality. Doors jam, gears grind, and the once seamless clockwork mechanisms now clunk and whir erratically. The party will need to repair these mechanisms as they progress, which could involve finding spare parts within the tower, using their own skills to fix broken machinery, or even recruiting the tower's constructs to aid them. 

[li][b]Unstable Power Source:[/b] The fluctuations in power create unstable conditions within the tower. Rooms flicker in and out of existence, constructs behave unpredictably, and the constant hum of the tower's energy ebbs and flows erratically. This instability presents a major challenge for the adventurers. They'll need to find a way to stabilize or replace the power source, which might involve venturing into dangerous parts of the tower, mining fresh Earthheart Emeralds, or delving into arcane energy manipulation techniques.

articles.title: Key: Dark Nun's Church
articles.content:
[h1]Foreword[/h1]
What follows is taked from the [b][i]The Dark Nun's Church[/i][/b] module published by [url:https://www.pennybloodadventures.com/]Penny Blood Adventures[/url]. It has been changed to fit the world and campaign (and to adjust some grammar that bothered me).

[h1]Introduction[/h1]
People are going missing. Religious chanting can be heard in the middle of the night. A foul smell rises from the depths of a dark and evil church!

The Dark Nun’s Church is a 5E compatible adventure for groups of 2-5 people. The adventure is designed for fifth-level players but can be expanded to support players up to and beyond level 16.

Have lots of fun, and don’t let the Dark Nuns get you!

[h1]Running the Adventure[/h1]
The adventure is written for 5th Edition Dungeons and Dragons. The D&D Player’s Handbook will have detailed instructions on how to play.

The monsters, spells, and magic items are unique to this game. Each room will have a breakdown of the room description and a list of the monsters, traps, magic items, non-player characters, and treasure. Special items are highlighted in bold text. Details for the Monsters, Magic Items, and Non-Player Characters can be found at the back of the book.

You should run the game how you like. There are no hard and fast rules for how your adventurers move through the game. A Random Encounter table is located at the end of the adventure to ensure chance encounters happen.

You will see that there are several different versions of the Maps. Maps with a DM in the name are for the Dungeon Master (DM). The DM maps have labels for all the rooms that match the names in this adventure book. The maps without DM can be given to the adventurers. The maps are available in full color, night version (very cool when presented on a large screen TV), printer-friendly in black and white, and Sepia Tone version.

Finally, the game includes separate cards for the Maps, Monsters, Magic Items, and Non-Player Characters in both Black & White and color.

[h1]Background[/h1]
The Dark Nuns worship at a Church located outside of the small village of Kilwich. The church was dedicated to Mandos, but is now run by the Dark Mother Superior who has a twisted view of how to worship him. She is a powerful, dark half-elf using the "Books of New Living," three cursed books that contain horrific spells, to reanimate corpses back to life.

[h1]Story Hook[/h1]
People from Kilwich have been taken against their will to the church. The innkeeper, Grilia, an old tiefling, suspects that the Dark Nuns have taken his sister, Lena, and her husband, Tatum. He offers 100GP to anyone who can find and bring Lena and Tatum back.

[quote]
To fit my campaign the hook is that the party has been asked to make possible the marriage of a young couple (see @[The Secret Ceremony](plot:93731b17-b5e9-4347-81a9-304763198ad5) ). It must be done out of town but on consecrated ground. A supposedly abandoned church several miles outside the city is to be used...except it's not so abandoned!  Also, the village got named Kilwich.
[/quote]

[h1]DM Map[/h1]
[img:5685530|center|nolink]

articles.title: Key: Depths of Erdea
articles.content:
[h1]Note[/h1]
[i]This has been modified version from the original adventure (full credits, below). It has been changed to fit the world of Antheros and the Haven Irregulars campaign. [/i]

[h1]A Lost Manor[/h1]
Erdea Manor is a Labyrinth Lord adventure location for a party of level 3 to 6 characters, expecting an average level of 4.5.  This issue[sup]1[/sup] contains the first two of four levels of the Manor, the remaining two levels will be released in Issue 5, hopefully in the next couple of weeks.

While Erdea Manor was written for Labyrinth Lord, it also has a few monsters from the Advanced Edition Companion - you will need said volume for the quasits and some spell effects found herein.

The maps for Erdea Manor are not of the same visual quality as most maps from my blog and products. These maps were drawn in pencil on plain white paper without a grid as guide or later inking. The end result is a slightly rougher look than most of my maps.

As always, if you have any comments or recommendations regarding Dyson's Dodecahedron, please write!

[i]Dyson Logos dyson.logos@gmail.com[/i]

[h2]History[/h2]
In ages past, the Erde clan cemented their hold over the region with a great manor house overlooking their holdings - drawn forth from the hills of stone by strange (and many say dark) magics.

However, as the power of the clan waned, so did the symbol of said power. The manor began to sink and collapse, but also the great hill it stood upon also began to surrender to the earth. Two hundred years ago all that was left of the family were a few eccentric would-be nobles, and the lands of the manor no longer looked over the expanse of the region, but was instead slowly sliding into a broad depression.

Recently a clan of feckless dwarven explorers moved into the ruins of the manor and have basically ignored the structure itself, instead setting up to begin mining operations nearby with some of the old manor structures as their base of operations. But as with all great plans...

A group of ogres has moved into the ruins of Erdea Manor in the last week following a treasure map that their leader acquired from some unlucky adventurers that finished their careers as ogre soup mix. They quickly took over the surface area of the manor, and have isolated or slain the other creatures that were here before - with a group of dwarven holdouts still sealed into the old keep. Vera, the ogress who leads the clan, has already ventured further into the ruins, and the rest of the clan is here to watch her back and they will return if chased off in order to check to see if she returns from the depths.
articles.footnotes: [sup]1[/sup] This intro is from [i]Issue 4[/i] but the entire adventure is documented here.
