articles.content:
[h1|beast]Mine Beast[/h1]
[block:1592896]

[block:1592913]

[h1|sell]Sellsword[/h1]
[block:1592916]

articles.title: MYOB: On the Way to the Mine
articles.content:
The mine is about a day and a half's journey north from Oakridge. As evening approaches, the party can set up a camp near the road. Shortly after, a group (5) of rough-looking individuals come into view, clearly [mark]sellswords[/mark] by their appearance and weapons. Attracted by the campfire, they ask if they can join for the warmth.

If the party refuses, these eager-for-conflict sellswords quickly turn hostile, initiating a fight. If welcomed, however, the sellswords sit with the party, engaging in light conversation. They inquire about the party's plans and destination, offering unsolicited advice to steer clear of the mine due to the rumoured dangers lurking there.

Though the conversation starts off friendly enough, an underlying tension is evident. Eventually, a small argument over a seemingly minor issue breaks out, leading to a scuffle between the party and the sellswords. 

After the party handles the situation, they discover a message on one of the sellswords. It reads, [aloud]"They are heading to the mine. Act swiftly!" [br]--- T[/aloud]
The simple signature,  "T", leaves the party with questions about who might be behind this warning.

[h3]Loot[/h3]
The party can find 45 golds, weapons, and armour on their bodies.

articles.title: MYOB: The Mine
articles.content:
[h1]Generic features[/h1]
Upon entering the coal mine, the party is greeted by a dry, dusty atmosphere resembling a natural cave system rather than a man-made structure. In certain corridors, the ceiling stretches beyond their sight. The walls, formed from natural rock, have taken on a nearly black hue instead of light grey.

The absence of vegetation is apparent, with only a few resilient mushrooms and patches of lichen clinging to the darkened surfaces. A thick layer of coal dust covers everything within the mine, painting the interior in shades of black. Any interaction with the mine's surfaces leaves behind black marks on skin and clothing.

[h2]1. The Entrance [/h2]
The mine entrance stands at the road's end, impossible for the party to overlook. Tracks for mine carts extend from within, disappearing beneath a sturdy, double-winged metal door that secures the entrance. The door is clearly designed to withstand force, leaving brute strength as a futile option for entry.

Centred on the door is a lock, ingeniously crafted in the silhouette of a mine cart, with the keyhole located precisely in its centre. Though the door shows signs of rust, it has been well-maintained, suggesting it can still swing open smoothly with the correct key.The party has three methods at their disposal to open the mine's doors:
[ul]
[li][b]Using the Key: [/b] If the adventurers have successfully obtained the key from mayor Gingar, they can simply use it to unlock the door.
[li][b]Lockpicking:[/b] A successful [u]Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check against a DC of 15[/u] will bypass the lock. This method requires the right tools.
[li][b]Magic:[/b] Lastly, the party can use magic to open the door. 
[/ul]


[h2]2. Mine Cart[/h2]
Just past the closed metal doors, the party encounters a mine cart filled with boxes. Opening any of the boxes reveals they contain mined coal ore, signaling the mine's active operations before it was sealed.


[h2]3. Mine Cart [/h2]
The tracks within the mine fork repeatedly, creating a labyrinth of potential paths. Choosing to follow the route to the right, the party comes across another mine cart. This one is loaded with coal.


[h2]4. Rock Slide [/h2]
As the party progresses, the mine's floor begins to incline upwards, eventually coming to a halt at the site of an old rockslide. Cobwebs drape over parts of the debris, indicating that it has remained undisturbed for some time. 

A keen eye and a successful [u]Intelligence (Investigation) check against a DC of 12[/u] could reveal a significant discovery: [u]a path exists through the centre of the rock pile[/u]. The dust and debris appear disturbed, suggesting that not long ago, a creature or a person made their way through this very passage.


[h2]5. Lake [/h2]
A small lake, its waters chillingly cold and hidden in darkness. On the southern rocks, lichen-type moss patches thrive, while the ground is dotted with edible brown mushrooms nearby. The party discovers human footprints near the lake's edge, leading away but vanishing towards the south after a short distance. A successful Wisdom (Survival) check against a DC of 14 reveals that these footprints are approximately a week old, suggesting a recent passage through this spot.


[h2]6. Camp of Pardin [/h2]
The tunnel ascends in a spiral before culminating at a small, circular plateau. It's evident that this secret spot was used as a makeshift campsite, though the scene is marred by tragedy.

[aloud]As you step onto the platform, surveying the camp with keen eyes, a chilling sound arrests your attention—the kind that sends a shiver down your spine. It's a deep, raspy breath emanating from the shadows, unnatural and unsettling. At first glance, nothing seems amiss; then, suddenly, the darkness itself seems to stir. 

Before you, a shadowy figure rises to its full, imposing height, standing on two legs, a sight both awe-inspiring and terrifying. Its skin, as black as the coal that lines the walls of this mine, bristles with bone spikes protruding at odd angles. Where one would expect fingers, there are grotesquely long, sharp claws nearly two feet long. 

This creature bears a vague resemblance to a werewolf, yet it is clearly something far more sinister. Its eyes, a piercing red, glow ominously in the gloom. As it opens its mouth to roar, a wave of the stench of decay attacks your nostrils. Towering over you at eight feet tall, with a physique that speaks of raw, untamed power, its skin appears as if no blade could pierce it.[/aloud]

The monster lunges at the nearest player without a moment's hesitation. Time to roll for initiative!

At the centre lies a bedroll soaked with blood alongside a corpse that has been gruesomely dismembered. Surrounding the grisly centrepiece are a backpack, several crates, and an open book on the ground. The ferocity of the attack is clear; the body shows signs of having been torn apart by something possessing both claws and sharp teeth, with half of it, including bones, being consumed.

A particularly damaged cage suggests a creature clawed out from within. This unfortunate individual is identified as Pardin, who met his end at the claws of the beast he believed he could control.

[h3]Treasure[/h3]
Should the players choose to investigate this grim tableau and succeed in an [u]Intelligence (Investigation) check against DC 12[/u], they will uncover several items of interest:
[ul]
[li]A petite ebony whistle that, intriguingly, produces no sound when blown.
[li]A leather bag containing 50 gold coins.
[li]A backpack with 20 yards of rope, a hook, a cooking set, and a pair of noble boots.
[li]A bill for 50 gold coins marked with a mysterious "T" signature, hinting at a connection to larger plots afoot.
[li]A diary, the last entry of which is smeared with blood and written in shaky handwriting, indicates Pardin's final moments. The pages, stained with blood, offer only glimpses of the complete handwritten text. For the journal see Appendix C. 
[/ul]


[h2]7. Mine Cart [/h2]
An old, rusty mine cart sits abandoned at the end of a track line, its metal frame worn and weathered from years of neglect and exposure to the damp air of this part of the mine.


[h2]8. Rock Slide [/h2]
A narrow mine tunnel, lined with buttresses, leads to a heap of rocks from an ancient rockslide. Thick coal dust covers every surface.


[h2]9. Old Coal Tunnel [/h2]
A narrow mining tunnel extends ahead, its ceiling supported by sturdy buttresses. The walls bear the unmistakable marks of pickaxes, with both small and large chunks of coal scattered throughout. The floor is blanketed in a thick layer of coal dust. 


[h2]10. Bedsits [/h2]
The miners’ quarter is in dire condition: beds are in ruins, and their textiles are shredded. Wooden boxes and pieces of furniture are scattered and broken across the floor. Claw marks mark every surface, a testament to the violence here. The odour is overwhelming, a pungent mix of urine and decay. Amidst the wreckage, the rotting food leftovers emit a foul stench, with spoiled fish standing out as especially repugnant. It's clear that anything of value has already been taken or destroyed.

A thorough investigation of the area which requires a successful [u]Intelligence (Investigation) check against a DC of 14[/u] reveals a few items overlooked in the chaos: a pickaxe in pristine condition, a satchel containing 87 coppers, and a small barrel of ale, somehow unharmed amidst the destruction.


[h2]11. Mine Cart [/h2]
At the end of the track, an old, rusty mine cart stands, its load of coal untouched and gathering a thick layer of dust, indicating it has been stationary for quite some time.3


[h2]12. Vines [/h2]
The tunnel leads into a dense thicket of thorny bushes, extending approximately 100 feet until the tunnel's end. The vines and thorns are daunting, making passage difficult. While fire can effectively damage them, these resilient plants are known to regrow within a few weeks, explaining why miners chose not to work in this tunnel.


[h2]13. Icah [/h2]
These are the remains of Icah, the unlucky miner. The body is beyond recognition, with scarcely any flesh remaining on the bones. A thorough search reveals that Icah met his end through the action of sharp claws, and his bones show signs of being gnawed on by a predator, possibly something akin to a bear. Among the sparse possessions found on Icah are 1 silver coin tucked in his pocket and a dagger, surprisingly well-preserved. His blood mixed with the thick coal dust and dried on the cold rock.


[h2]14. Coal Mining Tunnel [/h2]
This section of the mine served as the primary area for coal extraction. A fireplace surrounded by small stools and scattered tools suggests the miners spent considerable time here. Mugs and a teapot indicate breaks and moments of rest amidst the hard labour. Empty and full buckets are scattered throughout the area, showing that the miners had actively filled them with coal.


[h2]15. Rock Slide [/h2]
A makeshift ramp, constructed atop a pile of rocks, descends to another tunnel below. Players must make a [u]Dexterity (Acrobatics) check against a DC10[/u] to walk down the ramp safely while carrying their equipment. If they fail, they'll [u]fall off the ramp, resulting in a d4 damage[/u] from the fall. About halfway, the tunnel starts to ascend gradually.


[h2]16. Lake [/h2]
A small wooden boat is moored near the lake's shore, accompanied by a solitary stool, a bucket, and a fishing rod. Although the far side of the lake is mostly obscured by a large rock, a very faint light manages to filter through. Disturbingly, sounds of something animalistic rustling and gnawing can be heard across the water, adding an eerie tension to the atmosphere. 

The boat appears sturdy enough to carry 5 people, offering a potential means to investigate the source of the light and sounds. At the center of this lake is a small rock island, upon which a few boxes have been placed (all empty).


[h2]17. The Lair [/h2]
The far side of the lake reveals the monster's lair, a place both eerie and fascinating. The nest is adorned with eggs and strands of a slimy substance, both emitting a faint pink light that adds an otherworldly glow to the surroundings. 

Surrounding the lair, within the tunnel, your eyes catch the glint of shiny gold veins threading through the rock and gold nuggets scattered on the ground and embedded in the walls. This is the gold the miners stumbled upon before their fortunes took a dark turn. 

The air is filled with the sounds of the lair's younger inhabitants: small monster babies engaging in playful yet fierce interactions, biting and roaring at one another. The lair contains six eggs and four young monsters, or "puppies," each standing about a foot tall.

Upon noticing the players, the puppies hesitate as if assessing the new arrivals. This brief pause lasts a round before they collectively launch an attack. (See Appendix A). The encounter takes a dramatic turn when the first of these small monsters is slain, releasing a piercing, dying screech that reverberates through the lair.

Read out loud:
[aloud]As the life fades from the young monster before you, it releases a final, screeching scream with its dying breath. The sound is agonising, piercing through your ears and reverberating off the cave walls long after it has ceased. In the following silence, a massive shadow stirs from the depths of the lair, roused from its slumber by the call of distress. Rising to its full, daunting height on two legs, the creature before you is both majestic and horrifying. Its skin, as dark as the coal around you, is adorned with jutting bone spikes. Instead of fingers, it boasts long, razor-sharp claws, almost two feet long. Its eyes, glowing a fierce red, cut through the darkness. Upon opening its mouth to roar, the air fills with the foul stench of rot. Standing at an imposing eight feet tall, its form exudes sheer strength, and its skin seems impenetrable, hinting at a formidable opponent.[/aloud]

After the party has slain the monster, they have the opportunity to destroy the eggs, with fire proving effective against both the slime webs and the eggs themselves. Additionally, they can gather gold nuggets from around the lair. They can collect [u][roll:1d20] gold nuggets,each valued at 20 gold[/u] without resorting to mining. 

Should they conduct a thorough investigation of the tunnel - requiring an [u]Intelligence (Investigation) check against a DC of 14[/u] -, they can discover [roll:1d6+4] extra gold nuggets.

Beyond this, the tunnel holds no further secrets or treasures, leaving only the gold veins embedded in the rock, awaiting those who might mine them.

articles.title: MYOB: The Village
articles.content:
[h1]Oakridge[/h1]
Oakridge is a tranquil village that enjoys safety, mainly due to frequent patrols by the king's guards. Its recent growth can be attributed to a newly constructed road linking the kingdom's North and South, drawing caravans and travelers alike. To the village's north lies a monastery dedicated to Felicida, the goddess of happiness, whose followers make regular pilgrimages to Oakridge, imbuing the village with their joy.

The residents of Oakridge are primarily simple farmers and craftsmen. Notably, the village lacks its own guards, relying instead on the protection provided by the kingdom. There's a small shop catering to the community's needs, offering a range of general goods and farming equipment.

[h1]The Golden Wheat Inn[/h1]
The Golden Wheat Inn is a cozy and welcoming place in the center of Oakridge, known for its friendly atmosphere. The inn, named after the fields around the village, provides a warm spot for both travelers and locals. Made of wood and stone, the inn looks charming, with ivy and flowers around the outside.

Inside, the inn is just as inviting. The main room is always alive with people talking, the sound of mugs clinking, and the warmth from the fireplace. Wooden beams across the ceiling and tables lit by candles make it a comfortable spot for guests to enjoy a meal or a drink.

Rico, the innkeeper, runs the Golden Wheat with care. It's famous for its good food that uses fresh, local ingredients and old family recipes. The rooms at the inn are simple but clean, offering a peaceful rest for anyone who's been traveling. The Golden Wheat Inn is a key part of life in Oakridge, offering a place where everyone can feel at home.

An intriguing offer hangs on the inn's wall:
[aloud]
Free Night on the House!![br]
Simply devour our Hefty-Boy[sup](tm)[/sup] steak, one (1) loaf of bread and two (2) flagons of ale in under an hour!
[/aloud]

A Constitution check at DC 15 is required to succeed and gains the winnder a free night's stay, including breakfast.

[h2]Plot[/h2]
While the party enjoys their lunch at the Golden Wheat, chatting with Rico, the innkeeper, a group of miners nervously approaches them. 

[container:tablebox-20][b]Other Names[/b][br]'Diggy' Fuff[br]Sater Toup[/container]The spokesperson for the miners, known as [mark]Cham Clowder[/mark],  bears the marks of at least 50 years of life, his skin wrinkled and darkened from decades spent in a mine. His behaviour is one of honesty and transparency, reflecting his sincere desire for the well-being of the miners and their families. When he addresses the adventurers, he nervously holds his dusty hat in his hands, twisting it back and forth, a clear sign of his anxiety. Humble and hesitant, they share their story. These miners from the coal mine have always relied on it for income, but a stroke of luck led them to discover a gold vein four weeks ago—a potential fortune for both them and the town.

Their joy, however, was short-lived. Strange occurrences plagued them a fortnight into mining. Bedsits were vandalised, tools scattered and broken, and food devoured as if by wild animals. Then came the monster: black with red eyes, long claws, a walking horror. The beast grabbed Icah, one of the young miners, ripped his body apart, and feasted on his flesh.

Terrified, they abandoned the mine, prompting mayor Gingar Terion to seal it off, fearing the creature's escape. 

Now, the miners, desperate and with little to offer, ask the party for help. They promise 150 gp upfront and a five per cent share from the mine if the party can rid it of the monster and restore normal operations. 

But first, they must convince Mayor Gingar to hand over the key.


[h1]The Terion Residence[/h1]
The mayor's residence rests peacefully by the lake, boasting a charming garden with benches. Inviting the party for afternoon tea, he shares tales of the village's growth and newfound popularity, thanks to increased traffic on the main road.

As the party brings up the topic of the mine, his behaviour shifts to sadness, and he becomes nervous. He acknowledges the issue but emphasises the dangers lurking within the mine, dangers that should not be unleashed. Fearful for the village and concerned that news of the creature could divert travellers away, he firmly denies the party access, refusing to part with the key.

If the party inquires why mayor Gingar Terion did not seek assistance from the king's castle, he hesitantly explains that the severity of the threat within the mine was unclear. He believed it premature to alarm the crown. "It wasn't time yet," he murmurs, a shadow of doubt flickering across his face. "We didn't fully grasp the magnitude of the danger lurking beneath our feet."

However, a tumultuous inner conflict lies beneath his mask of concern for protocol. The mayor is acutely aware of his wife Theresia's secret dealings with Pardin and her ambitious, if not dangerous, plans to ascend the social ladder. This knowledge places him at a moral crossroads, torn between his duty to uphold the law and his loyalty as a husband. The thought of betraying Theresia by exposing her schemes to the authorities—and possibly condemning her to custody—weighs heavily on his conscience. Thus, he finds himself trapped in a shaky balance, attempting to protect her secret while fulfilling his responsibilities as mayor.

The party have four choices: 
[ol]
[li][b]Threatening mayor Gingar:[/b] While this approach may secure the key for the party, it has [u]significant consequences[/u]. Choosing to intimidate the mayor into compliance will 
[ul][li]tarnish the party's reputation within Oakridge
[li]complimentary accommodations at the inn will be revoked - if they had it -, 
[li]word of their strong-arm tactics will spread quickly. 
[li]local merchants, influenced by the mayor's account of events, will increase their prices by at least 10%, making every purchase in the village more costly for the adventurers. 
[/ul]
To succeed, the player needs to roll a [u]Charisma (Intimidation) check against DC14[/u].

[li][b]Steal the key:[/b] The party can attempt to covertly take matters into their own hands.

[li][b]Leave and find another way:[/b] Through lockpicking, magic, or other means, they can explore alternative methods to open the sealed mine.Notably, the party might observe the mayor's unease when the mine is mentioned, which could originate from genuine fear rather than malicious intent.

[li][b]Persuading the mayor:[/b] The party can persuade the mayor to cooperate. However, convincing him won't be easy, given his strong protective instincts towards his wife and his genuine concern that the monster in the mine poses a real threat to the village. It will require a successful [u]Charisma (Persuasion) check with a difficulty class DC18[/u].
[/ol]


[h2]Stealing the Key[/h2] 
[u]The key is hidden in the mayor's office, within a secret compartment in his desk.[/u] The adventurers must break into the office. This can be achieved by bypassing the fence from the lakeside or directly through the main entrance. The residence is unguarded, and the mayor and his wife sleep in their bedroom.

Should the adventurers encounter the mayor during the operation, word of their intrusion will inevitably spread throughout Oakridge, souring the villagers' perception of them—unless the party manages to unveil the truth or deal with it otherwise.

articles.title: NotLD Conclusion
articles.content:
The players successfully defeat the Cursed Doll and use the Cursed Mirror to trap and release her soul. With the 
doll’s destruction, the dark magic that haunted the toy shop dissipates, and the eerie presence within the shop fades. 
The souls of the animated toys are freed, and the shop returns to its lifeless state. However, the emotional weight of 
the toy maker’s tragedy lingers. The players have freed the town from the curse, but the remnants of the toy maker’s 
sorrow are still felt. 

As the final blow strikes the Cursed Doll, her porcelain body shatters, and a haunting, sorrowful wail echoes 
through the room. The pieces of the doll crumble to dust, and for a brief moment, the air feels lighter. The dark 
magic that once filled the room fades, and the strange, oppressive energy dissipates. The toys that had been 
moving moments before now lie still, their glassy eyes no longer filled with malevolence, but simply empty once 
more. 

You hold up the Cursed Mirror, and as you gaze into its cracked surface, you see the faint image of a young girl—
her face no longer twisted in rage, but instead peaceful, calm. With a final whisper, she thanks you, her form 
growing faint until it disappears entirely. The mirror goes cold in your hands, the magic within it spent. All around 
you, the dolls collapse to the ground, lifeless once more, and the shop falls into an eerie, heavy silence. 
The curse has been broken. The town is safe. Yet, as you step out of the basement workshop and into the dark 
streets once more, a chill runs down your spine. The toy maker's sorrow may have been silenced, but the legacy of 
his grief lingers, a quiet reminder that some tragedies can never fully be erased. 

[h1]Reward for Completing the Adventure[/h1]
[h3]Gold & Trinkets[/h3] Upon searching the toy shop after the curse is lifted, the players find a hidden stash of gold coins 
beneath the floorboards (200 gp), along with a collection of rare trinkets and mechanical toys, worth an additional 
150 gp. 

[h3]Experience Points (XP)[/h3] Award the players 1,800 XP each for defeating the Cursed Doll, breaking the curse, and 
solving the mystery of the toy shop. 

[h3]Reputation in the Town[/h3] The players become local heroes for saving the town from the haunting curse. Merchants 
and townsfolk offer them discounts and hospitality whenever they return, and they are forever remembered as the 
ones who lifted the curse of the toy shop.

articles.title: NotLD Outside the Toy Shop
articles.content:
Lucille accompanies the players to the dark street but refuses to enter the toy shop, remaining nervous and distant. As the players approach the shop, animated dolls ambush them from the shadows, forcing them into a fight while Lucille stands back, too frightened to intervene. 

[aloud]
The cobblestone street is eerily quiet, the soft tap of your footsteps the only sound breaking the silence. Around you, the shops and homes are dark, their windows shuttered tight, as if the residents are too scared to even peek outside. Lucille follows a few steps behind, nervously glancing over her shoulder, muttering about "cursed dolls" and "things that shouldn’t be moving at night." A chill wind whistles down the narrow alleyways, carrying with it the faint scent of dust and decay. The moon hangs low in the sky, casting long, distorted shadows that stretch across the empty street. As you near the corner where the toy shop stands you can see a sign over the doorway naming the shop: Pandora's Box. 

Here Lucille stops, her face pale. "I...I won’t go any further. They’re out there, I can feel it." 

Before you can respond, movement catches your eye. Small, twisted shapes slip out from the shadows—dolls, marionettes, and toy soldiers, their glassy eyes reflecting the moonlight. One of the dolls twitches, its head turning slowly in your direction. Then, without warning, they rush toward you, their tiny hands and sharp wooden limbs clawing at your feet. Lucille gasps and stumbles back, clutching her mace but making no move to help. "They're coming for us!" she houts in a trembling voice. 
[/aloud]

[h1]Activity [/h1]
The players must defend themselves from the ambush by the animated dolls, who use their small size and stealth to get close before attacking. Lucille is too terrified to fight, retreating behind the players and staying out of harm’s way. The dolls use their False Appearance ability to hide among the crates and barrels lining the street, forcing players to make Perception checks (DC 13) to spot them before they strike. 

[h1]Terrain [/h1]
The cobblestone street is uneven and slippery from a recent rainstorm, requiring players to make a DC 12 Dexterity (Acrobatics) check if they move more than 10 feet in a turn. Failure causes them to slip and fall prone. The street is narrow, with tall buildings on either side. Players can attempt to climb onto the rooftops with a successful DC 14 Strength (Athletics) check, gaining a height advantage over the dolls but losing access to some areas of the street. 

[h3]Cover[/h3] Crates and barrels line the street, providing half cover (+2 AC) for players and the dolls alike. 

[h3]Visibility[/h3] The street is dimly lit by moonlight. Any attack made beyond 30 feet is at disadvantage due to poor visibility unless the player has darkvision or a light source. 

[h1]Lair Actions[/h1]
[h2]Initiative count 20[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Toy Ambush[/h3] small mechanical toys hidden beneath the crates and barrels spring to life. These toys are not full monsters but cause all creatures within 10 feet to make a DC 13 Dexterity saving throw or take 3 (1d6) slashing damage as the toys scurry out and slice at ankles. 

[h3]Tangled Threads[/h3] Marionette strings suddenly drop from the rooftops, attempting to ensnare the players. All players must make a DC 14 Dexterity saving throw or become restrained as the strings wrap tightly around their limbs. A restrained player can use an action to make a DC 14 Strength check to break free. 
[/container]

[h2]Initiative count 10[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Eerie Lullaby:[/h3] A haunting melody fills the air, echoing from the toy shop. Each creature within 30 feet of the toy shop must make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened until the end of their next turn. Players who succeed on the saving throw are immune to this effect for the remainder of the encounter. 
[/container]


[h1]NPC[/h1]
Lucille 

[h1]Monsters[/h1]
3-5 Animated Dolls

articleParent.title: Key: Night of the Living Dolls

articles.title: NotLD The Toy Shop
articles.content:
This eerie encounter takes place in the abandoned toy shop, where players must solve a puzzle to reveal the entrance to a hidden basement workshop. The chest in the shop contains a cursed mirror that holds the secret to the toy maker’s dark experiments. The players will eventually use the mirror to free the soul of the toy maker’s daughter, trapped inside the final boss, the Cursed Doll. 

[aloud]
The inside of the toy shop is a time capsule of faded joy, now twisted into something far more sinister. Shelves are packed with dozens of dolls, marionettes, and mechanical toys, all frozen in place as if waiting for their owner to return. Suspended from the ceiling of the workshop are numerous marionettes, their strings hanging down loosely. 

Dust coats everything and the faint smell of decay lingers. Some of the toys are half-finished, their vacant eyes and stitched mouths twisted into grim expressions. The floor creaks beneath your feet as you step inside, the air growing colder the deeper you venture. Each doll seems to watch you as you move, their lifeless eyes reflecting the flickering candlelight that barely illuminates the room. 

A large, locked chest sits in the corner, surrounded by scattered tools and old blueprints. The floorboards beneath it look uneven, as though something might be hidden below. Thee dolls lie nearby, each one seemingly placed with intent, their small hands pointing toward the chest.

Looking up, behind some of the suspended toys you can see a ladder attached to the ceiling that appears to allow access to the attic via a trap door.

Over everything there’s a sense of something unfinished in the air—a lingering presence that beckons to you. 
[/aloud]

[h1]Activities [/h1]
[b]Explore the Shop:[/b] The players will need to investigate the toy shop, noticing strange details such as the uneven floorboards beneath the chest and the deliberate placement of the dolls. 

[b]Revealing the Hidden Workshop:[/b] Players must interact with the dolls in the correct order to trigger a mechanism that reveals the hidden entrance. 

[h1]Explore the Shop [/h1]
As the players step into the abandoned toy shop, they are immediately met with an unsettling atmosphere. The shop, once a place of joy, is now a dust-filled relic of the past, where strange details lurk in every corner. The players will need to use their investigative skills to uncover the hidden secrets within the shop, noticing oddities that hint at something darker lying just beneath the surface. Through careful observation and interaction, they will piece together the clues that lead to the hidden workshop and the source of the curse. 

[h2]Key Details and Clues to Investigate[/h2]
[h3]Uneven Floorboards[/h3] 
As the players explore the shop, they may notice that the floor beneath the large, locked chest seems slightly uneven. The chest appears heavy, but the floorboards beneath it creak more than usual, as though they are covering something beneath. A successful DC 12 Perception or Investigation check will reveal that the floorboards under the chest have been tampered with and likely hide something below. This is the first clue leading the players toward discovering the hidden basement workshop. 

[h3]Deliberate Placement of Dolls[/h3] 
The dolls scattered around the shop are not as haphazardly placed as they initially seem. Upon closer inspection, the players will notice that several dolls appear to have been positioned with purpose—some are standing upright on shelves, others sit on the floor near the chest, each facing in the same direction. These dolls, when examined closely, have small, intricate details such as distinct markings or symbols on their clothing. These symbols match up with the clues in the toy maker’s journal, providing a trail that leads to the puzzle of placing the dolls in the correct order. Players can make a DC 12 Investigationcheck to uncover the significance of the dolls’ positioning and realize that they are part of a larger mechanism. 

[h3]The Toy Maker’s Journal[/h3] 
On a dusty workbench near the rear of the shop, the players will find an old, frayed journal filled with scribbled notes and sketches. The journal is a crucial piece of evidence, providing insight into the toy maker’s grief and obsession with his daughter, Olivia. Pages of the journal mention his daughter’s favorite dolls, their colors, and how they once brought her comfort. As the players sift through the journal, they will find cryptic hints related to the order in which the dolls should be placed on the display stand to unlock the chest. A [u]DC 12 Intelligence (Investigation) check allows the players to piece together the relevant clues[/u] from the journal and apply them to the puzzle. 

[h3]Dust Patterns and Footprints[/h3] 
The toy shop has not seen visitors in quite some time, but certain areas of the shop show signs of more recent activity. [u]The dust on the floor is disturbed near the display stand, and faint footprints lead from the stand to the chest. [/u]These subtle disturbances suggest that the shop has been visited, or at least manipulated, recently. The players may notice this detail with a [u]DC 10 Perception check, leading them to focus their attention on the display stand and chest[/u], both of which are key components of the puzzle. 

[h3]Hidden Arcane Symbols[/h3] 
Hidden among the toys and shelves are faint, glowing symbols that seem to pulse with residual magic. These symbols are tied to the puzzle and indicate that the chest and display stand are enchanted. A character who makes a [u]DC 13 Arcanacheck can sense the magic and realize that solving the puzzle[/u] is the only way to unlock the chest and reveal the hidden workshop. The symbols may also provide a hint as to the purpose of the cursed mirror hidden inside the chest. 

[h2]Interaction with the Environment[/h2]
[h3]Examining the Chest[/h3] 
The chest itself is an elaborate, beautifully crafted object that seems out of place in the dusty, dilapidated shop. Ornate carvings of children playing and delicate toys are etched into its surface, though some of the images appear twisted, as if warped by a dark influence. The chest is locked and cannot be forced open through normal means, but it radiates a faint magical aura. A successful DC 14 Arcana check reveals that the chest is protected by a magical lock, which will only be released once the dolls are placed in the correct sequence on the display stand. 

[h3]Physical Interaction[/h3] 
Players may choose to physically interact with the dolls or the chest, inspecting their details more closely. Upon picking up the dolls, they will notice small, subtle features such as chipped paint or etched numbers on the bottoms of their shoes. These numbers correspond to the toy maker’s journal entries, indicating the order in which the dolls should be placed. Each time a doll is placed in a slot on the display stand, the players will hear a faint click if it is correct or nothing if the placement is wrong. 

[h1]Revealing the Hidden Workshop[/h1] 
The players encounter several dolls scattered throughout the toy shop, some placed strategically on shelves, others resting haphazardly near the locked chest. A careful inspection reveals that these dolls hold a deeper purpose—they are the key to uncovering the hidden entrance to the toy maker's workshop. The toy maker’s journal, found on a nearby workbench, contains cryptic notes about his daughter’s favorite toys, offering subtle clues to the correct sequence in which the dolls must be placed. 

[h2]Puzzle Setup[/h2]
There are three dolls scattered throughout the shop, each with distinct features (e.g., a doll in a blue dress, one with a red bonnet, a marionette with golden strings). Players must examine the dolls and determine the correct order to place them on a display stand near the chest. Each doll correlates to a hint in the journal, which the players can find with an Investigation check (DC 12). 

The journal contains fragmented sentences like: [ul][li]
“Her favorite was always the one in blue, closest to her heart." 
[li]"She never went anywhere without the doll with the golden strings." 
[li]"Red, of course, was for celebrations... always last." 
[/ul]

These hints direct the players to place the dolls in a specific sequence based on their appearance and sentimental value to the toy maker’s daughter. 

[h2]Player Actions[/h2]
Players must place the dolls on the display stand in the correct order. Once a player figures out the correct sequence using the clues from the journal, they can make a DC 14 Intelligence (Investigation) check to see if their deduction is correct. 

[b]The correct order is:[/b] Blue Dress (1st), Golden Strings (2nd), Red Bonnet (Last). 

[b]If the players succeed[/b] in placing the dolls in the correct order, the floorboards beneath the chest creak and shift, revealing a hidden staircase that leads down to the basement workshop where the Cursed Doll resides. The chest will also unlock and the top will open. 

[b]If the players fail the puzzle[/b] by placing the dolls in the wrong order, the Marionette Whip Trap is triggered. Strings from the marionettes hanging above snap to life, lashing out like whips. Each player must make a [u]DC 13 Dexterity saving throw or take 2d6 slashing damage[/u] from the razor-sharp strings. The dolls fall to the ground as the trap resets, requiring the players to try again. 


[h1]Marionette Whip Trap [/h1]
Suspended from the ceiling of the workshop are numerous marionettes, their strings hanging down loosely. At first glance, they appear lifeless, but they hide a dangerous mechanism designed to lash out at anyone who fails to unlock the cursed chest. When triggered, the strings animate, whipping through the air like razor-sharp wires, targeting all creatures in the room. 

[h3]Detection[/h3] 
The marionettes appear to be ordinary, dusty toys hanging from the ceiling. A player investigating the room may notice a faint shimmer or tension in the strings upon closer inspection. A successful DC 14 Perception check will reveal that the marionettes are rigged to animate, while a DC 14 Investigation check will show that they are tied to the puzzle mechanism. 

[h3]Disable[/h3] 
The trap can be disabled by disarming the puzzle mechanism before triggering it. A player may attempt to bypass the trap with a DC 15 Sleight of Hand or Thieves' Tools check, carefully disconnecting the marionette strings from the puzzle's trigger. Alternatively, if the marionettes are damaged (AC 10, 5 HP per marionette), they become inoperable, rendering the trap ineffective. 

[h3]Trigger[/h3] 
The trap is triggered when the players fail to solve the puzzle mechanism on the locked chest. The marionette strings animate the moment the puzzle is failed. The strings whip out at lightning speed, covering the immediate area (20-foot radius) around the hanging marionettes. The strings lash out for one round, targeting all creatures within range. 

[h3]Effect[/h3]
When the trap is triggered, all creatures within 20 feet of the marionettes must make a DC 13 Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, the creature takes 2d6 slashing damage as the strings cut through the air with deadly precision. On a successful save, the creature takes no damage and manages to dodge the lashing strings. 


[h1]Inside the Chest [/h1]
After the players solve the puzzle and reveal the entrance to the hidden workshop, the ornate chest near the display stand will unlock and open. Inside is a powerful artifact—the Cursed Mirror—which holds the key to freeing the toy maker’s daughter’s soul. 

[h3]Examining the Mirror[/h3] 
Upon opening the chest, the players find the mirror, beautifully crafted yet unsettling. The mirror appears to shimmer slightly, and a faint, eerie reflection can be seen within—possibly the ghostly visage of the toy maker’s daughter. 

[u]Players can examine the mirror using an Arcana check (DC 15).[/u] Success reveals that the mirror is cursed and is capable of trapping souls within its reflective surface. A soul trapped inside the mirror can be transferred to another vessel, such as a doll. The toy maker used this mirror to capture his daughter’s soul and transfer it into the Cursed Doll. 

If the players [u]fail the Arcana check, they are unaware[/u] of the mirror’s full potential and may not realize that it can be used to save the girl’s soul during the final encounter. This may lead to a tougher battle or the risk of accidentally trapping one of their own party members within the mirror if they attempt to use it recklessly. 

[h3]Purpose of the Mirror[/h3] 
The players will eventually need to use this mirror during the final battle with the Cursed Doll. By holding the mirror up to the Cursed Doll during the encounter, they can draw the trapped soul of the toy maker’s daughter back into the mirror, allowing her to be freed and released from her torment. However, the mirror could also be used for darker purposes—to trap other souls if the players so choose. 


[h1]Terrain[/h1]
[h3]Low Lighting[/h3] 
The shop is dimly lit, with only a few flickering candles and what little light filters through the grimy windows. Players without darkvision or a light source will have disadvantage on Perceptionchecks made to investigate the shop. 

[h3]Hidden Entrance[/h3]
The floorboards covering the hidden workshop stairs can only be removed by solving the puzzle. Players who attempt to force the floor open without solving the puzzle will find the boards are magically reinforced. 

[h3]Hanging Marionettes[/h3]
The ceiling is lined with marionettes, their strings hanging low. Players who move too quickly or fail to notice the strings may get tangled. Any player running through the room must make a DC 12 Dexterity check or be restrained by the strings for 1 round, requiring an action to break free (DC 12 Strength check). 

[h1]Lair Actions[/h1]
[h2]On initiative count 20[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Strings from the Shadows[/h3] 
A marionette strings drop from the ceiling, attempting to ensnare the players. Each creature within 20 feet of the cursed doll must make a DC 14 Dexterity saving throw or be restrained by the strings. A restrained creature can use its action to make a DC 14 Strength or Dexterity check to free itself. 
[/container]

[h2]On initiative count 10[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Doll’s Laughter[/h3] Eerie giggling echoes through the shop, disorienting the players. All creatures within 30 feet of the cursed doll must make a DC 13 Wisdom saving throw or be frightened until the end of their next turn. Creatures that succeed on this saving throw are immune to this effect for the rest of the encounter. 
[/container]

[h2]On initiative count 5[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Falling Toys[/h3] A nearby shelf collapses, scattering dolls and mechanical toys onto the floor, creating difficult terrain for the next 2 rounds in a 10-foot radius. Any creature moving through the area must make a DC 12 Dexterity check or fall prone. 
[/container]

articleParent.title: Key: Night of the Living Dolls

articles.title: NotLD The Workshop
articles.content:
In this final encounter, the players face the Cursed Doll, the malevolent spirit of the toy maker’s daughter. The players must fight her in the heart of the workshop, where her power is strongest. To win, the players must trap her soul in the cursed mirror and release it to put her spirit to rest. 

[aloud]
As you descend into the hidden workshop, the air grows thick and cold, and a deep sense of unease fills the room. Half-finished dolls lie strewn across workbenches, their glass eyes staring blankly ahead. Mechanical parts hang from the walls, some still faintly ticking with the last vestiges of life. In the center of the room stands a broken mirror, its surface cracked but glowing faintly with a sickly, pale light. Strange arcane symbols are scrawled across the walls and floors, glowing faintly as though feeding off some dark energy. The atmosphere feels heavy, oppressive, as if the very air itself is charged with dark magic. 

From the shadows, a figure steps forward—a life-sized doll with delicate porcelain features, her face cracked and her eyes burning with malevolence. She raises her hand, and the room seems to come alive around her. Dolls fall from the shelves, marionettes swing down from the ceiling, and mechanical toys begin to rattle and shift. In a voice filled with sorrow and rage, she hisses, "You abandoned him... you left us to suffer!" The time has come to stop the cursed doll, or risk becoming the next victims of her twisted wrath. 
[/aloud]

[h1]Defeating the Cursed Doll [/h1]
The only objective is to defeat the Cursed Doll in combat, but damaging her physically won’t be enough. The players must use the Cursed Mirror (found in the toy shop) to trap her soul and release it. 

[ul]
[li]During combat, players can use the Cursed Mirror to [b]absorb her essence[/b] by holding it up and making a [u]DC 15 Intelligence (Arcana) or Wisdom (Insight) check as an action[/u]. 

[li][b]Once her soul is trapped inside[/b], a player must make a [u]DC 14 Charisma saving throw to free the spirit[/u] and bring peace to the toy maker’s daughter. 
[/ul]

[h1]Terrain[/h1]
[h3]Confined Space[/h3] 
[container:indent-40]
The workshop is cluttered with workbenches, tools, and scattered doll parts. Moving through the space is difficult. Any area between the shelves or workbenches counts as [u]difficult terrain, halving movement speed. [/u]
[/container]

[h3]Shattered Glass[/h3] 
[container:indent-40]
The broken shards of the cursed mirror are scattered across the floor. If a player or creature moves more than 10 feet in a single round, they must make a [u]DC 12 Dexterity saving throw or take 1d4 slashing damage[/u] from the sharp glass shards. 
[/container]


[h1]Lair Actions[/h1]
[h2]On initiative count 20[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Strings of Control[/h3] Hanging marionette strings shoot down from the ceiling, attempting to ensnare the players. Each creature must make a [u]DC 14 Dexterity saving throw or become restrained by the strings[/u]. A restrained creature can use its action to make a [u]DC 14 Strength or Dexterity check to break free.[/u] 
[/container]

[h2]On initiative count 10[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Falling Dolls[/h3] Several dolls fall from the shelves, animated by dark magic. Each player within 10 feet of a shelf must make a [u]DC 13 Dexterity saving throw or take 2d6 bludgeoning damage[/u] as the heavy dolls crash down upon them. 
[/container]

[h2]On initiative count 5[/h2]
[container:indent-40]
[h3]Eerie Reflection[/h3] The cursed mirror flares to life, and the reflections of the players in the shards animate and attack. Each player must make a [u]DC 14 Wisdom saving throw or be haunted by their reflection[/u], taking 1d4 psychic damage as the image reaches out to claw at their mind. 
[/container]

[h1]Monsters [/h1]
[roll:1d4] x [spoiler][block:1473394]|Animated Doll[/spoiler]

[spoiler][block:1473398]|Cursed Doll[/spoiler]

articleParent.title: Key: Night of the Living Dolls

articles.title: On the Road to Mandalai
articles.content:
[h2]Players Information[/h2]
A group of traveling thespians and musicians are seeking a well-armed escort to accompany them on their tour to the distant city of Mandalai. They plan to take their new acts to many of the towns and villages along the road and are willing to pay the standard rates plus a small percentage of the gross take from their performances. The trip will take approximately two months, depending on how many stops are made. @[Fenris Redbeard](person:b2213c6d-55d8-4ee8-afee-cb7865cb19eb), the leader, can be found at the @[Sign of the Jester](landmark:d3fc11ef-1de2-4c62-ac0e-3e998101195e).

[h2]GM's Notes[/h2]
@[Fenris Redbeard](person:b2213c6d-55d8-4ee8-afee-cb7865cb19eb) is indeed hiring extra muscle for his annual trek, for the road to Mandalai is rumored to be plagued with orcs this year, driven from their usual haunts in the mountains to raid in the valley. His daughter @[Clairone](person:ae57adf0-5f7d-4e4b-b86d-07b94275e391) is accompanying him, as a bard. She is very pretty and the apple of her father's eye. Unknown to either Fenris or @[Clairone](person:ae57adf0-5f7d-4e4b-b86d-07b94275e391), the chest used to store @[Clairone](person:ae57adf0-5f7d-4e4b-b86d-07b94275e391)'s costumes in has a false bottom, in which the @[Thieves' Guild](organization:08dce3b2-80f7-468b-be7b-dd5fdbfac799) has secreted valuable papers and jewelry to smuggle out of Haven to their contact in Mandalai.

@[Toric Habadash](person:56d1a91a-fdf9-42ca-b7a4-9ae3aa47831d), one of the troupe's thespians, is the @[Thieves' Guild](organization:08dce3b2-80f7-468b-be7b-dd5fdbfac799) agent assigned to make sure the chest reaches its destination. He is only a first level thief, and this is his first assignment. He is enamoured of @[Clairone](person:ae57adf0-5f7d-4e4b-b86d-07b94275e391), however, and about a week into the trip will make one pass too many, and Fenris will thrash him and throw him out of the troupe (if one of the player characters is skilled in acting, he might fill thespace).

Toric will not leave the area, but will skulk around, and will try to steal the chest after a day or so. If the opportunity presents itself, he may try to recruit highwaymen to make a raid on the party, attempting to impress them with his Guild status. If the party of player characters have shown a thievish bent, he may try to enlist their aid. Nor is he above leading a wandering monster or a patrol of orcs to the troupe, if it will serve his ends.

The player characters have a chance of (WIS + 2% per show) of noticing that there is a particular man who always seems to be in the audience wherever they play. A dark fellow in ordinary wilderness garb. This is @[Yohan](person:9b4f73c4-dfab-478a-86c2-4e6fab01a288), the other @[Thieves' Guild](organization:08dce3b2-80f7-468b-be7b-dd5fdbfac799) agent (not even the Guild trusts Toric!) who has been trailing the troupe, unbeknownst to even Toric. A ranger among the party has a 5% chance per level of noticing that they are being trailed. Otherwise, no one will see him. @[Yohan](person:9b4f73c4-dfab-478a-86c2-4e6fab01a288) will simply trail the troupe until Mandalai, where he will make contact with another Guild agent who will steal the chest. @[Yohan](person:9b4f73c4-dfab-478a-86c2-4e6fab01a288) will defend the troupe with arrows, secretly, if it looks like the chest might fall into the wrong hands, but he will not reveal himself, and will avoid contact (see NPC's, Street of Harps).

Other members of the troupe include @[Simba the Strong](person:7da3461f-aed2-4063-b233-e8e4a7a55494) (beast master), Rafael Swiftrider (horse trainer), halfling brothers @[Bing](person:1c2912cc-9c2e-4c7c-afbf-4851697692ed) and @[Bob](person:a6d18909-f289-433c-ad20-5c682eac3f83), 5 other acrobats/thespians, 3 musicians, two roustabouts, and a cook. Two of the thespians are intermediate level archers, the roustabouts are green fighters (with blunt weapons), and Simba and Rafael are veterans. The troupe will only fight if unavoidable, that’s why they hired guards.

The troupe travels in five gypsy-style caravan wagons, with three heavy cage wagons, and two supply wagons. The caravan wagons are drawn by heavy horses and the others by mules. Rafael owns a riding horse. All others ride wagons or walk. Player character guards will be expected to travel point and rear guard, as well as shotgun, and must have their own horses if they expect to be mounted and must also provide their own gear.

articles.title: Plot: Free City of Haven
articles.content:
[h1]Free City of Haven Plot Elements[/h1]

The are two types of plot element in this section:

[ul]
[li][b]@[Street Encounters](plot:ed9475dd-aa16-4b27-afb4-0f19689d8d6c):[/b] Small random encounters on the streets of Haven

[li][b]@[Scenarios](plot:96156d28-a953-455f-9d76-03279dcaabfc):[/b] Larger encounters that take longer to play out and can be mini adventures in themselves
[/ul]

These have been take from the original The Free City of Haven setting and modified as appropriate for this world.

articles.title: Plot: The City Watch
articles.content:
[h1]Foreward[/h1]
What follows is based upon [i][b]The City Watch[/b][/i], a series of adventures obtained via [url:https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/skd/one-hour-rpg-the-city-watch/]Kickstarter campaign[/url] of the same name (full credits, below).  It has been modified to fit within the @[Free City of Haven](settlement:e22fc33b-10f4-4078-ac2c-fea0be7d20f8) setting in this world.

[h1]Introduction[/h1]
In these ten adventures, the player characters (PCs) are all members of the @[Haven City Watch](organization:d24af512-917c-4a1b-98d0-0b9d9934be00). Their duties will involve patrolling the streets, manning the city gates, investigating crimes, and doing whatever @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de), the captain of the guard, tells them to.

All of the adventures are stand alone (except the final adventure, which is a two parter), though there are recurring characters and themes, so it would be better to play them in order. 

Each should take about an hour to play. If it is important for you to stick to that hour, it will be the GM’s responsibility to ensure that a game does not go over (or too far under). The GM needs to set the pace, speeding things up or slowing things down according to how the players are doing. To aid the GM in this, each adventure is split into several acts, and each of these is given an estimated duration. Sometimes, suggestions are provided as to what to leave out (or add in) if the players get behind (or ahead of) schedule. Also, the GM should not be afraid to cut to the next act if necessary, possibly even in the middle of action or combat. Combat can take up a lot of time in a Role Playing Game, and for that reason many of the combat encounters in these adventures are designed not to be fought to the death.

The adventures are intended to be played with fifth edition of the world’s oldest RPG system, and the stats and abilities for any monsters, spells, and magical items in [i] [b]bold italics[/b] [/i] are  listed in the core rule books, or can be found online. Page numbers for these books are also given if likely to be needed. 

Finally, you might like to know that there is a webpage supporting the adventure. It includes handouts, artwork, pre generated characters, links to playtest videos, and other details for GMs . It also has a contact form that can be used to ask questions or offer feedback. The webpage is: tickettocarcassonne.com/the-city-watch/

[h1]Session Zero[/h1]
If your players are new to tabletop role playing games (TTRPGs), you should make the first session a way to teach them what to expect, and to give an overview of how to play.

Start by having them create their characters and give them back stories. Alternatively, you might prefer to use the pre-generated characters which are available to download from the webpage (see introduction). These characters already have backstories that will enable them to interact with some of the NPCs. 

Either way, PCs are equipped with light armour, a spear, manacles, and a horn for summoning help. They carry three gold pieces each, and one portion of rations. Optionally, the GM might allow each PC to create their own character. 

The party's commander, Watch Captain @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de), is a former adventurer herself, and as such she knows the value of having magic users and rogues on her team. She has selected her recruits from the most recent graduation class at the Academy, specifically looking for those who want more than just patrolling the streets and knocking heads.

@[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) will instruct them in their duties and, as part of the induction process, will test their  combat (and other) skills. The new recruits could perform a few rounds of combat with combat dummies, other guards (or maybe each other!) using wooden swords so players can get a feel for how combat works. 

[h1]Key NPCs[/h1]
-- Lord Tograf :: The benevolent and well loved ruler of the city. He is a retired adventurer and a very rich old man. --
-- @[Porky](person:e267fbb1-0d16-45d6-88c5-2bae4738a1dc) :: The Half orc landlord of @[The Giggling Gryphon](landmark:29260de7-ef14-4aa9-a123-5d0858f00927) tavern, which is the PCs’ home base. --
-- Watch Captain @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) :: A powerful woman in her forties, she is a former adventurer and a formidable warrior. She takes her responsibilities seriously and she expects a lot from her people. --
-- Count Jaspar :: An eccentric and slightly sinister wizard, he is one of Lord Tograf’s closest advisors. He is not well liked by the other noble citizens, but Tograf finds his skills useful in running and protecting the city. --
