articles.title: TCW: Panic on the Streets
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
You are patrolling south along Fogelberry St. (L6) in the Labyrinth when you hear screaming, shouting, and animal noises coming from somewhere ahead of you. Turning west onto Cross St. you see a crowd has gathered around a snarling wolf in the middle of the street. A small sobbing child, a young boy, is hiding behind a nearby wooden crate, terrified. Some of the people in the crowd are trying to rescue the boy, threatening the wolf with sticks, shouting, and trying to scare it off.

The wolf is clearly about to attack somebody. Roll for initiative.
[/aloud]

The [spoiler][block:559973]|Wolf (MMp341)[/spoiler], attacks and the PCs must fight it to the death. If the player characters defeat this creature, the body will disappear into thin air, and the boy will stand up from behind the crate. The player characters might notice (DC10 perception check) that he is not sobbing but laughing, and he is holding a magic wand. 

Another creature will appear out of thin air, summoned by the wand, and attack. [roll:1d4] (or choose):
[ol]
[li][spoiler][block:443368]|Constrictor Snake (MM p320)[/spoiler]
[li][spoiler][block:546702]|Giant Rat (MM p327)[/spoiler]
[li][spoiler][block:567916]|Giant Lizard (MM p326)[/spoiler]
[li][spoiler][block:559973]|Wolf (MM p341][/spoiler]
[/ol]

After the players kill this creature (which will also disappear), the boy will attempt to summon another. The players might well rush to stop him, but the wand is actually out of charges. Any character who hurts the boy will incur the wrath of the crowd.

 [b]When the last creature dies...[/b]
[aloud]
Suddenly, a voice in the crowd shouts “My purse! Someone has stolen my purse!” and a murmur goes through the crowd, as others notice they have been pickpocketed too. One man points at a cloaked figure sneaking away towards an alleyway, and says to you “It was him!” and the cloaked figure breaks into a sprint.
[/aloud]

Hopefully, the players will give chase. If the PCs decide not to pursue the thief, the GM could allow them to question the boy, who can identify him if asked in the right way. Or perhaps a witness in the crowd recognised him. His name is Hux , and he can be found in the house from Round 8, above.
[quote]
 [b]Chase Rules:[/b] The first player to react and say ‘I am chasing’ starts 30’ behind the thief. All other players start 40’ behind the thief. [b]Have the players keep track of their distance.[/b] If one of them closes the gap to less than 10’ before the 8th round, they catch the thief and he immediately surrenders — go straight to The Epilogue. However, if the thief is ever more than 50’ ahead of all the PCs at the end of a round, then he manages to duck into an alleyway unseen, and has escaped.

In a crowded area, if a PC shouts “Stop thief” or something similar, and rolls a successful DC10 persuasion check, a citizen will try to stop the thief, and succeed in slowing him down slightly, in which case everyone catches up 10’.

In an open area, a PC could attack with a ranged weapon (at a cost to them of 10’ as they slow down) or spell (at a 20’ cost). If they hit the thief with a projectile, they will show him down by 10’. If they miss however, there is a 30% chance they will hit an innocent bystander.

[b]Round 1:[/b] The thief is fast. All characters perform a [b]DC10 athletics skill check[/b] to keep up with him. If they fail, they fall 10’ further behind (i.e. add 10' to their distance).

[b]Round 2:[/b] As he turns a corner (south towards Southbridge St.), the thief passes a pile of crates stacked high, and he pulls them over behind him. PCs need to perform a successful [b]DC10 acrobatics check[/b] to jump over them or fall 10’ further behind. 

 [b]Round 3:[/b] The thief emerges onto Southbridge St., turns west and runs down it, dodging through pedestrians. Any PCs with a [b]speed other than 30’ will gain or lose ground[/b] here. A character with a speed of 35’ will gain 5’.

 [b]Round 4:[/b] At the Thieves Market, the PCs and the thief must all push their way through the crowd (make [b]DC10 strength or dexterity checks[/b], PCs’ choice); anyone who fails loses loses 10’. The thief’s score is +1 and if he fails, the party gains 10'.

 [b]Round 5:[/b] The thief has ducked into another alleyway, where the PCs see another pile of boxes and crates, but this time the thief has used them like a ladder and has run onto the roof. The PCs must follow him, but they [b]can only climb the crates in single file[/b], so if any are equally far behind, someone must drop back 5’.

 [b]Round 6:[/b] The PCs chase the thief along the rooftops. Any PCs with a [b]speed other than 30’ will gain or lose ground[/b] here again. Everyone is now getting tired. All PCs and the thief must make DC10 constitution checks (the thief’s score is +0).  Anyone who fails lose 10’, or if the thief fails the party gains 10'.

 [b]Round 7:[/b] The thief finds some wooden steps leading down to another alley, so  he goes back down and continues running. Some of the steps are slippery, as he goes back down and continues running. Some of the steps are slippery, as it has been raining recently. Everyone must make a [b]DC10 dexterity saving[/b]  throw or fall, losing 10’ and taking [roll:1d4] damage. The thief has +1. 

 [b]Round 8:[/b] The thief darts up another alley and starts banging on a door. After a The thief darts up another alley and starts banging on a door. After a few moments (during which time the PCs will catch up) the thief’s friends will  emerge. They are emerge. They are [spoiler][block:1237175]|Bandits (MM p343)[/spoiler] and there are the same number of enemies (including the quarry) as there are PCs in the chase.  The original thief, however, has possibly overestimated his friends’ loyalty. [b]If the PCs threaten or reason with them[/b], and succeed in a DC10 intimidation or persuasion check (their choice), they go back indoors and shut the door.  Otherwise, they threaten the PCs and possibly attack with clubs.
[/quote]

[h1]Meeting Lucille[/h1]
There are two ways the party will meet watchman @[Lucille Rilossa](person:ce375714-67ed-4fad-810b-bc3b0eaf0fad):
[ul][li]If the PC's catch the thief (in round eight or sooner), Lucille will appear to assist. She was blowing her whistle to summon other watchmen that might be in the area.
[li]If at the end of any round the thief is about to get away, Lucille appears just in time to prevent it. She will trip the thief and jump on top of him, holding him until the PCs arrive.
[/ul]

Either way, when the dust settles, additional watch members will show show up. Lucille will offer to take the thief to Market Barracks, allowing the PCs to go on their way. If they want to do it then no problem, she will accompany them. 

[h1]Aftermath[/h1]
The PCs are sitting at a table in @[The Giggling Gargoyle](landmark:29260de7-ef14-4aa9-a123-5d0858f00927), relaxing after their shift. The landlord, @[Porky](person:e267fbb1-0d16-45d6-88c5-2bae4738a1dc), is struggling with the Riddling Rascal conundrum in today’s newspaper, and asks for help. If an intelligent PC gets it right, @[Porky](person:e267fbb1-0d16-45d6-88c5-2bae4738a1dc) will be pleased, and give them a beer on the house. 

[container:indent-40]
[b]Q:[/b] What is mysterious and sounds like a fiddle?[br]
[br] 
[b]A:[/b] A riddle
[/container]

After a while, @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) comes into the bar looking for the PCs. She has heard about the day’s events and wants to check in on them. She tells the PCs that the thief (and his gang, possibly) are recovering from their wounds in jail. Several stolen purses were found on his person, and he has confessed. The boy with the wand was working with the thief to cause a distraction so that the thief could more easily pick pockets. If the PCs managed to acquire the wand, @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) will ask for it back, saying it must be returned to its rightful owner.

If the PCs have done well, @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) will remark that she is impressed by them, and will try to keep the team together in future shifts where possible. She will also give them each a reward of one [spoiler][block:491457]|Potion of Healing (DMGp187)[/spoiler] each, since they are clearly warriors, and they might need healing in the future. 

On the other hand, if the boy from Act One was injured, or any innocent bystander hurt, or if the thief escaped, she will rebuke the PCs, and there will be no reward. 

Either way, she will end by saying “See you for your shift tomorrow.”

articles.title: TCW: The Arena
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
It has been another busy day for you on gate duty, with lots of merchants and travellers coming and going. In the early evening, a stranger arrives at the gate and tries to enter the city. When you ask him his name and his business, he looks flustered, and says:

“My name is Kifka. I am attempting to smuggle a monster into the city to participate in an illegal pit fight this evening in a secret location in a warehouse near the docks.”

When you look surprised at his candour, he adds “Oh dear. I didn’t mean to tell you that.”
[/aloud]

[h1]Act I[/h1]
Kifka is a [spoiler][block:1237043]|Wererat[/spoiler] (MM p209) who has been cursed to tell the truth. If the PCs interrogate him further, he can say some or all of the following:

[ul]
[li]“Ha ha . My real name isn’t Bob. It’s Kifka. Oh dear I was trying to lie there but it didn’t come out right.”[/li][br]
[li]“I think I know what’s happened. A few hours ago, further up the road, I murdered a traveller and stole his money and valuables. I think this amulet that I took from him must be cursed and is making me tell the truth all the time.” [/li][br]
[li]“I’m not even human you know I’m a shapeshifter. In a moment I am going to change into a giant rat and kill you all.” [/li][br]
[li]“I hate telling the truth, I’m a natural liar.”[/li][br]
[li]“I am trying to take the amulet off right now, but my hands won’t do as I am telling them.” [/li][br]
[li]“I’ve got a zombie in my magic bag.” [/li]
[/ul]

Kifka will transform, then engage the others in combat, hoping his immunity to non-magical/non-silvered weapons damage will be enough to see him prevail. If he feels he might lose, he will try to force his way past the PCs and into the city. He does indeed have a [spoiler][block:1237050]|Zombie[/spoiler] (MM p316) in a [spoiler][block:1237066]|Bag of Holding[/spoiler] (DMG p153).

If the PCs can grapple and manacle Kifka, they prevent him from entering the city. They can then question him further and get the exact location of the pit fight.

If Kifka escapes, or is killed, the PCs will have to do some investigating, and possibly stake out likely venues (of which there are the same number as there are PCs) but should be able to identify the correct warehouse by the suspicious comings and goings.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
[container:tablebox-30]
[b]Play this Act by ear.[/b]

If the PCs have raided the warehouse without backup, they are likely to struggle. They might have to endure a few rounds of combat, but all is not hopeless because the monsters will also attack each other, and the event leaders and participants will try to escape if they can, rather than take on The City Watch. 

On the other hand, if the PCs have asked Lyza for backup, the warehouse could be surrounded by dozens of guards, and things would go differently.
[/container]

The event, organised by the Burnt Rope Gang, is a gambling session based on a series of pit-fights between various monsters that the gang has captured. In cages around the room are one of each of the following: [spoiler][block:1237081]|Cockatrice[/spoiler] (MM p42), [spoiler][block:1237050]|Zombie[/spoiler] (MM p316), [spoiler][block:1237097]|Giant Centipede[/spoiler] (MM p323), [spoiler][block:1237114]|Grick[/spoiler] (MM p173), [spoiler][block:1235982]|Goblin[/spoiler] (MM p166), [spoiler][block:1237145]|Ogre[/spoiler] (MM p237).

The gang plan to make the monsters fight each other in an arena in the middle of the room, while spectators and punters cheer and jeer.

If the PCs have been very indiscrete in finding the place, the organisers will have cancelled the event. Fearing The City Watch , they have released all the monsters from their cages and fled.

Otherwise, perhaps the PCs make their move while the event is underway, or about to start. The organiser is [i][b]Braxter[/b][/i], a heavily tattooed man with a shaved head, who is one of The Enigmas. Use the [spoiler][block:1237164]|Thug[/spoiler] stat block (MM p350) if necessary. He also has a couple of henchmen ([spoiler][block:1237175]|Bandits[/spoiler] MM p343) with him for backup.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
Lyza will assess the PCs’ performance. If the PCs have done well, it is suggested that they advance to Level Three at this point. If they have done badly, delay that until the end of the next session.
articles.fullfooter: [center]Copyright © Steve Dee 2022[/center]

articles.title: TCW: The Armoury
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
It is early in the morning and the sun is just coming up. Everything is quiet, as most of the city’s inhabitants are still asleep. Most of @[The City Watch](article:b6a3cf89-af08-4102-abe7-bf68c1f9bd94) are asleep too, but a few are on duty.

You are among those few. You are on patrol through the quiet streets, halfway through an uneventful shift. The patrol route takes you past a familiar building, The City Guard Armoury, where the city’s weapons, shields and armour are stored. There is normally a guard on duty outside the building, and the last time your route took you past the building, about twenty minutes earlier, you had said hello to your colleague Julio. This time, however, he is not there, and his post is deserted.
[/aloud]

[h1]Act I[/h1]
Julio’s body is just out of sight inside  the building. He has been stabbed. A criminal gang has killed him, and released two [spoiler][block:1236744]|Rust Monsters[/spoiler] (MM p262) into the armoury.  The creatures are currently munching their way through The City Guard’s weapons and armour; the PCs must stop them.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
The bakery across the street is open, and if the PCs question the owners, they will find out that the baker, Mr. Muffin, and his wife did actually witness @[The Attack](plot:39fc88b4-925f-47af-a236-be2a5b47e93c). Mr. Muffin is reluctant to get involved, but Mrs. Muffin is actually the owner of the dog Billy, who the PCs rescued (or at least tried to rescue) in session 2, so she will be very happy to return the favour by telling the PCs all that she knows.

What Mrs. Muffin knows:
[ul]
[li]A group of shifty looking men attacked the guard without warning. Soon afterwards, a horse and cart turned up with two giant bugs on the back in a wooden cage. The men released the bugs into the armoury, shut the door, and ran away.
[li]Mrs Muffin did actually recognise some of them they had been in her shop a few times while they were (she now realises) casing the joint. They were members of the Shadow Rats gang.
[li]The Shadow Rats gang are a small group of petty criminals, who would not usually be able to carry out an organised attack like this.
[li]She doesn’t know where the gang is based.
[li]She has heard rumours recently that the city has a new crime boss someone who is organising all the small gangs into one big gang.
[li]A few days ago she happened to see some of the Shadow Rats outside her shop, in deep discussion with someone she happened to recognize. She recognised this person because he is a local celebrity he was that young wizard chap with the yellow beard who was on the winning team of @[The Escape Dungeon](plot:23448b49-1936-4424-8e0b-32620402b325) tournament a few weeks ago! He lives in the big house on Centaur Street.
[/ul]

[h1]Act III[/h1]
When the PCs arrive at Centaur Street, they will find the house locked and apparently empty. The door can be broken down on a DC15 strength check. Inside, it is clear that the house belongs to a wizard. There are papers, books and magical ingredients scattered all over the place.

The house is indeed empty, apart from one room, the study, which is locked from the inside. This door can be broken down on a DC12 strength check. Once inside, anyone with any knowledge of arcana will identify that the room’s occupier has been  focusing on spells to summon monsters, and to open up magic portals. There is a summoning circle painted on the floor.

The study also contains all that remains of the young wizard one of his severed feet. The rest of the wizard has been eaten by a [spoiler][block:1236748]|Mimic[/spoiler] (MM p220) that he summoned and failed to control.

The mimic is injured (on half hit points, since Yellowbeard put up a fight) and is now disguised as a chest in the corner, waiting for the PCs to become its next meal.

If the PCs search the study they will find a pair of  [spoiler][block:1237901]|Gauntlets of Ogre Power[/spoiler] (DMG p171) which they can keep.

[h1]Epilogue[/h1]
The papers reveal that the wizard, Yellowbeard, had recently been recruited by someone called ‘The Nameless One’ to head up the Shadow Rats and undermine @[The City Watch](article:b6a3cf89-af08-4102-abe7-bf68c1f9bd94) as much as possible. He sent the rust monsters to the armoury, and his next idea had been to install a mimic in the guards’ barracks, but this has not gone according to plan.
articles.fullfooter: [center]Copyright © Steve Dee 2022[/center]

articles.title: TCW: The Attack
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
It is early in the morning, just after dawn, and you are on patrol in the most affluent part of the city, when you hear horns being blown, the signal that there are guards who need help.

As you approach the source of the noise, you hear panicked voices shouting, as well as rumbling and crashing, and then suddenly a monstrous roar, followed by more crashing and shouting. Terrified citizens run past you, trying to escape.
[/aloud]

[h1]Act I[/h1]
[aloud]
When you arrive on the scene, you see an enormous creature has entered the city by tunnelling under the city wall, demolishing part of it in the process. Other City Watch guards are unsuccessfully trying to fend off the creature with spears.
[/aloud]

The PCs should notice that they are not far from Lord Tograf’s mansion.

The beast is a [spoiler][block:1237306]|Mole Dragon[/spoiler] and four other guards are attempting to hold it off.

After a round or two of combat, it should be obvious that the creature cannot be stopped. It will then resume tunnelling, heading in the direction of the mansion. Its location can be seen from disturbances on the surface (its crest breaks the surface like a shark’s fin). It tunnels at 30’ per turn but the PCs can outrun it easily.

Hopefully, the PCs will decide to run ahead and warn Lord Tograf . If not, Lyza will appear, take command, and order them to do that.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
[aloud]
As you approach the residence, you can hear a high pitched squealing coming from a garden behind the mansion.
[/aloud]

Assuming they go around back...
[aloud]
In a garden bhind the mansion you see three men standing beside a cart. In a cage, on the cart there appears to be a much smaller version of what you were just fighting. This one is the size of a large dog, and seems to be crying.
[/aloud]

[spoiler][block:1237164]|Braxter[/spoiler] and two [spoiler][block:1237175]|henchmen[/spoiler] are lurking there. The beast in the cage is baby mole dragon crying for its mother...who is on her way!

The GM should make it clear that the baby is just behind the mansion, and that the mother will soon come crashing through the building to get to it.

If the PCs act quickly, they can defeat the enemy, and return the baby to its mother before any more devastation is caused. The baby could also be used to lure the mother back out of the city. If the PCs fail, or take too long, the mother will arrive, destroying Lord Tograf’s mansion on the way.

Assuming the PCs start running towards the mansion as soon as the mole dragon starts tunnelling, and they investigate the noise straight away, they will have five turns to defeat the enemy, leaving one turn to move the cart to a position where the mother can find it without destroying the mansion. The GM should build tension during this time, describing how the monster is getting closer and closer.

Lord Tograf is in the mansion with his family. The noise will wake them but not in time to evacuate the building before the Mole Dragon reaches it, unless the PCs intervene. If the mansion is destroyed, Lord Tograf will survive, badly injured. It is up to the GM to decide if there are any other casualties.

If the PCs fail to reunite the baby with its mother, Count Jasper will eventually arrive on the scene and do it for them, or calm the Mole Dragon down using magic, and send it back out into the wilderness.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
In The Giggling Gargoyle, the mood will very much depend on whether or not the PCs managed to prevent a disaster.

If you intend to play Sessions 9 and 10, Lyza will appear and inform the PCs that she has identified the mastermind behind all the recent attacks (perhaps from a captured henchman, using the cursed amulet from session 7). The villain is none other than Count Jasper, and he must be stopped. Jasper will be a very dangerous adversary, and Lyza tells the PCs she will need their help!

articles.title: TCW: The Curse
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
You are working a shift on the North Gate. Merchants and travellers have been passing through all day, but it is now mid afternoon, traffic has slowed down somewhat, and it is nearly the end of your shift.

A solitary figure is spotted on the road in the distance, approaching the city on foot. As the figure gets closer, you see it is an old woman somebody that you recognise. You remember her leaving the city a few weeks ago after falling out with Lord Tograf because of her refusal to stop breeding dangerous animals. You notice that she has a baby red dragon perched on her shoulder, which is explicitly forbidden.
[/aloud]

[h1]Act I[/h1]
The woman will try to gain entry to the city, and whatever the PCs do she will find a reason to start verbally abusing them. This will culminate with her cursing the PCs and then disappearing in a cloud of smoke. The curse:

 [center] [b]“A curse! A spot upon your head,[br]Rainbow colours ... then you’re dead!”[/b] [/center]

The PCs will notice they all have a red spot on their forehead.

In fact, there is no curse the whole thing is a prank. The old woman is actually Count Jasper in a magical disguise, and the dragon is an illusion. The dot is harmless; it will however, change colour over the course of this adventure, to orange, then yellow, then green, then blue, then purple, then black then it will disappear.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
A nearby fellow guard, come to take over the PCs’ watch, can, if necessary, spell out that this curse seems to suggest they are going to die unless they find a cure, quickly. There are a few places the PCs could go to for help. However, Count Jasper has spent the past few days going about the city in disguise, spreading a rumour about a new curse that is "going around". 

The rumour: This new curse can be broken by drinking a shot of [i][b]Owlbear[/b][/i] urine stirred with a twig from an [i][b]Awakened Tree[/b][/i].

Wherever the PCs go (most likely a healer or the nearby Temple of Esteem), they could hear this rumour. Note however, that it is Lyza's day off and she is out of town, “training a new horse”. Healers/clerics might try to remove the curse, but they will fail. The count has even placed a book in the city library entitled "How to Remove Curses" which describes just this one curse in (suspiciously specific) detail and its supposed cure.

If the PCs go to The Giggling Gargoyle, Porky will be visibly upset to see his friends have been cursed, and will keep telling them how sorry he is. He will however, not repeat the rumour (though one of his customers could).

If they investigate the history of the old woman they can find out where she lived before she left, and her neighbours will describe her as a sweet old lady who had an unfortunate obsession with deadly creatures. They never saw any evidence that she had magical abilities. She mainly bred giant centipedes and scorpions, but she left the city when she acquired a dragon egg and Lord Tograf refused to let her keep it. The neighbour doesn’t think it was a real dragon egg. He thinks the people who sold it to her were con artists.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
If the PCs approach Count Jasper at his mansion, they will find the front door unlocked. In the entrance hallway though, is a [spoiler][block:1237905]|Rug of Smothering[/spoiler] (MM p20) that attacks uninvited visitors.

Count Jasper is actually waiting for the PCs inside, and he will pretend to be keen to help. He will repeat the rumour if the PCs have not heard it yet, and tell them the owlbear and the awakened tree are no longer in the escape dungeon, but that The Giggling Gargoyle has a bottle of owlbear urine behind the bar. Regarding the Awakened Tree, he tells them it has been moved to the private gardens by the South Gate.

Alternatively, if they try to break into the escape dungeon, they will find the entrance locked. The door has a carved dragon’s head on it, painted red, and a riddle carved into the wood of the door:

 [center] [b]"If you touch me you feel pain. [br]I eat, I dance, I go out in the rain.”[/b] [/center]

As soon as anyone says the word "fire" there will be a click, and a blast of fire will issue from the dragon’s mouth, doing 1d10 [roll:1d10] damage to anyone standing in front of the door. Afterwards, Count Jasper will magically appear (“to investigate”) and tell them the same info described above.

[h1]Act IV[/h1]
At the South Garden, the gates are locked, and a notice outside says:

 [center] [b]To enter, say your name and purpose.[/b] [/center]

When a PC says “your name and purpose” the gate will open. If they do not solve this riddle, they can climb over the wall with a DC15 athletics check. The wall has spikes on it though, so a failed roll will result in 1d6 [roll:1d6] damage.

Inside the garden are several [spoiler][block:1236663]|Awakened Trees[/spoiler] and [spoiler][block:1236792]|Awakened Shrubs[/spoiler] (MM p317)
which will attack any intruders. A PC can grab a twig and escape on a DC15 dexterity check (with disadvantage if they have to climb back over the wall again). Each failing of this check will result in 1d6 [roll:1d6] clubbing damage.

[h1]Act V[/h1]
In The Giggling Gargoyle , which is quite busy, an unusually surly Porky will demand one gold piece each per shot of "OB". If any PC drinks it, the curse will not be lifted; instead, two things will happen in succession:

[row]
[col]
[dc]1.[/dc]From the shadows in back of the room, Count Jasper (disguised as the old woman again) will step forward, laughing at the PCs and declaring them fools. He will then cast off the disguise and reveal himself, before babbling on about how there never was a curse, and that now Porky lost the bet. “That stupid half orc said that nobody would ever come in and drink a shot of OB but he was wrong!”
[/col]
[col]
[dc]2.[/dc]Lyza will enter the bar and start furiously berating the Count. How dare he pull this stunt on her people? And what's this about a fire trap/Rug of Smothering? ...someone could have been killed!
[/col]
[/row]

While this is going on, the PCs will have a last chance to salvage a victory. Jasper has left his goblet of wine unattended on the bar, next to the bottle of OB. Porky will notice this, and wink at one of the PCs and gesture towards it. If the PCs (aided by a distraction from Porky and/or Lyza if necessary) can sneak some OB into the wine and trick Jasper into drinking it, they can each earn an [i][b]Inspiration Point[/b][/i] (DMG p240). Jasper will howl in fury and storm out of the bar. When he has left, everyone will laugh. 

If the PCs decline to drink the OB, because they suspect something is up, the old woman/ Jasper will still appear, but will be angry and refuse to accept that Porky won the bet, muttering something about cheating before storming off. Again, there will be laughter and inspiration point, and Porky will give the PCs free beer all night.

articles.title: TCW: The Escape Dungeon
articles.content:
[h1]Act I[/h1]
[aloud]
Late afternoon one day as you are returning to the Gryphon from partol you can hear loud voices coming from the Great Room. Pausing to listen you realize that the two raised voices are @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) and @[Mikyl Swiftsword](person:403bf324-8208-4b8f-98d1-8e98ded30ba5) and she is protesting something he is asking (ordering?) her to do.  Suddenly their voices drop in volume - maybe they heard you come in?  After a moment you can hear them continue quietly in a earnest matter.

Creeping to the great room door where you can overhear without being seen, you manage to get the following pieces of their conversation.
[container:indent-40]
[b]@[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de):[/b] ... not here to be dancing dogs for you.[br]
[b]Mikyl:[/b] But you'll dance for de la Riis?

[b]@[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de):[/b] ... not a private investigation service at your beck and call.[br]
[b]Mikyl:[/b] But you'll run after Claudia's mutt?

[small]After mostly harsh whispers you can't make out your hear footsteps approaching
the door you're listening at.[/small]

[b]Mikyl:[/b] You'll do this or so help me ...
[/container]

The door bursts open and Swiftsword stomps past not even noticing you there. He storms out the door and is gone.
[/aloud]

Allow some time for player action and then provide the following background when appropriate.  Or have @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) provide the information.

[aloud]
Every year, the city of Haven plays host to a tournament. Adventurers and would be heroes arrive in the city to take part in a challenge to test their wits and their strength. Each group pays a bag of gold coins as an entrance fee, and the winning group takes the gold as a prize, minus a little commission for the organiser of the tournament, of course. The organiser is Count Jasper, a Haven nobleman and a talented sorcerer, who is known throughout the city for his wealth, his love of riddles, and his cruel sense of humour.

The tournament takes place in tunnels and caverns that have been carved out beneath the city. Teams of adventurers descend into this underworld and compete to be fastest to make it through a series of challenges and return to the surface. The tournament is officially called The Haven Open Hero Championship, but everyone just calls it "The Escape Dungeon".

Every year, Duke @[Fernando di Cotillion](person:4ead6296-2499-4be8-984e-f8b35b973d6c) pays to submit a team made up of members of The City Watch, chosen by @[Mikyl Swiftsword](person:403bf324-8208-4b8f-98d1-8e98ded30ba5), the Commander of the Watch. This year, following your recent heroics, he has selected you.
[/aloud]

Actually, Swiftsword is hoping for a collossal failure that he can use as an excuse to terminate @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de)'s command.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
Count Jasper is magically watching the proceedings from afar, and will taunt and insult the players with a booming disembodied voice.

You can use the booming voice to make it clear that the PCs are in a race, and hurry them up if they are taking too long.

[container:tablebox-80][img:4560623][/container]
[hr]
[h2]Room 1[/h2]
[aloud]
The booming voice (Count Jasper) says, “Take one item each from the table”. 
[/aloud]
On the table are a number of items that number is one more than the number of PCs. The GM should pick the two items in the 1st columns below, then add any of the others from the 2nd column until you have one more than the number of PCs.

[row][col]
[ul]
[li]An eight sided die made of brass (Always rolls 6)
[li]A small scarlet fish
[li]A salt shaker
[/ul]
[/col][col]
[ul]
[li]A small drawstring pouch
[li]Parchment tied up with string
[li]A silver key, broken into two halves
[/ul]
[/col][/row]

If you have more than 4 players, make up a few more useless items so that the table always has one item more than the number of PCs. 

The items are:
[ul]
[li]The brass die is magical and if rolled, always lands on a 6. 
[li]The pouch contains half a dozen pebbles. 
[li]The parchment has a picture of a circle on it, and the radius is drawn in. 
[li]The small scarlet fish is a red herring. 
[li]The salt shaker is just what it claims to be. It's about half full.
[li]If the silver key can be mended (e.g. using [spoiler][block:364593]|Mending[/spoiler]- PHB p259) it can be used to open one of the locks in room 3. 
[/ul]


If anyone touches an item after they have taken one already, a blue spark causes them 1hp damage every round until they put it back.
[hr]
[h2]Room 2[/h2]
[aloud]
The passage ends at a "T" intersection. On the wall there is sign saying:[br][br]
[center]
Right or Left,[br]
Take your pick.[br]
Gain or pain,[br]
Treat of trick.
[/center]
[/aloud]

To the left is a dead end, and a pit trap. Perception check, DC 15 to spot. 
[hr]
[h2]Room 3[/h2]
There are three giant paintings on the wall, but closer inspection reveals they are portals, and the PCs can step into them. Each location has a silver key visible in it.   Any number of PCs may enter each portal, but it would likely be quicker if they split up.

The silver keys are all the same and match the broken one from Room 1 (if it has been fixed).

The door at the far side has three silver locks, each requiring a silver key. All keys must be inserted before any will turn (i.e. you can't use one key three times) to unlock the door.

[container:indent-40]
[h3]Left Painting[/h3]
A forest scene. The silver key is hanging from a nail in the trunk of a tall tree. It is guarded by a minotaur holding a club. The minotaur is wearing a helmet which is strapped to its horns so it cannot remove it - the helmet has a built-in visor that covers the minotaur's eyes, so that it is effectively blind.

A PC can get the key if they pass a DC10 stealth check (with advantage if they can distract the [spoiler][block:1236549]|minotaur[/spoiler] (MM p223) in some way, such as by throwing pebbles), but if they fail, the minotaur will attack with +5 to hit and [roll:1d10+3] damage. After the encounter, the PC can grab the key and pass back through the painting. The minotaur will sense the key has gone and roar with anger.

[h3]Middle Painting[/h3]
An icy waste. The key is on a shelf at the top of a frozen and slippery flight of stone steps. The PC must pass a dexterity DC15 check with disadvantage to climb them. Each failure results in 1hp damage as they fall, and they waste some game time. Once every ascent, a sniggering [spoiler][block:1236583]|Kobold[/spoiler] (MM p195) will appear and throw a snowball. If it hits (i.e. the kobold passes DC 10 check) PC must pass another DC15 check or fall. Any sort of fire spell will melt the ice and allow the PC to proceed without a DC check (and the kobold will sigh and stay hidden).

[h3]Right Painting[/h3]
In this picture, the viewer is high up in the mountains looking out across a rope bridge that stretches out across a chasm towards a pillar of rock 50 metres away. A silver key can be seen hanging on a hook there. To reach it, a PC must cross the bridge. As soon as they step on the bridge, a [spoiler][block:1236599]|Manticore[/spoiler] (MM p213) will fly in and attack the PC with its tail spikes. If the PC runs at full speed as soon as they see the monster, they can reach the key before an attack if they pass a DC15 athletics check.  The PC cannot avoid an attack on the return trip, though they can dodge. 
[/container]
[hr]
[h2]Room 4[/h2]
[aloud]You enter a room with a door on the far side. To get to the door, you must cross a 4x4 grid of tiles on the floor. The tiles have symbols painted on them. The first row is made up of geometric shapes, the second punctuation marks, the third letters, and the fourth pictures of animals.

As you approach the tiles, the disembodied voice of Count Jasper booms out:
[center]"Unlocking the door is easy as pie.[br]But do it wrong and perhaps you might die.“[/center]
[/aloud]
[container:tablebox-30][img:4566941][/container]

Actually, he says "easy as pi", and the correct sequence is:

[center]
[b]triangle[/b] (3 sides) -- [b]full stop/period[/b] (decimal point) -- [b]I[/b] (Roman numeral 1) -- [b]bull[/b] (4 legs)

 [b]Or, 3.14, which is 'pi'[/b]
[/center]

The door will unlock when the tiles have been stepped onin the correct order. Stepping on an incorrect tile does 1d6 damage electric shock and you must restart from the beginning.

[hr]
[h2]Room 5[/h2]
This room contains an angry [spoiler][block:1236645]|Owlbear[/spoiler] (MM p249) locked in a cage. A portcullis blocks the only way forward out of the room. There are eight brass levers on the wall next to it. The only way to open the portcullis is to pull lever #6. Pulling any other lever will open the Owlbear’s cage.

If the players ask, yes, the levers are made of the same type of brass as the die from Room One. 
[hr]
[h2]Room 6[/h2]
There are three doors, each with a riddle on them:

[row]
[col3]
LEFT DOOR[br]
I come before house, top, and frog.[br]
All around me is the yap of a dog.
[/col3]
[col3]
MIDDLE DOOR[br]
The head of a raven, the middle of a bat,[br]
I have claws and teeth, and the tail of a cat.
[/col3]
[col3]
RIGHT DOOR[br]
I come before fish, and also bean[br]
I come after royal, but I am not queen.
[/col3]
[/row]

Behind each door is a room containing the door to the next room, and a monster the PCs must fight: an [spoiler][block:1236663]|Awakened Tree[/spoiler] (MM p317), a [spoiler][block:1236668]|Giant Rat[/spoiler] (MM p327), and an [spoiler][block:1236671]|Ochre Jelly[/spoiler] (MM p243), respectively.
[hr]
[h2]Room 7[/h2]
[aloud]
You enter a very large room that is barren except for a door on either side and a sign on the far wall.  Stepping futher into the room you hear the booming voice say: "Do not follow the instruction on this sign. Do the opposite!"  

Looking at the sign you can see it says: [b]"You must not refrain from not going through the door on the right."[/b]
[/aloud]

The left corridor leads to a net trap triggered by a tripwire (DC15 to spot) and a dead end. The right corridor slopes upwards to the exit.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
[aloud]
You emerge, blinking, into the light, and hear the cheer of a crowd. You hear the voice of Count Jasper congratulating you on escaping the dungeon, and you see that you are in the market square. Lord Tograf , wearing fine robes and a turban, is smiling at you from a throne on a raised platform, surrounded by other members of the city’s nobility on comfortable chairs, plus @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de),  A healer rushes over to tend to your wounds.

Later on, in the prize ceremony, Count Jasper announces, to a huge cheer, that the winning team was the group calling itself The Enigmas, consisting of a red haired warrior woman, a young wizard with a yellow beard, and a heavily tattooed man with a shaved head. 
[/aloud]

If [spoiler][block:1235856]|Barnacle Betty[/spoiler] from [i]@[The Nightmare](plot:a927eb9b-6d0b-464c-91a5-7b4eed63e091)[/i] survived, and the PCs ever laid eyes on her, the warrior woman will look oddly familiar to the PCs.

If the PCs have done well, then end this session with the following:
[aloud]
@[Duke Fernando](person:4ead6296-2499-4be8-984e-f8b35b973d6c) comes to the stage to make a special announcement that the team representing The City Watch this year has done better than ever before, and Haven should be proud of them. The rest of The City Watch cheer loudly, banging their spears on their shields in a heart warming show of support. While this is going on, @[Lyza](person:7bdbde76-bf2e-4c7d-9d8a-87b0d66a54de) gives you a thumbs up, and mouths the words “Well done.”
[/aloud]

If you feel that the players have performed exceptionally well and solved every puzzle and encounter as quickly and efficiently as reasonably possible, then it is acceptable that they could actually win the tournament. However, they are not eligible for the prize money, as they did not pay the entrance fee.

[h1]Epilogue[/h1]
When the party eventually makes their way back to the Gryphon they will find Porky trying to work out today's riddle.

[aloud]
In halls of stone where echoes sleep, [br]A guardian of secrets, its vigil keeps. [br]With no eyes to see, no ears to hear,[br] Yet senses all who venture near. [br]What am I?
[/aloud]

If the players are having a hard time Porky will say...

[aloud]
Oh yeah, there are 4 choices: A) A ghostly whisper B) A magical rune C) A stone golem D) A living shadow
[/aloud]

The answer is B) A magical rune.  It is explained as follows:

[container:indent-40]
The riddle describes something in halls of stone (often found in dungeons or ancient temples), which doesn't have physical senses but can detect presence. A magical rune fits this description as it is often depicted as being enchanted to sense and react to the presence of adventurers or intruders.
[/container]
articles.fullfooter: [center]Copyright © Steve Dee 2022[/center]

articles.title: TCW: The Nightmare
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction [/h1]
 [aloud]
You are on patrol along the harbour in the South Docks late at night when a sudden crashing noise and the sound of splintering wood sends you running towards the dock where the noise came from. Once there, you are surprised to see that a big wooden sailing ship bearing the name [i]The Nightmare[/i] has crashed into one of the jetties, virtually destroying it, before coming to a stop. You can see nobody on deck, and the ship is eerily quiet.

Standing nearby is the harbour master, a Halfling named [b]Roscoe[/b], scratching his head. Seeing you, he says “I don't like the look of this. Where are the crew, and how did it get here with no one at the wheel?”

After a pause, while you take it all in, he adds "The other thing I don't like the look of, is that" and he points upwards to the flag attached to the main mast a skull and crossbones.
 [/aloud]
 
 [h1]Act I[/h1]
The answer to the mystery is that the 15 strong crew of [i]The Nightmare[/i] were mostly killed in their sleep by a small pack of [i][b]Ghouls[/i][/b] that got on board late at night. A few pirates managed to wake up in time to fight back, killing most of the ghouls before being overcome themselves. Only The Nightmare ’s captain and one ghoul (or possibly two, see below) have survived.

The captain has a [spoiler][block:476556]|Ring of Invisibility[/spoiler] (DMG p191), and she has used it to evade the ghoul She has desperately and singlehandedly  steered the ship to the nearest portover a period of several days. The captain is still on board, invisible, and she will watch the PCs from a distance. She hopes they will kill the remaining ghoul, because there is a valuable treasure in the hold that she wants to reach.

[container:tablebox-30]
 [b]This part of the adventure is all about creating an atmosphere and instilling a feeling of dread in the players.[/b]
[/container]
The PCs will probably now investigate the ship. If the players hesitate too long, they will hear a faint female voice calling for help from inside the ship. It is the captain, trying to lure the PCs on board. She wants the ghoul dealt with quickly before too many other people arrive.

A map of the ship and key are below. However, in addition to these numbered rooms, the GM should add the random events described below whenever the mood feels right [roll:1d5] .

[ol]
[li]The PCs hear a sound to their right and spin the torch around just in time to see a rat scurrying across the room. It hides behind some crates.
[br]
[li]The PCs hear soft footsteps around them, but can't be sure where they are coming from, and they don’t see anyone there. 
[br]
[li]On top of a bench someone will find a dead rat with a bite taken out of it.
[br]
[li]A player will tread on something. When they lower the torch to see what it is, it is a severed and heavily tattooed human arm.
[br]
[li]The PCs hear a sustained creaking sound, then the ship suddenly lurches, causing the PCs to stagger, but not fall. They realise that the tide is going out, and the falling river level is causing the ship to move.
[/ol]

[container:tablebox-50]
 [center][b]The Nightmare: Floorplan[/b][/center]
[img:4841799|nolink]
[/container]
[mark]The main deck (1), foredeck (2) and quarterdeck (3) can be investigated by the light of the moon. Everywhere else is dark requiring some sort of light for those without darkvision. Note that even with darkvision, a light (which cancels darkvision) is required to see colour. Roscoe can throw them a torch if they ask. Dark vision alone will not [/mark]
[br][br]
[ol]
[li][b]Main Deck:[/b]  The main deck is fairly typical for a ship of this type except for the complete lack of crew. Stairs lead up to the foredeck (2). Beside each staircase is a door. In the middle of the deck is a grated hatchway with stairs down on either side. The quarterdeck overhangs the main deck somewhat and you can see the ship's wheel above. Below the overhang can be seen three doors and the stairway up. [br][mark]A careful search of the deck (perception, DC 14) will turn up a blood stain on the top steps of the stairs just forward of the central hatch.[/mark]

[li][b]Foredeck:[/b] There are giant crossbows (loaded) on the foredeck. Nothing seems out of place.

[li][b]Quarterdeck:[/b] There is a huge, dried bloodstain at the helm. Upon seeing that you can also see small blood spatters leading back down the stairs. [mark]On a perception check (DC 12, DC 8 if strong light is used) it as though a body was dragged that way.[/mark]

[li][b]Captain’s Quarters:[/b] The room is furnished with a double bed and a leather armchair. It is lavishly decorated with fur rugs, silk tapestries and numerous pretty ornaments - statuettes, vases, coloured glass bottles. There is little of value in here. A PC passing a perception check (DC 10) will notice that the room seems to belong to a woman.

[li][b]Officers Mess:[/b] This room occupies the entire space under the foredeck. There is a large table scattered with plates, utensils, mugs and the remains of a meal. There is also a desk with charts and other papers strewn about. An overturned inkwell has spilled its contents, which are now dried.

[li][b]Lower Deck:[/b] The lower deck is dark and reeks of death and decay. A door leads to the officers’ quarters in the stern (8). The trail of blood leads into the crew's quarters (5). Hundreds of flies buzz about and the fetid stench is so overpowering the PCs must make a [mark]constitution check (DC 13) to avoid throwing up.[/mark] As soon as one PC throws up , the others have disadvantage on their rolls. In the room is a barrel full of severed body parts crawling with maggots. There is also a bolted hatch in the floor.

[li][b]Crew Quarters:[/b] Hammocks are attached to beams and walls. Several of them are covered with dark maroon stains, and there are more dried pools of blood dotted around the room, and blood spatters are all over the floor and up the walls. There are discarded swords, daggers, scimitars, and rapiers lying around.

[li][b]Officers Quarters:[/b] The first officer (starboard) and quartermaster (portside) live here. These rooms are better than crew quarters and have several posonal touches such as a picture or tow, reading material etc. And signs of a struggle and blood stains everywhere.

[li][b]Ship Stores:[/b] This area contains typical supplies for a ship - food, water etc. along with materials for making repairs.
[/ol]

Eventually the PCs should realize they have searched everywhere on the ship, except for the bolted hatch in the middle of the deck leading down into the cargo hold (10). The bolt slides back easily, and the hatch swings open to reveal a rope ladder leading down into the darkness.

[h1]Act II[/h1]
[aloud]
As you creep nervously down the ladder, the stench is somehow getting worse. There are more flies and rats than you have ever seen in one place before.

Your torches shed enough light for you to be able to tell that this part of the ship is the cargo hold. There are sacks and barrels dotted around, but your eyes are drawn to a mound, aft of the ladder. As your eyes adjust to the gloom, you realise that you are looking at a pile of putrid, dismembered corpses. Then you notice that one of the corpses is still intact, and is slowly moving around the pile. Its hairless skin is grey, it is tall and emaciated, and it has huge hands with terrible claws at the end of its long fingers with which it is holding a severed human limb. You can't tell in the darkness whether it is an arm or a leg, but the creature is gnawing on it the way that a human might eat a chicken leg.

When the creature sees you, it drops its meal, and fixes its malevolent sunken green eyes on you. It smiles, revealing a mouth full of pointed teeth, and a long thin tongue that flicks around its teeth and lips. Suddenly, with terrifying speed, the Ghoul attacks.
[/aloud]

The [spoiler][block:469842]|Ghoul[/spoiler] (MM p148) ferociously launches itself [mark]at the nearest non-elf target. If there are more than 3 PCs, there will be a [i][b]second ghoul[/b][/i] hiding behind a barrel[/mark], which will attack shortly after the first one.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
The captain,[spoiler][block:1235856]|Barnacle Betty[/spoiler]knows that the pirates in her crew kept their valuables on their persons at all times, and they had all recently been paid following some successful piracy. This means that the pile of corpses in the hold includes about 10 pouches of gold coins and other treasure. Now that the captain has lost her ship and her source of income, she needs that gold. When all ghouls have been killed, she will make her move.

If the PCs are still strong, she will use her invisibility to attack from the shadows, or try to trick the PCs into running away or surrendering by suggesting there is powerful magic at work and/or reinforcements on the way.

If the PCs are weak (maybe one of them is unconscious), she will threaten them directly, saying she will fire a crossbow bolt at the head of the most injured PC, unless they stand aside and let her fetch the gold.

If her tricks and threats are unsuccessful, Betty will try negotiating and/or bribing the PCs, offering to share the gold with them. She will try to remain invisible so that the PCs will not be able to recognise her later. If she is forced to reveal herself, she will be wearing a bandana as a facemask. If she has to fight, she is a formidable adversary.

Betty does not want to kill the PCs. She knows that if she kills one member of The City Watch then others will come looking for her. If she is winning the fight, she will encourage the PCs to surrender and let her take the gold. The PCs might not be able to defeat the captain in direct combat (or there might not be time if restricted to one hour). However, there are a few other ways the encounter could end:

[ul]
[li]The PCs could lock her in the hold by going back up the ladder and bolting the hatch while she is distracted looking for the gold
[br][br]
[li]If she loses more than half her her hit points she will flee, abandoning the gold.
[br][br]
[li]If the PCs allow her to (or cannot stop her), the captain will grab as much gold as she can and then flee The Nightmare, leaving the PCs to recover.
[br][br]
[li]Another patrol of City Watch will eventually arrive on the scene (especially if a PC has rung their watch-bell), and the PCs will hear them on the decks above. The captain will flee when this happens, abandoning the gold.
[br][br]
[li]If the PCs accept a bribe or defeat the captain and take the treasure themselves, they will each be able to get 10gp and a pearl worth 50gp. 
[/ul]

[h1]Epilogue[/h1]
Back in The Giggling Gargoyle , which is quiet (it is early morning, after all ), the traumatised PCs are sitting in silence at a table with a beer each, staring into space. There are probably loose ends to tie up in the story, so Lyza will appear again for a debrief. Either way, Porky will try to cheer them up with today’s Riddling Rascal conundrum:

[container:indent-40]
[b]Q:[/b] What has three hands, three feet, and three eyes?[br][br]
[b]A:[/b] Two Pirates
[/container]

Porky thinks this is hilarious.

articles.title: TCW: The Quest
articles.content:
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
[aloud]
Following the revelation that Count Jasper is the chief villain behind the recent assassination attempt and the rise in organised crime, Captain Lyza has enlisted you in her plan to bring him to justice. She has sent you on a mission to visit her old friend Marcus the Mage, a wizard who lives in a white tower on the edge of a forest half a day’s ride north of the city. Although there are known to be a number of apprentices living at the tower, it is Marcus himself who greets you at the door, looking concerned. He says that he has been expecting you, then ushers you inside and offers you tea.
[/aloud]

[h1]Act I[/h1]
Marcus [b]will[/b] impart the following information:
[ul]
[li]Long ago, Marcus, Lyza and Lord Tograf, along with a formidable warrior named Henk, were friends. Actually, they were a party of adventurers...until some of them got too old. Marcus, especially, is now very old and frail.[/li][br]
[li]He has been in communication with “young Lyza ” and he wants to help her (and the PCs) defeat this young upstart Count Jasper that she has been telling him about.[/li][br]
[li]The PCs must go on a quest to acquire The Sceptre of Exilium that is hidden in the tunnels inside Mount Malice.[/li][br]
[li]He has drawn them a map to where the sceptre has been hidden.[/li]
[/ul]

[container:tablebox-30]Marcus is unaware that since retiring from adventuring (and gaining a few pounds) Henk has been going by the nickname Porky, or that the tavern he bought was The Giggling Gargoyle.[/container]
He [b]might[/b] impart the following, if the players ask, or if it comes up naturally:
[ul]
[li]Henk was a h alf orc who fought with a giant battle axe. Marcus is no longer in touch with him, and the last he heard, Henk had bought a tavern in the city.[/li][br]
[li]Marcus and Lyza have recently been communicating using magic and a crystal ball.[/li][br]
[li]Marcus has also been using magic to find out what Jasper has been up to, and how he can be stopped.[/li][br]
[li]Jasper has not always been evil his experimentation with summoning and demonic forces has shifted his alignment.[/li][br]
[li]Jasper made a deal with a demonic entity to become more powerful. [/li][br]
[li]The Sceptre of Exilium is a powerful holy artifact that was crafted centuries ago by ancient priests to banish the demon that Jasper is allied with. It originally housed a huge emerald.[/li][br]
[li]Count Jasper acquired this artifact several years ago, and dismantled it, placing the sceptre in Mount Malice and the emerald in a dungeon on the far side of the kingdom. Lyza is currently on her way to retrieve the emerald. [/li][br]
[li]If it could be reunited with the emerald, the sceptre would be Jasper’s weakness.[/li][br]
[li]The sceptre is protected by magic and by a powerful monster that Jasper has summoned, but Marcus does not know what it is.[/li][/ul]

Finally, Marcus will gift the PCs with some magic items he thinks will be useful for them, then he insists they get some sleep they can talk more in the morning.
[ul]
[li]A [spoiler][block:1237335]|Gem of Seeing[/spoiler] (DMG p172)
[li]An [spoiler][block:1237337]|Immovable Rod[/spoiler] (DMG p175)
[li]A [spoiler][block:1237338]|Chime of Opening[/spoiler] (DMG p158) with two charges left
[li]A quiver of 10, +1 Arrows, and a bow if required
[li]A copper ring
[/ul]

However, when Marcus wakes the PCs at dawn, he seems agitated. He says events have moved on faster than he thought they would, and they must hurry up and leave at once. He urges them to find the Sceptre of Exilium, return to Port Perro, and hope that young Lyza is successful in obtaining the emerald. 

[h1]Act II[/h1]
[aloud]
Marcus’s map leads you to a small cave entrance on the side of the mountain. At the back of the cave is an opening to a narrow tunnel which leads deeper into the mountain. In single file, you follow the tunnel until it ends at a large oak door. On the door is carved the head of a white dragon, and below that is a riddle:
[center] [b][i]
I come before cube, age, and rink.[br]
Warm me up if you want a drink.
[/i][/b][/center]
[/aloud]

This is a trap. When someone says the word “ice”, anyone standing in front of the dragon’s head will be blasted with 20hp of cold damage (halved on a successful DC10 dexterity save). The door will still not open. It is magically locked and needs to be opened with a chime of opening.

[h1]Act III[/h1]
[aloud]Beyond the door is a cavern with a narrow exit tunnel at the far side. Two skeletons wearing armour and carrying weapons appear to stand guard on either side of the tunnel entrance. There is a dark redish-brown line drawn across the middle of the room[/aloud]

The line is drawn with blood, now dried, and must be crossed to get to the door. The skeletons are harmless and the armour/weapons are ragged and useless. 

As soon as a PC enters the tunnel, a magical trap is activated, and a huge scythe begins to swing in the tunnel, doing 1d10 damage to the character in the front (halved if they pass a DC10 dexterity save). It swings erratically and unpredictably, and to get past it each player must pass a DC15 dexterity check or take 1d10hp [roll:1d10] damage. Alternatively, the scythe can easily be disabled using an Immovable Rod.

[h1]Act IV[/h1]
[aloud]
The tunnel opens out into a huge cavern with stone walls and a high ceiling. In the middle of the cavern the Sceptre of Exilium is visible on a stone plinth. It is guarded by a creature that resembles a giant eyeball with dangling tentacles like a jellyfish.
[/aloud]

The [spoiler][block:1237331]|Floating Eye Guardian[/spoiler] levitates above the sceptre, always just out of reach of melee attacks, and it will fight to the death and never abandon the sceptre. Each turn it will attack with one of the following spells:
[ul]
[li][spoiler][block:557471]|Magic Missile[/spoiler] (PHB p257)[/li][br]
[li][spoiler][block:361124]|Fire Bolt[/spoiler] (PHB p242)[/li][br]
[li][spoiler][block:379166]|Poison Spray[/spoiler] (PHB p266)[/li][br]
[/ul]

It is immune to slashing, bludgeoning  and piercing from non-magical weapons, as well as conditions such as charmed, paralyzed, petrified.

If a PC misses their shot with a magical arrow, they can spend a turn recovering the arrow from where it went after it bounced off the stone wall.

[h1]Epilogue[/h1]
This session ends with the PCs back on the road again, approaching Port Perro, which they can see on the horizon. They are pleased with the success of their mission, but there is a tension in the air as the PCs know that the biggest test is yet to come.

articles.title: The Bartered Brides
articles.content:
[h2]Players Information[/h2]
The players (if there are no dwarves in the party) are approached by a group of three dwarves, dressed in rough homespun and stout boots. The spokesman, a well-muscled fellow with intelligent eyes and an easy manner, named @[Arkin arn Boltar](person:414cbdaf-4576-4324-b82e-2cd4a4b0c7fc), explains to the players that he and his brothers Balkin and Corvin have come to town to court certain young dwarvish ladies, and to take them back to their silver mine as wives. 

Unfortunately, while the ladies are willing, their fathers are not convinced of the brothers' claim to wealth in their mine. Now news has come that orcs have been sighted near the mine, and their presence is needed at home. But to leave off courting now would certainly lose them any chances they had of gaining their wives. Arkin has therefore decided that they must take their wives now, by force, if necessary, and return to the mine. But for this they will need help, and they will offer 1,500gp in raw silver ore if the players will aid them in their quest.

[h2]GM's Notes[/h2]
Arkin's story is basically true. He is too much a backwoodsman to be a liar. His ego also does not permit him to even consider that the ladies involved might not be entirely willing to leave the relative comforts of city life for a rough underground mine in orc-infested mountains. Another fact of which Arkin may not be totally aware is that while dwarven women are generally not common, in @[The Dwarrow](settlement:fa91fd8a-f74e-409d-a703-26a9b8e09954) they are even less so, and are even more highly prized than in the dwarven cities. For Arkin and his brothers to kidnap three of these rare treasures would be a capital crime.

The girls involved are all daughters of working-class dwarves, and are described below, including the places where they may be found, and their willingness to accept the "proposal" of the brother who has been courting them.

@[Asgird asha Nikkolai](person:8afd8026-854d-4bc9-90aa-13707299c09b), the chosen bride of Balkin, is the daughter of Nikkolai, who runs @[The Boar's Head](landmark:5311f2ab-f5fe-4f72-90fe-25bf6ef85417) Tavern. She can be found in the tavern during the afternoon and evening, serving ale to numerous young dwarves who are all hoping to meet her father's approval for her hand. She lives with her parents and other relations in a one-story stone house on an alley just off Foundry Street. There is a 45% chance that she will come willingly, otherwise, she will fight unless silenced or threatened with force. She will also try to escape, given the slightest opportunity, or to call for help.

@[Elorie asha Balin](person:5fb8a463-b1a7-433b-aa6c-f268b8b79eb0) is a member of the large Malbar family and feels very much like a small fish in a very crowded pond. She spends much of her daytime hours cooking and washing dishes in the dormitory kitchen just across the alley from the printing workshop. She is accompanied here by her married sister and a younger cousin and is escorted home late at night by a group of male relatives to the crowded Malbar home on Printer's Alley. If approached by Covin arn Boltar while at the dormitory kitchen, she has a 75% chance of being agreeable. She will respond favorably 55% of the time under any other circumstances.

@[Dallarie asha Orli](person:34d13930-2e07-4bcb-b7ef-cbc32d59a799), a very shy and retiring maiden, is devoted to her father, the grouchy innkeeper of @[The Axe and Anvil](landmark:35735272-bfaa-4d56-b7fb-e554036fe7e6), where the Boltar brothers have been staying, and is very much in awe of @[Arkin arn Boltar](person:414cbdaf-4576-4324-b82e-2cd4a4b0c7fc), whose eye she has unknowingly caught. She never leaves the inn and stays in the family quarters unless doing chores around the inn. There is a 50% chance that her mother is with her or within call whenever she is around the guest rooms. There is only a 15% chance that Dallarie will come willingly, but only a 10% chance that she will do more than weep or try to run if forced to accompany the players.

Needless to say, if the brothers and the players actually do manage to make off with one or all of the girls, the families will raise a great hue and cry that will gather a determined posse of male relatives and hopeful suitors. The Boltar brothers will head straight back to their mountain, unless persuaded to do otherwise by the players, and certainly that is the direction in which the posse of d20+20 [roll:1d20+20] dwarves will be looking for them, out for blood.

- @[The Dwarrow](settlement:fa91fd8a-f74e-409d-a703-26a9b8e09954)

- Axe and Anvil


[b]Key Participants[/b] [br]
- @[Arkin arn Boltar](person:414cbdaf-4576-4324-b82e-2cd4a4b0c7fc)
